subject: Ophthalmology
question: Which of the following is not true regarding eye donation?
options: Eyes can be removed from a living human being with consent.
Donated eyes can never be sold or bought.

explanation.short: Ans. A) Eyes can be removed from a living human being with consent.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Anyone of any age can pledge to donate eyes after death . Donated eyes can never be bought or sold. Eyes CANNOT be removed from a living human being inspite of his/her consent & wish. Contraindications for eye donation include transmissible diseases like – HIV, HBsAg, syphilis, TB etc.

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Which among the following is a method of short-term storage after eye ball retrieval?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . MK Medium (McCarey-Kaufman Medium) : This is a medium used for the storage of corneal tissues . However, MK medium is typically used for short-term preservation of up to 4 days . It's often used in scenarios where corneal transplantation is planned shortly after retrieval .
• Option C . Cryopreservation : This method involves freezing the tissue for long-term storage . Cryopreservation is not commonly used for corneal storage due to the risk of damaging the corneal endothelial cells during the freeze-thaw process.
• Option D . Organ Culture Method : This is a method used for longer-term storage of corneal tissues , allowing storage for up to 4 weeks . It involves keeping the cornea in a special nutrient medium at a controlled temperature, which is higher than that used in MK medium or moist chamber storage.
• Educational Objective:
• The Moist chamber storage is a method used for the short-term storage of corneal tissue after eyeball retrieval , suitable for maintaining the viability of the cornea for up to 24-48 hours .
explanation.ref: pg 137/ systemic & community ophthal/ AK KHURANA comprehensive ophthalmology / 6 th edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: When was NPCB established in India?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• The National Programme for Control of Blindness (NPCB) was launched in India in 1976 with the goal of reducing the prevalence of blindness through comprehensive eye care services and improving accessibility to eye care facilities across the country. This program has been pivotal in addressing various eye-related issues and enhancing the quality of life for many individuals.
• Key aspects of the National Programme for Control of Blindness (NPCB) include:
• Focus on Preventable Blindness : A significant emphasis is placed on preventing blindness caused by conditions that are easily treatable, such as cataract, which is the leading cause of blindness in India. Community Participation: The program encourages community involvement and utilizes a decentralized approach, engaging primary health centers and local healthcare workers to maximize its outreach and impact. Capacity Building: It aims to strengthen the infrastructure for eye care services by upgrading existing facilities, providing training to eye care professionals, and increasing the availability of specialized equipment and materials. Partnerships : The NPCB collaborates with various governmental and non-governmental organizations, including international agencies, to pool resources, share expertise, and expand its reach and effectiveness. Monitoring and Evaluation : Regular monitoring and evaluation mechanisms are in place to assess the program's performance, identify areas for improvement, and ensure the achievement of its targets.
explanation.ref: Pg 480/ systemic & community ophthal/ AK KHURANA comprehensive ophthalmology / 6 th edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: According to NPCB,a visual acuity below which of the following levels is classified as blindness?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• NPCB definition of blindness:
• Presenting visual acuity < 3/60 or counting fingers at 3 metres or central visual field less than 10 degree in better eye .
• Old definition: Visual acuity less than 6/60 or central field less than 20 degree in better eye.
explanation.ref: pg 476/ systemic & community ophthal/ AK KHURANA comprehensive ophthalmology / 6 th edition

question: What is the visual acuity below which it is termed as low vision according to WHO?

question: According to WHO, a visual acuity of less than what value is defined as a criterion for blindness?

question: The following are the major blinding eye conditions for immediate attention to achieve the goals of VISION 2020, by WHO, except?

subject: Ophthalmology
question: What is the commonest cause of blindness in children?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Vitamin A Deficiency : This used to be one of the leading causes of preventable blindness in children , especially in developing countries . Vitamin A deficiency can lead to xerophthalmia and keratomalacia , resulting in severe visual impairment or blindness. However, with improved nutrition and supplementation programs, its prevalence has significantly decreased in many parts of the world.
• Option B . Congenital Cataract : This is a significant cause of visual impairment and treatable blindness in children . Congenital cataracts can be isolated or part of systemic syndromes , and early detection and surgical intervention are crucial for visual development.
• Option C . Retinopathy of Prematurity (ROP) : ROP is a disease of the eye affecting prematurely born babies . It's related to the use of supplemental oxygen in the neonatal period . ROP can lead to blindness but is preventable with proper screening and timely treatment.
• Educational Objective:
• While vitamin A deficiency, congenital cataract, and retinopathy of prematurity are significant causes of childhood blindness , refractive errors are currently recognized as the most common cause of visual impairment in children due to their high prevalence and frequent lack of correction.
explanation.ref: pg 483/ systemic & community ophthal/ AK KHURANA comprehensive ophthalmology / 6 th edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Which of the following is false regarding the guidelines laid down by the government of India for organising eye camps?
options: Presently SICS & phacoemulsification techniques are recommended
At least 5 operating surgeons should be present
Both the eyes should never be operated at one go
Cases with surgical risks such as diabetes, hypertensives & cardiac problems should not be operated in camps

explanation.short: Ans. B) At least 5 operating surgeons should be present
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Presently SICS & Phacoemulsification Techniques are Recommended : Small Incision Cataract Surgery (SICS) and Phacoemulsification are modern and efficient techniques for cataract surgery . These methods are indeed recommended for their safety, effectiveness, and relatively quick recovery times.
• Option C . Both the Eyes Should Never be Operated at One Go : This is a standard guideline in eye care , especially in camp settings . Operating on both eyes simultaneously increases the risk of complications, including infection. Therefore, it is recommended to operate on one eye at a time.
• Option D . Cases with Surgical Risks Such as Diabetes, Hypertensives & Cardiac Problems Should Not be Operated in Camps : This statement is generally true. Patients with systemic conditions like diabetes , hypertension , or cardiac problems are at a higher risk of complications during and after surgery . Such cases typically require more comprehensive pre-operative and post-operative care than what might be available at an eye camp.
• Educational Objective:
• Recognize that the requirement of having at least 5 operating surgeons at eye camps is not a standardized guideline by the Government of India , unlike the recommendations for SICS & Phacoemulsification techniques, the practice of not operating on both eyes simultaneously, and the avoidance of operating on high-risk patients in camp settings.
explanation.ref: pg 487/ systemic & community ophthal/ AK KHURANA comprehensive ophthalmology / 6 th edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Which of the following is not a low vision aid (LVA)?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Keplerian Telescope : This is a type of telescopic device used as a low vision aid . It's designed to help people with low vision see objects at a distance , like street signs or bus numbers.
• Option B . High Plus Spectacles : These are spectacles with high magnification lenses . They are used as low vision aids to help people with poor vision to read or perform other close-up tasks .
• Option D . Video Magnifier System : This is a device that uses a camera to display enlarged images on a screen . It's a common low vision aid used for reading and viewing detailed objects.
• Educational Objective:
• Recognize that TORIC IOLs , while used in eye surgery to correct astigmatism , are not classified as low vision aids (LVAs), unlike devices like Keplerian telescopes , high plus spectacles , and video magnifier systems that are specifically designed to assist those with low vision.
explanation.ref: pg 354/ theory and practise of optics and refraction/4 th edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Use of all the following agents enhances the bioavailabiltity of drugs in the eye, except?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Hydroxypropyl Methylcellulose (HPMC) : This is a viscoelastic polymer used as a viscosity-enhancing agent in eye drops . It increases the retention time of the drug on the eye's surface, thus enhancing bioavailability.
• Option B . Hyaluronic Acid : Often used in ophthalmic formulations, hyaluronic acid has viscoelastic properties that can increase the contact time of the drug with the ocular surface , thereby enhancing drug absorption and bioavailability.
• Option D . Polyvinyl Alcohol : This is another viscosity-enhancing agent used in ophthalmic preparations . It helps in prolonging the contact time of the drug on the ocular surface, thereby improving drug bioavailability.
• Educational Objective:
• Sodium Chloride is used for tonicity adjustment in ophthalmic solutions. It does not enhance the bioavailability of drugs in the eye , unlike viscosity-enhancing agents like Hydroxypropyl Methylcellulose , Hyaluronic Acid, and Polyvinyl Alcohol.
explanation.ref: PARSONS DISEASES OF EYE 22 ND EDITION PAGE 146

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Which of the following solutions has the greatest anti-inflammatory action?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . 0.1% Dexamethasone Acetate : Dexamethasone is a potent corticosteroid . It is highly effective in reducing inflammation and is commonly used in ophthalmology for its strong anti-inflammatory properties.
• Option B . 0.1% Fluorometholone : Fluorometholone is less potent compared to dexamethasone and prednisolone . It is often used for inflammatory conditions of the eye where the risk of steroid-induced intraocular pressure (IOP) increase is a concern.
• Option C . 1% Medroxyprogesterone : This is a synthetic progestin and is not typically known for strong anti-inflammatory effects in ophthalmic use.
• Educational Objective:
• Understand that 1% Prednisolone Acetate , due to its high potency and concentration , is likely the most effective among the listed options for providing strong anti-inflammatory action in ophthalmic use .
explanation.ref: PARSONS DISEASES OF EYE 22 ND EDITION page 154

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Which of the following beta blockers is known to increase the perfusion of optic nerve head?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Timolol : A non-selective beta blocker widely used in the treatment of glaucoma . While effective in lowering intraocular pressure , it does not have a significant effect on increasing optic nerve head perfusion.
• Option B . Carteolol : Another non-selective beta blocker with intrinsic sympathomimetic activity . It lowers intraocular pressure but does not specifically increase optic nerve head perfusion.
• Option C . Levobunolol : Similar to Timolol , Levobunolol is a non-selective beta blocker used for lowering intraocular pressure . It is not known to significantly affect optic nerve head perfusion.
• Educational Objective:
• Due to its beta – 1 blocker selectivity , Betaxolol has a lesser effect on pulmonary and vascular beta-2 receptors. It is known to potentially increase blood flow to the optic nerve head, which could be beneficial in glaucoma patients where optic nerve head perfusion is a concern.
explanation.ref: PARSONS DISEASES OF EYE 22 ND EDITION PAGE 157

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Which of the following preservatives is the most epitheliotoxic?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Povidone : Often used as a lubricant in artificial tears , povidone is generally gentle on the epithelial cells of the eye . It's less likely to cause irritation or damage to the surface of the eye compared to some other preservatives.
• Option B . Chlorobutanol : This preservative is known for its mildness and is less irritating to the ocular surface. However, it is less commonly used due to its limited antimicrobial efficacy and stability issues in certain formulations.
• Option D . Sodium Perborate : This is a preservative that decomposes into water and oxygen upon contact with the eye , making it much gentler on the epithelial cells.
• Educational Objective:
• Benalkonium Chloride is the more epitheliotoxic preservative agent . This is a common preservative in ophthalmic solutions but is known for its cytotoxic effects on the corneal and conjunctival epithelial cells . It can disrupt the tear film and cause ocular surface disease with long-term use.
explanation.ref: PARSONS DISEASES OF THE EYE PAGE 158

subject: Ophthalmology
question: In SAFE strategy for Trachoma, ‘surgery’ stands for correction of?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Ptosis, or drooping of the eyelid , is not the target of surgery in the SAFE strategy for Trachoma. Ptosis may be caused by a variety of conditions , but it is not a direct consequence of Trachoma.
• Option C. Squint, or strabismus , is a condition where the eyes do not properly align with each other when looking at an object. It is not specifically addressed in the SAFE strategy for Trachoma.
• Option D. A Hordeolum, commonly known as a stye , is an acute infection of the oil glands of the eyelid . This is not a condition treated by the surgery component of the SAFE strategy for Trachoma.
• Educational Objective
• In the SAFE strategy for Trachoma, "Surgery" refers to the surgical correction of Trichiasis , a condition where eyelashes turn inward and rub against the cornea , potentially leading to corneal scarring and blindness . This intervention is crucial to prevent the progression of visual impairment in Trachoma patients .
• Pg 72/ CONJUNCTIVA/ Comprehensive Ophthalmology AK KHURANA/ 6th Edition
explanation.ref: Pg 72/ CONJUNCTIVA/ Comprehensive Ophthalmology AK KHURANA/ 6th Edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
A 25-year-old individual presents to the clinic with sudden-onset severe eye redness, copious purulent discharge, and marked eyelid swelling. The patient reports recent unprotected high risk sexual contact. What is the most likely causative organism of this hyperacute conjunctivitis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/09/picture6_xEOz45s.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Pseudomonas Aeruginosa, a less common cause of conjunctivitis , is typically associated with contact lens wearers . While it can cause a severe and sight-threatening infection , it is not typically associated with the sexual transmission or the severe purulent discharge seen in gonococcal conjunctivitis.
• Option C. Chlamydia Trachomatis can cause inclusion conjunctivitis , particularly in sexually active young adults . However, its presentation is usually more subacute or chronic , and not as hyperacute or severely purulent as gonococcal conjunctivitis.
• Option D. Adenovirus is a common cause of viral conjunctivitis , often leading to watery discharge , irritation , and conjunctival redness . However, adenoviral infections do not typically cause the severe purulent discharge or the marked eyelid swelling seen in gonococcal conjunctivitis.
• Educational Objective
• In a young adult presenting with sudden-onset severe eye redness , copious purulent discharge , and marked eyelid swelling , especially with a history of high-risk sexual behavior , gonococcal conjunctivitis caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae is the most likely diagnosis . Prompt identification and treatment are crucial to prevent complications .
explanation.ref: Pg 64/CONJUNCTIVA/Comprehensive Ophthalmology AK KHURANA/6 th Edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: In which type of keratoconjunctivitis are Horner-Tranta-spots observed?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Phlyctenular Keratoconjunctivitis is an allergic , non-infectious type of conjunctivitis , often associated with tuberculosis or staphylococcal infections . It is characterized by the presence of small , whitish-yellow nodules ( phlyctenules ) on the conjunctiva or cornea , but not Horner-Tranta spots.
• Option C. Epidemic Keratoconjunctivitis is a highly contagious viral conjunctivitis , caused by adenovirus , particularly serotypes 8 , 19 , and 37 . Symptoms include watery discharge , red eye , and photophobia . Follicles on the conjunctiva , not Horner-Tranta spots, are a characteristic finding .
• Common clinical manifestations of Epidemic Kerato-Conjunctivitis (EKC).
• Photomicrograph of the inferior conjunctival fornix of a patient with acute EKC , showing lymphoid follicles and subconjunctival hemorrhage . Photomicrograph of an eye with corneal subepithelial infiltrates ( white arrows ).
• Option D. Trachoma, Caused by the bacterium Chlamydia trachomatis , this chronic infectious disease leads to conjunctival scarring , entropion , trichiasis , and potentially blindness . It does not feature Horner-Tranta spots but rather follicles and scarring on the conjunctiva.
• Educational Objective
• Horner-Tranta spots , which are characteristic of Vernal Keratoconjunctivitis , are small , white dots at the limbus caused by aggregates of eosinophils and epithelial cells , differentiating it from other forms of keratoconjunctivitis .
• Pg 80/ CONJUNCTIVA/ Comprehensive Ophthalmology AK KHURANA/ 6 th Edition. Pennington, Matthew & Saha, Amrita & Painter, David & Gavazzi, Christina & Ashrafali, Mohamed & Zhou, Xiaohong & Chodosh, James & Rajaiya, Jaya. (2019). Disparate Entry of Adenoviruses Dictates Differential Innate Immune Responses on the Ocular Surface. 7. 10.3390/microorganisms7090351.

subject: Ophthalmology
question: FISTO classification is used for:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Alkali Injury : The classification for ocular chemical burns, like alkali injuries, is typically the Roper-Hall or Dua classification. These systems assess the extent of limbal ischemia and corneal damage to predict prognosis.
• Roper Hall classification of alkali injuries.
• Option B. Vernal Keratoconjunctivitis ( VKC ) is often graded based on the severity and type of conjunctival changes , such as papillary hypertrophy and corneal involvement . However, the FISTO classification is not used for VKC.
• Option D. Ocular herpes simplex is typically diagnosed based on clinical features and confirmed by laboratory tests . Classification systems for herpes simplex keratitis focus on the location and type of the lesions (e.g., dendritic ulcers , stromal keratitis ), rather than using the FISTO classification.
• Educational Objective
• The FISTO classification is specifically used for grading Trachoma , assessing the presence and severity of Follicles , Inflammation , Scarring , Trichiasis , and Opacity to guide treatment and prognostic evaluation .
• Pg 70/ CONJUNCTIVA/ Comprehensive Ophthalmology AK KHURANA/ 6 th Edition Soleimani, Mohammad & Naderan, Morteza. (2020). Management Strategies of Ocular Chemical Burns: Current Perspectives. Clinical Ophthalmology (Auckland, N.Z.). 14. 2687-2699. 10.2147/OPTH.S235873.

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
A 28-year-old sexually active female presents to the emergency department with redness, purulent discharge, and marked eyelid swelling in the left eye. On further evaluation, the diagnosis of gonococcal conjunctivitis is confirmed. Which of the following complications is the patient at the highest risk for if left untreated?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/09/picture8_fg79zm9.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Cataract Formation: While cataract formation is a potential complication of ocular infections , it is not the primary concern in gonococcal conjunctivitis. Corneal involvement and subsequent complications are more common in this condition.
• Option C. Retinal Detachment: Retinal detachment is not typically associated with gonococcal conjunctivitis. It occurs due to the separation of the retina from the underlying tissue and is not a common sequela of this type of conjunctivitis.
• Option D. Optic Neuritis: Optic neuritis refers to inflammation of the optic nerve and can lead to vision impairment . It is not a direct complication of gonococcal conjunctivitis. Complications in gonococcal conjunctivitis usually involve the anterior segment of the eye , such as the cornea .
• Educational Objective
• The patient with gonococcal conjunctivitis is at the highest risk for the complication of Corneal ulceration . This complication can result in significant vision loss and highlights the importance of early diagnosis and treatment of gonococcal conjunctivitis to prevent severe ocular damage .
explanation.ref: Pg 64/ CONJUNCTIVA/ Comprehensive Ophthalmology AK KHURANA/ 6 th Edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 7-day-old neonate develops conjunctivitis, diagnosed as neonatal inclusion conjunctivitis caused by Chlamydia. Which of the following serotype is responsible?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Serotypes A, B, Ba, C: These serotypes of Chlamydia trachomatis are primarily associated with trachoma , a chronic keratoconjunctivitis that is a major cause of blindness in certain parts of the world. Trachoma is not typically seen in neonates.
• Option C. Serotypes L1, L2, L3: These serotypes are responsible for lymphogranuloma venereum (LGV) , a sexually transmitted infection that affects the lymphatic system and is not typically associated with neonatal conjunctivitis.
• Option D. Serotypes 8 & 19: These serotypes are not standard classifications for Chlamydia trachomatis. They might be a mix-up with another classification system or an error.
• Educational Objective:
• In the case of neonatal inclusion conjunctivitis caused by Chlamydia trachomatis , the responsible serotypes are D to K (option B). These serotypes are known for causing urogenital infections in adults and can lead to inclusion conjunctivitis in neonates when transmitted during childbirth .
• Pg 67/ CONJUNCTIVA/ Comprehensive Ophthalmology AK KHURANA/ 6 th Edition; https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK441840/

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 28-year-old male patient visited the ophthalmologist with complaints of Red, Watery Eyes with a slight gritty sensation since the past 10 days. After examination, he is diagnosed with Epidemic Keratoconjunctivitis. Which Adenovirus Serotype is responsible for this condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Serotypes 10 & 12: These serotypes are not typically associated with epidemic keratoconjunctivitis. Adenovirus serotypes 10 and 12 are more commonly associated with respiratory illnesses .
• Option B. Serotypes 3 & 7: These serotypes can cause various adenoviral infections , including some forms of conjunctivitis, but they are not the most common causes of epidemic keratoconjunctivitis.
• Option C. Serotypes 3 & 8: Serotype 3 is associated with a range of adenoviral infections , including conjunctivitis , but serotype 8 is one of the primary serotypes known to cause epidemic keratoconjunctivitis .
• Educational Objective:
• Serotypes 8 & 19 of adenovirus are most frequently responsible for cases of epidemic keratoconjunctivitis , which is characterized by symptoms like red, watery eyes and a gritty sensation .
explanation.ref: Pg 74/ CONJUNCTIVA/ Comprehensive Ophthalmology AK KHURANA/ 6 th Edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 2-day-old neonate was brought to the ophthalmologist with complaints of red eyes with watery discharge. Mother also gave history of usage of an ophthalmic medication to prevent infection. After examination, a diagnosis of ophthalmia neonatorum is made. Which drug used must have caused this?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Azithromycin is an antibiotic and is not known to cause chemical conjunctivitis. Azithromycin eye drops or ointment may be used to treat bacterial causes of ophthalmia neonatorum , like Chlamydia trachomatis .
• Option C. Fluoroquinolones are broad-spectrum antibiotics and are generally not used as prophylaxis in newborns. They are not typically associated with causing chemical conjunctivitis.
• Option D. Tetracycline is another antibiotic option for the prevention of bacterial ophthalmia neonatorum , especially chlamydial infections . Like azithromycin, tetracycline is not known to cause chemical conjunctivitis.
• Educational Objective:
• Given the history and clinical presentation, the most likely cause of the red eyes with watery discharge in this neonate is chemical conjunctivitis due to silver nitrate prophylaxis . This reaction is known as " silver nitrate conjunctivitis " and is characterized by a mild , self-limiting conjunctivitis with watery discharge , typically appearing within the first few days after administration .
• Pg 77/ CONJUNCTIVA/ Comprehensive Ophthalmology AK KHURANA/ 6th Edition
explanation.ref: Pg 77/ CONJUNCTIVA/ Comprehensive Ophthalmology AK KHURANA/ 6th Edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
A 15-year-old male presented with complaints of decrease in vision in both eyes. He also give history of itching and irritation in both eyes, on and off. On examination, the conjunctiva shows cobblestone papillae and discrete white dots along the limbus. The diagnosis is?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/08/02/whatsapp-image-2023-06-12-at-19012101101025-1.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Phlyctenular conjunctivitis:
• Occurs as an allergic response to endogenous allergens ( Tuberculous proteins , Staphylococcal proteins ). Most of the times there is solitary conjunctival nodule but at times there may be two nodules . In a few days the nodule ulcerates at apex which later on gets epithelised.
• Option B. Epidemic Keratoconjunctivitis:
• Follicles : more marked in lower lid Pseudomembrane on the conjunctival surface Superficial punctuate keratitis: distinctive feature of EKC , becomes apparent after 1 week of the onset of disease Preauricular lymphadenopathy
• Option C. Cicatricial Conjunctivitis:
• Chronic conjunctivitis with conjunctival fibrosis.
• Educational Objective:
• Presence of Cobblestone Papillae and Horner Trantas Spots are suggestive of a diagnosis of Vernal Keratoconjunctivitis .

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
A 20-year-old female of thin stature, presented to the OPD with redness, irritation and watering in both eyes for the past 1 month. She had a history of loss of appetite & weight. Examination showed a pinkish white nodule with ulcerated apex at the limbus. What would be the investigation of choice?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/09/picture16_iHMqqnR.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. ELISA: is used for diagnosing viral components .
• Option B. KOH MOUNT: Is used for diagnosing fungal elements .
• Option D. Tonometry: Is the measurement of intraocular pressure .
• Educational Objective:
• Phylectenularconjunctivitis warrants further investigation of TB . Hence, Mantoux test must be performed in this patient.
explanation.ref: Pg 84/ CONJUNCTIVA/ Comprehensive Ophthalmology AK KHURANA/ 6 th Edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Identify the condition shown in the clinical photograph below.

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/09/picture22_JFHNW5m.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Ankyloblepharon: Adhesion between eyelids .
• Option B. Trichiasis: Inward curling of eyelash .
• Lower lid shows inward turning of eyelashes called trichiasis .
• Option C. Chalazion: Meibomion cyst . Sterile chronic granulomatous inflammatory lesion (lipogranuloma) of the meibomian, or sometimes Zeis , glands caused by retained sebaceous secretions .
• Educational Objective:
• Symblepharon is an adhesion between the bulbar and the palpebral conjunctiva
explanation.ref: Pg 376/ Eyelids/ C omprehensive Ophthalmology AK KHURANA/ 6 th Edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A child weighing 6kgs is brought to a healthcare facility with complaints of vision problems. Upon examination, the healthcare provider observes dry, grayish- white triangular, foamy patches on the bulbar conjunctiva, known as Bitot spots. These spots appear due to Vitamin A deficiency, so what is the dosage of Vitamin A to be given to this child?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. 50,000 IU i.m. on day 0, 14 : This misses the dose on day 1 , which is recommended for immediate follow-up .
• Option C. 1 lakh (100,000) IU orally on day 0, 1, 14 : This dosage is too high for a child under 12 months of age.
• Option D. 1 lakh (100,000) IU orally on day 0, 14 : Again, this dosage is too high and also misses the critical dose on day 1 .
• Educational Objective:
• The dosage of Vit A supplementation for a 6 kg child is " 50,000 IU IM on day 0, 1 , and 14 ," adhering to WHO guidelines for vitamin A supplementation in young children with signs of vitamin A deficiency .
explanation.ref: KHURANA COMPREHENSIVE OPHTHALMOLOGY 5 TH ED. PG 465

subject: Ophthalmology
question: SAFE strategy comprises of:

explanation.short: Ans. B) Surgery to Correct Lid Deformity
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Surgery to correct cataract is not part of the SAFE strategy. Cataract surgery is the treatment for cataracts , which is a different eye condition .
• Option C. Surgery to correct glaucoma is not part of the SAFE strategy. Glaucoma surgeries are aimed at reducing intraocular pressure and are unrelated to trachoma.
• Option D. While corneal opacity can be a consequence of advanced trachoma , the surgery in the SAFE strategy specifically refers to the surgical correction of trichiasis to prevent corneal damage , not to surgeries that address corneal opacity itself.
• Educational Objective:
• Surgery to correct lid deformity , is part of the SAFE strategy for trachoma control .
• Pg 479/ Systemic & Community Ophthal/ AK KHURANA Comprehensive Ophthalmology / 6 th Edition
explanation.ref: Pg 479/ Systemic & Community Ophthal/ AK KHURANA Comprehensive Ophthalmology / 6 th Edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: SAFE strategy is an effective intervention demonstrated in developing nations for the control of blindness caused by which of the following?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Cataracts are a leading cause of blindness globally and are characterized by the clouding of the lens in the eye. The primary intervention for cataracts is surgical removal of the cloudy lens and, if necessary, its replacement with an artificial lens .
• Option B. Glaucoma is a group of eye conditions that damage the optic nerve , often due to abnormally high intraocular pressure . The treatment mainly involves eye drops , laser treatment , or surgery to lower intraocular pressure and prevent further damage to the optic nerve .
• Option D. Onchocerciasis, a lso known as river blindness , onchocerciasis is caused by the parasite Onchocerca volvulus . It's transmitted to humans through the bites of infected blackflies . The strategy for controlling onchocerciasis primarily involves mass drug administration , particularly with the medication ivermectin .
• Educational Objective:
• In the context of global public health interventions , the SAFE strategy (Surgery, Antibiotics, Facial cleanliness, Environmental improvement) is an effective approach specifically designed for the control and elimination of trachoma , a major cause of preventable blindness in developing nations.

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 14-month-old child was diagnosed with xerophthalmia. What is the dosage of vitamin A to be given?
options: 200,000 IU orally or 100,000 IU by IM injections
100,000 IU orally or 50,000 IU by IM injections
10,000 IU orally or 5000 IU by IM injections
20,000 IU orally or 10,000 IU by IM inections

explanation.short: Ans.A)200,000 IU orally or 100,000 IU by IM injections
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. 100,000 IU orally or 50,000 IU by IM injections : This is the correct dosage for children aged 6-11 months . For a 14-month-old , this dosage is too low .
• Option C. 10,000 IU orally or 5000 IU by IM injections : This dosage is significantly lower than the recommended amount for treating xerophthalmia .
• Option D. 20,000 IU orally or 10,000 IU by IM injections : This dosage is also much lower than what is recommended for a child with xerophthalmia .
• Educational Objective:
• For a 14-month-old child , the appropriate dosage would be 200,000 IU orally or 100,000 IU IM , following the schedule mentioned above.
explanation.ref: Pg 469/S ystemic & Community Ophthal/ AK KHURANA Comprehensive Ophthalmology / 6 th Edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Identify the False Statement:
options: Pseudopterygium is due to an Inflammatory Process
A probe can be passed underneath a Pterygium
Pterygium occurs in the Palpebral Aperture
Neoplasm can occur in a Long-Standing Pterygium

explanation.short: Ans. B) A Probe can be passed underneath a Pterygium
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Pseudopterygium is due to an inflammatory process: This is true . Pseudopterygium occurs as a result of an inflammatory process , often secondary to trauma or surgery , leading to the adhesion of the conjunctiva to the underlying cornea .
• Option B. A probe can be passed underneath a pterygium : This is true . Pterygium is a fibrovascular growth of conjunctiva over the cornea , and it is typically possible to pass a probe underneath it. This differentiates it from pseudopterygium , where adhesions prevent passing a probe underneath.
• Option C. Pterygium occurs in the palpebral aperture : This statement is true . Pterygium commonly occurs in the palpebral aperture , particularly in the area exposed to sunlight and wind , often on the nasal side .
• Educational Objective:
• Pterygiia are not preneoplastic lesions.
• Pg 88/CONJUNCTIVA/Comprehensive Ophthalmology AK KHURANA/6th Edition. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK558907/#:~:text=A%20'probe'%20test%20can%20be,is%20non%2Dprogressive%20in%20nature.&text=It%20is%20a%20yellowish%20colored,conjunctiva%20close%20to%20the%20limbus.

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The lymphatics from the medial part of the conjunctiva drain into which group of lymph nodes?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Pre-auricular Lymph Nodes are located just in front of the ears . They are responsible for draining lymph from the lateral part of the eyelids and conjunctiva , the frontal and temporal regions of the scalp , and the external auditory meatus.
• Option B. Parotid Lymph Nodes, s ituated around the parotid gland , these nodes primarily drain the parotid gland itself , as well as parts of the face and scalp . They are not typically involved in the direct lymphatic drainage of the conjunctiva.
• Option D. Submental Lymph Nodes, l ocated under the chin , these nodes drain the central lower lip , floor of the mouth, tip of the tongue, and lower incisors . They are not directly involved in the drainage of the conjunctiva.
• Educational Objective
• The lymphatics from the medial part of the conjunctiva primarily drain into the submandibular lymph nodes . This is crucial to know as it can help in diagnosing and understanding the spread of infections or other pathologies related to the eye and its adjacent structures .
explanation.ref: Pg 61/ Conjunctiva/ Comprehensive Ophthalmology/ AK KHURANA/ 6 th Edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: All of the following are features of trachoma except:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Herbert Pits are small , shallow depressions found on the limbus (the border between the cornea and the sclera ) in the advanced stages of trachoma . They are the result of resolved limbal follicles and are a characteristic sign of chronic or healed trachoma . This feature is indeed associated with trachoma.
• Option C. Artl’s Line is a scar line seen in the sulcus subtarsalis in the upper eyelid in chronic trachoma . It is formed due to the healing of conjunctival scarring and is a sign of advanced disease . This feature is a part of trachoma's presentation .
• Option D. Trichiasis refers to the inward turning of the eyelashes , which rub against the cornea and conjunctiva , causing irritation and potential corneal damage . Trichiasis is a common complication of chronic trachoma due to scarring of the eyelid .
• Educational Objective:
• Cobblestone papillae are not a feature of Trachoma.
explanation.ref: Pg 68/ CONJUNCTIVA/ Comprehensive Ophthalmology AK KHURANA/ 6 th Edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Identify the condition.

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/09/picture9_G1sr7MU.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Phlyctenular Conjunctivitis : This type of conjunctivitis is an immune response often associated with tuberculosis or staphylococcal infection . It is characterized by small, white , nodular lesions ( phlyctenules ) on the conjunctiva . While it causes discomfort and redness , it does not specifically manifest as angular conjunctivitis.
• Option B. Hyperacute Conjunctivitis is typically caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae or Neisseria meningitidis and is characterized by a very rapid onset and severe symptoms , including copious purulent discharge . It does not specifically present as angular conjunctivitis.
• Option D. Trachoma , chronic infection caused by Chlamydia trachomatis leads to follicular conjunctivitis initially and, over time, can cause scarring . Trachoma typically involves the entire conjunctiva and not just the angular areas.
• Educational Objective:
• Angular conjunctivitis caused by Moraxella Axenfeld is characterised by inflammation of conjunctiva and lid margins near the angle .
explanation.ref: Pg 66/ CONJUNCTIVA/ Comprehensive Ophthalmology AK KHURANA/ 6 th Edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 28-year-old female came to the ophthalmologist with complaints of irritation in the eyes, with whitish discharge collecting and redness at the angles of the eyes. She was diagnosed with Angular conjunctivitis. What is the most common causative organism of this infection?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Chlamydia Trachomatis is known to cause trachoma and inclusion conjunctivitis .
• Option C. Neisseria Gonorrhoeae causes hyperacute purulent conjunctivitis , which is characterized by a rapid onset and severe , copious purulent discharge . It is not typically associated with angular conjunctivitis.
• Option D. Staphylococcus Aureus can cause various types of eye infections , including styes and blepharitis , and is a possible cause of conjunctivitis .
• Educational Objective:
• Moraxella axenfeld Is the primary causative agent of angular conjunctivitis .
• Pg 66/ CONJUNCTIVA/ Comprehensive Ophthalmology AK KHURANA/ 6 th Edition
explanation.ref: Pg 66/ CONJUNCTIVA/ Comprehensive Ophthalmology AK KHURANA/ 6 th Edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: What is the incubation period for Gonococcal Ophthalmia Neonatorum?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. 6 hours is too short for the incubation period of gonococcal ophthalmia neonatorum . The infection usually takes longer to manifest symptoms .
• Option B. 20 days is too long for the incubation period of gonococcal ophthalmia neonatorum. The infection typically presents much sooner after birth .
• Option D. 10 days is longer than the usual incubation period for gonococcal ophthalmia neonatorum.
• Educational Objective:
• The symptoms of gonococcal ophthalmia neonatorum, such as redness, swelling , and purulent discharge from the eyes , generally appear within 2 to 5 days after birth in neonates exposed to Neisseria gonorrhoeae during delivery.
explanation.ref: Pg 77/ CONJUNCTIVA/Comprehensive Ophthalmology AK KHURANA/6 th Edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
A 6-day-old baby was brought to the OPD with complaints of Incessant Cry, Purulent Discharge from both eyes. The probable Diagnosis is?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/09/picture14_VjybWwf.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Epiphora refers to excessive tearing . It is common in infants due to congenital nasolacrimal duct obstruction . While it can cause tearing and some discomfort , epiphora typically does not lead to purulent discharge. It's more associated with watery discharge and might be accompanied by mucous but not the kind of purulent discharge described in the question. Congenital Nasolacrimal Duct Obstruction can be treated by Criggler’s massage , or may require Syringing and Probing and Dacryosurgery .
• Option C. Ptosis is a drooping of the upper eyelid . It can be congenital or acquired and varies in severity . While ptosis can be present in infants , it would not typically present with purulent discharge or incessant crying unless associated with other eye conditions.
• Option D. Congenital Cataract can lead to a decrease in vision but would not cause purulent discharge. The main signs would be a white reflex and possibly a lack of visual responsiveness or squint .
• Educational Objective:
• Based on the symptoms described – incessant crying and purulent discharge from both eyes in a 6-day-old baby – the most probable diagnosis is Ophthalmia Neonatorum . This condition necessitates prompt medical evaluation and treatment to prevent complications such as corneal damage and potential blindness .
explanation.ref: Pg 77/ CONJUNCTIVA/ Comprehensive Ophthalmology AK KHURANA/ 6 th Edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Treatment of Ophthalmia Neonatorum includes:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Povidone iodine 2.5% solution is an effective antiseptic and has been used in some settings for the prevention of ophthalmia neonatorum , particularly against bacterial and viral pathogens . However, it is not typically used as the primary treatment once an infection is established.
• Option B. 1% tetracycline eye ointment is a traditional treatment for ophthalmia neonatorum , especially for cases caused by Chlamydia trachomatis . Tetracycline eye ointment is effective against a broad range of pathogens .
• Option C. Ceftriaxone 75-100mg/kg/day IV 4 TID is an effective treatment for ophthalmia neonatorum caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae . Gonococcal conjunctivitis is a severe form of the disease and requires systemic antibiotic therapy , such as ceftriaxone .
• Educational Objective:
• Both tetracycline eye ointment and ceftriaxone are used in the treatment of ophthalmia neonatorum , the use of povidone iodine 2.5% solution is more preventive than curative in established cases.
explanation.ref: Pg77/ CONJUNCTIVA/ Comprehensive Ophthalmology AK KHURANA/ 6 th Edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 12-year-old girl is brought to the OPD with complaints of irritation and watering of eyes since few days. After examination, a diagnosis of Phlyctenular keratoconjunctivitis is made. What type of immunological response is it?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Type 1 Hypersensitivity is an immediate hypersensitivity reaction , commonly associated with allergies (e.g., pollen, pet dander). It involves IgE antibodies and the release of histamine and other mediators from mast cells.
• Option B. Type 2 Hypersensitivity involves antibody-mediated cytotoxic reactions , where antibodies (usually IgG or IgM ) bind to antigens on the surface of cells, leading to their destruction . It's seen in conditions like hemolytic anemia , rheumatic fever , and some drug reactions.
• Option C. Type 3 Hypersensitivity involves immune complex-mediated reactions , where antigen-antibody complexes are deposited in various tissues, leading to inflammation and tissue damage . This type of reaction is seen in diseases like systemic lupus erythematosus and post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis .
• Educational Objective:
• Phlyctenular conjunctivitis is considered a Type 4 (D) hypersensitivity reaction . It occurs as a delayed response to antigen exposure , where sensitized T cells release cytokines that cause inflammation and the characteristic lesions of the condition
explanation.ref: Pg 83/ CONJUNCTIVA/ Comprehensive Ophthalmology AK KHURANA/ 6 th Edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: What is Stage 3A of the New xerophthalmia classification given by WHO in 1982?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Night Blindness: This is the earliest clinical sign of vitamin A deficiency , classified as stage XN in the WHO classification . Night blindness means difficulty seeing in low light or darkness .
• Option B. Bitot’s Spots: These are foamy , triangular spots on the white part of the eye (conjunctiva) and are indicative of a more advanced vitamin A deficiency . They are classified as stage XB in the WHO classification.
• Option D. Xerophthalmic Fundus: This is a condition where there are changes in the retina due to severe vitamin A deficiency , classified as stage XF in the WHO classification.
• Educational Objective:
• Stage 3A of the New xerophthalmia classification given by WHO in 1982 , is Corneal Ulceration .

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
A 12-year-old boy presents to the ophthalmology clinic with complaints of itching, redness, and mucous discharge from both eyes, particularly during the spring and summer months. On examination, you observe the presence of giant papillae on the upper tarsal conjunctiva. What type of immunologic reaction is most likely involved in the pathogenesis of this condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/09/picture5_YiqSI5w.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Type 2 Hypersensitivity Reaction involves antibodies ( IgG or IgM ) directed against cell surface or tissue antigens , leading to cell damage . This mechanism is typically involved in diseases where there is a reaction against self-antigens or modified host cells , and it is not associated with VKC.
• Option C. Type 3 Hypersensitivity Reaction involves the formation of immune complexes ( antigen-antibody complexes ) that deposit in tissues and cause inflammation . Commonly seen in conditions like systemic lupus erythematosus and serum sickness , it is not the mechanism involved in VKC.
• Option D. Type 4 Hypersensitivity Reaction , also known as delayed-type hypersensitivity , this reaction is cell-mediated , primarily involving T cells . It is the mechanism behind conditions such as contact dermatitis and tuberculosis skin tests , but not VKC.
• Educational Objective
• The symptoms and signs of Vernal Keratoconjunctivitis, such as itching , redness , mucous discharge , and giant papillae on the upper tarsal conjunctiva , are indicative of a Type 1 hypersensitivity reaction , where IgE - mediated immune responses play a central role in the pathogenesis of the condition
• 80/CONJUNCTIVA/Comprehensive Ophthalmology AK KHURANA/6th Edition
explanation.ref: 80/CONJUNCTIVA/Comprehensive Ophthalmology AK KHURANA/6th Edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 22-year-old male, came to the ophthalmologist with complaints of pain, redness, watering in the eyes along with photophobia since 2 days. After examination, a diagnosis of haemorrhagic conjunctivitis is made. Which is the most common causative organism of this condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. While adenovirus is a common cause of viral conjunctivitis , it typically does not cause hemorrhagic conjunctivitis. The conjunctivitis caused by adenovirus is usually characterized by watery discharge , redness , and follicular conjunctival reaction .
• Option B. Cytomegalovirus (CMV) can cause such as retinitis in immunocompromised individuals , but it is not a typical cause of hemorrhagic conjunctivitis.
• Option C. Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) can lead to various ocular complications due to immune suppression . However, it is not directly associated with hemorrhagic conjunctivitis.
• Educational Objective
• The most common causative agent of hemorrhagic conjunctivitis , which presents with symptoms like pain, redness, watering , and photophobia , is Enterovirus , particularly Enterovirus 70 and the Coxsackie A24 variant.
• pg 77/ CONJUNCTIVA/ comprehensive ophthalmology AK KHURANA/ 6 th edition Wright PW, Strauss GH, Langford MP. Acute hemorrhagic conjunctivitis. Am Fam Physician. 1992 Jan;45(1):173-8. PMID: 1309404.

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Which of the following statements is not true about EKC?
options: Pseudomembrane Lining the Lower Fornix and Palpebral Conjunctiva
Topical Steroids can be used during Active Inflammation
Superficial Punctate Keratitis is a typical feature of EKC.

explanation.short: Ans. C) Topical Steroids can be used during Active Inflammation
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Pseudomembrane lining the lower fornix and palpebral conjunctiva : In EKC, the formation of pseudomembranes is a common feature. These are fibrinous exudates that can be seen on the conjunctiva , particularly in severe cases. This statement is true.
• Option B. Preauricular lymphadenopathy is present : EKC often leads to preauricular lymphadenopathy , which is swelling of the lymph nodes near the ear . This is a response to the viral infection and is a common clinical finding in EKC . This statement is true.
• Option D. Superficial punctate keratitis is a typical feature of EKC : This is true. Superficial punctate keratitis , which is the presence of small , scattered lesions on the cornea , is a hallmark of EKC . It's a result of the inflammatory response to the viral infection .
• Educational Objective:
• In the context of Epidemic Keratoconjunctivitis (EKC) , the use of topical steroids during active inflammation is not universally recommended due to potential adverse effects and should be approached with caution .
• Pg 74/ CONJUNCTIVA/ Comprehensive Ophthalmology AK KHURANA/ 6 th Edition
explanation.ref: Pg 74/ CONJUNCTIVA/ Comprehensive Ophthalmology AK KHURANA/ 6 th Edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Identify the probable diagnosis:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/09/picture13_VxHCFuM.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Bacterial Conjunctivitis typically presents with redness of the eye, tearing , and a purulent or mucopurulent discharge . While the conjunctiva can be inflamed, bacterial conjunctivitis does not usually cause a subconjunctival hemorrhage.
• Option B. Epidemic Keratoconjunctivitis c aused by adenovirus , this condition presents with symptoms such as watery discharge, redness, swelling of the eyelids , and a feeling of irritation or foreign body sensation in the eye . It can be associated with subconjunctival hemorrhage , but other symptoms typically dominate the clinical picture , like corneal involvement (keratitis).
• Option C. Herpetic Conjunctivitis is caused by herpes simplex virus and presents with eye redness, tearing, and may have characteristic dendritic ulcers on the cornea when examined under a slit lamp . Subconjunctival hemorrhage is not a typical feature of herpetic conjunctivitis.
• Educational Objective:
• Given that the primary presentation in the question is subconjunctival hemorrhage , the most likely diagnosis is D. Haemorrhagic Conjunctivitis .
• Pg 77/ CONJUNCTIVA/ Comprehensive Ophthalmology AK KHURANA/ 6 th Edition
explanation.ref: Pg 77/ CONJUNCTIVA/ Comprehensive Ophthalmology AK KHURANA/ 6 th Edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
A 5-year-old boy from a rural area presents to a local health clinic with complaints of vision problems. Upon examination, you notice small, raised, triangular, foamy, and whitish-gray lesions on the bulbar conjunctiva lateral to the cornea in both eyes. The rest of the ocular examination is unremarkable. The patient's parents report a diet primarily consisting of maize. What is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/09/picture17_AHGdtbp.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Pterygium (plural: pterygia) is a non-cancerous , wedge-shaped growth of tissue that typically starts on the conjunctiva and can extend onto the cornea
• Option C. A pinguecula is a small , raised, white - or yellow-colored growth that is limited to the conjunctiva ; it can occur on the inner or outer side of the eye. A pinguecula may contain deposits of protein , fat or calcium . For most people it does not affect vision , but in severe cases, it can interfere with the way the tears cover the eye, causing dryness , redness and inflammation .
• Option D. Conjunctival nevus, also known as a conjunctival melanocytic nevus , is a benign pigmented lesion that occurs on the conjunctiva. Conjunctival nevi appear as flat or slightly raised , well-defined , pigmented spots on the conjunctiva . They can vary in color from light brown to dark brown or black , depending on the amount of melanin pigment .
• Educational Objective:
• R aised , triangular , foamy , and whitish-gray lesions on the bulbar conjunctiva is suggective of Bitot’s spot and requires further evaluation for Vit A deficiency .
explanation.ref: Pg 468/ Systemic and Community Ophthalmology/ Comprehensive Ophthalmology AK KHURANA/ 6 th Edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Which among the following is not true about subconjunctival haemorrhage?
options: Occurs due to sudden rupture of conjunctival capillaries
Blood dyscrasias like anemia, leukemia can cause SCH
Assurance is the most important part of treatment

explanation.short: Ans. D) None of the above
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Occurs due to sudden rupture of conjunctival capillaries: This is true . Subconjunctival hemorrhage typically occurs due to the spontaneous rupture of small blood vessels in the conjunctiva . This can happen due to minor trauma , sudden increases in pressure (like coughing , sneezing , or straining ), or even spontaneously without a clear cause .
• Option B. Blood dyscrasias like anemia, leukemia can cause SCH : This is also true . Blood dyscrasias (disorders related to the blood) such as anemia or leukemia can predispose an individual to SCH . These conditions can affect the clotting mechanism or the integrity of blood vessels , making them more susceptible to bleeding .
• Option C. Assurance is the most important part of treatment : This statement is generally true . In most cases, subconjunctival hemorrhage is a benign , self-limiting condition that does not require specific medical treatment . The primary approach is to reassure the patient about the nature of the condition, as it often resolves on its own without intervention . However, if an underlying systemic cause is suspected, such as hypertension or a blood dyscrasia, appropriate medical evaluation and treatment for these conditions are necessary.
• Educational Objective:
• Subcinjunctival Haemorrhage occurs due to sudden rupture of conjunctival capillaries , or blood dyscrasias . Assurance is an important part of treatment .
explanation.ref: Pg 90/ / CONJUNCTIVA/ Comprehensive Ophthalmology AK KHURANA/ 6 th Edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Safe strategy includes all, except which of the following?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Antibiotics: Trachoma is caused by the bacterium Chlamydia trachomatis . The World Health Organization (WHO) recommends the use of antibiotics such as azithromycin or tetracycline eye ointment to treat active infection . This is a cornerstone of the SAFE strategy for trachoma control .
• Option B. Facial Hygiene : Improved facial hygiene, especially regular face washing , can significantly reduce the transmission of trachoma . This is because the bacteria are spread through contact with discharge from the eyes and nose , particularly among children. Improved hygiene practices are an integral part of the SAFE strategy , aiming to reduce the incidence of new infections .
• Option D. Surgical Correction: This refers to surgical procedures to treat trichiasis , a late complication of trachoma where eyelashes turn inward and scratch the cornea , potentially leading to blindness . The surgery corrects the position of the eyelashes and is an essential component of the SAFE strategy .
• Educational Objective:
• In the context of the SAFE strategy for trachoma control, the correct components are Antibiotics , Facial hygiene , and Surgical correction . Enucleation , which is the surgical removal of the eye , is not a part of this strategy and is reserved for other severe , end-stage ocular conditions.
• KANSKI 9 TH EDITION PAGE 176
explanation.ref: KANSKI 9 TH EDITION PAGE 176

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Corneal endothelium receives most of its oxygen supply from:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. The tear film primarily supplies oxygen to the corneal epithelium (the outermost layer of the cornea), not the endothelium (the innermost layer).
• Option B. Atmospheric air is a major source of oxygen for the corneal epithelium when the eye is open. However, the corneal endothelium does not directly access oxygen from the air.
• Option D. The vitreous humour is the gel-like substance filling the space between the lens and the retina . It does not play a significant role in supplying oxygen to the corneal endothelium.
• Educational Objective
• The corneal endothelium, being an avascular layer , primarily receives its oxygen supply from the aqueous humour , which nourishes the inner layers of the cornea .
explanation.ref: pg 40/ anatomy and physiology of eye/ AK KHURANA/ 3 rd edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Which of the following pump system(s) is/are present on the corneal endothelium?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• The pump systems present on the corneal endothelium are:
• Na- K ATPase Na- H Bicarbonate dependent ATPase Carbonic Anhydrase Enzyme
explanation.ref: pg 45/ / Anatomy & Physiology of Eye /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
A 36-year-old woman who regularly wears contact lenses came to the ophthalmologist with complaints of severe pain in right eye along with redness and blurring of vision since the past few days. Her eye is shown below. Which of the following given options can be considered for her treatment?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/05/10/c29.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Cefazolin is a cephalosporin antibiotic used against a range of bacterial infections. However, it is not effective against Acanthamoeba and is not used in the treatment of Acanthamoeba keratitis.
• Option C. Tobramycin is an aminoglycoside antibiotic . While it is effective against various bacterial infections and is commonly used in treating bacterial keratitis , it is not effective against Acanthamoeba.
• Option D. Voriconazole is an antifungal medication . While it has a broad spectrum of activity , its use in Acanthamoeba keratitis is more limited. It may be considered in certain cases, particularly for fungal co-infections , but it's not a first-line treatment for Acanthamoeba.
• Educational Objective
• For the treatment of Acanthamoeba keratitis, chlorhexidine is considered effective and is commonly used, as opposed to antibiotics like cefazolin and tobramycin or antifungals like voriconazole, which are not primarily effective against this protozoan infection.
explanation.ref: KANSKI 9 TH EDITION PAGE 232

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The minimum oxygen tension for normal corneal hydration ranges between?

explanation.short: Ans. B) 11 to 19 mm of Hg
explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• The minimal O 2 tension for normal corneal dehydration ranges between 11 to 19 mm of Hg .
• Below this critical range, the cornea will hydrate and swell .
explanation.ref: pg 40/ anatomy and physiology of eye/ AK KHURANA/ 3 rd edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The most actively metabolising layers of cornea are?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Epithelium & Bowman’s Layer : The epithelium is a highly active metabolic layer . However, Bowman's layer, which lies beneath the epithelium, is an acellular zone and does not engage in active metabolism.
• Option B. Epithelium & Stroma : The stroma is the thickest layer of the cornea and contains collagen and keratocytes . While it has some metabolic activity, it is not as metabolically active as the epithelium or endothelium.
• Option C. Epithelium & Descemet’s Membrane : Descemet's membrane is a thin , acellular layer produced by the endothelial cells . It does not have significant metabolic activity.
• Educational Objective
• The epithelium and endothelium are the most actively metabolizing layers of the cornea , playing vital roles in corneal health, repair , and functionality.
explanation.ref: pg 40/ anatomy and physiology of eye/ AK KHURANA/ 3 rd edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Which of the following is/are true about MAURICE theory?
options: Scattered light is destroyed by mutual interference
Loss of transparency results if lattice arrangement is altered.

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective
• MAURICE or LATTICE THEORY
• Arrangement is altered by stromal edema & mechanical stress If the distance between collagen fibrils increases , it leads to bullae formation . Corneal transparency is maintained by Dehydrated state (hydration leads to loss of transparency) Avascularity Regular arrangement of corneal lamellae Unmyleinated nerve fibres
explanation.ref: pg 42/ anatomy and physiology of eye/ AK KHURANA/ 3 rd edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The normal corneal stromal swelling pressure is __ mm of Hg:

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Identify the false statement among the following:
options: Corneal epithelium being hydrophilic is crossed readily by polar drugs.
Fluorescein cannot penetrate intact epithelium which forms the basis of dye test.
Dipivefrin is a pro drug which is converted into epinephrine after absorption into the eye.
Contact lenses reduce the availability of oxygen to epithelium.

explanation.short: Ans. A) Corneal epithelium being hydrophilic is crossed readily by polar drugs.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Fluorescein and Intact Epithelium : Fluorescein cannot penetrate an intact corneal epithelium. The dye test with fluorescein is used to detect epithelial defects ; the dye will only penetrate where there are disruptions in the epithelium .
• Option C. Dipivefrin as a Prodrug : Dipivefrin is a prodrug of epinephrine . It is designed to be more lipophilic , allowing better penetration through the cornea . Once inside the eye, it is converted to epinephrine .
• Option D. Contact Lenses and Oxygen Availability : Contact lenses, especially those that are not gas-permeable, can reduce the amount of oxygen that reaches the corneal epithelium . This can lead to complications such as corneal hypoxia , especially with prolonged wear .
• Educational Objective:
• The corneal epithelium's lipophilic nature means that it is not readily crossed by polar drugs. Understanding the epithelium's characteristics is important for effective ocular drug delivery .
explanation.ref: pg 46/ anatomy and physiology of eye/ AK KHURANA/ 3 rd edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The corneal stromal lamellae consist of fibrils made of collagen except:

explanation.detail: • Educational Objective:
explanation.ref: pg 34/ anatomy and physiology of eye/ AK KHURANA/ 3 rd edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The effects of contact lens on cornea include all except?
options: Reduced availability of oxygen to the epithelium
Shift of aerobic metabolism to anaerobic
Inhibition of aerobic enzymes such as hexokinase

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Reduced Availability of Oxygen to the Epithelium: Contact lenses , especially those that are not gas-permeable, can reduce the oxygen supply to the corneal epithelium , leading to hypoxic conditions .
• Option B. Shift of Aerobic Metabolism to Anaerobic: When the oxygen supply to the cornea is reduced due to contact lens wear , the corneal cells may shift from aerobic to anaerobic metabolism to meet their energy needs .
• Option D. Inhibition of Aerobic Enzymes Such as Hexokinase: Prolonged hypoxic conditions caused by contact lens wear can lead to inhibition of enzymes involved in aerobic metabolism . Hexokinase is an enzyme involved early in the glycolysis pathway , and its activity can be affected by changes in the cellular environment due to contact lens use .
• Educational Objective
• Contact lens wear can lead to reduced oxygen availability to the cornea, a shift from aerobic to anaerobic metabolism , and an increase in lactate levels , not a decrease.

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Protein synthesis is maximum in which layer of cornea?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Stroma: While the stroma is the thickest layer of the cornea and contains collagen and keratocytes , its rate of protein synthesis is less compared to the epithelium. The stromal cells are relatively quiescent under normal conditions.
• Option C. Descemet’s Membrane: Descemet's membrane is a specialized basement membrane produced by the endothelial cells . While it does involve protein synthesis , particularly during its formation and repair , it is not the site of maximum protein synthesis in the cornea.
• Option D. Endothelium: The endothelium is a single layer of cells on the inner surface of the cornea . These cells are metabolically active and involved in maintaining corneal hydration and transparency , but their rate of protein synthesis is not as high as that of the epithelial cells.
• Educational Objective
• The corneal epithelium is the site of maximum protein synthesis in the cornea , reflecting its role in continual cell renewal and maintenance of corneal integrity .
explanation.ref: pg 37/ Anatomy & Physiology of Eye /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Major net weight is contributed by which biochemical component in the cornea?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Proteins are a crucial component of the cornea , particularly in the form of collagen in the stroma , but they do not constitute the majority of the cornea's weight.
• Option B. Mucopolysaccharides, or glycosaminoglycans, are important for the structure and function of the cornea , especially in maintaining its transparency and hydration . However, they do not make up the majority of the cornea's weight.
• Option D. While lipids are present in the cornea, they are not a major component in terms of weight. The cornea is relatively avascular and does not have a significant lipid content compared to other tissues.
• Educational Objective
• The cornea's major net weight is contributed by water , which is essential for maintaining its transparency, thickness , and overall function .
explanation.ref: pg 38 / Anatomy & Physiology of Eye /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The major glycosaminoglycans (GAGs) fractions found in cornea are all except:

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Cornea contains 3 major glycosaminoglycan fractions:
• Keratan Sulphate (50%) Dermatan Sulphate (25%) Chondroitin (25%)
• 2. AAO/ 2020–2021 BCSC Basic and Clinical Science Course / 2 Fundamentals and Principles of Ophthalmology/Part IV: Biochemistry and Metabolism/Chapter 8: Cornea
explanation.ref: pg. 39 / Anatomy & Physiology of Eye /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Metabolic pathways in cornea include all except:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Glycolysis is a fundamental metabolic pathway that occurs in the cornea . It involves the breakdown of glucose to produce energy , and it is especially crucial in the corneal epithelium .
• Option C. Krebs Cycle, also known as the citric acid cycle , this is a key metabolic pathway that takes place in the mitochondria . The cornea, particularly the endothelium and epithelium, uses the Krebs cycle for energy production.
• Option D . Hexose Monophosphate Pathway, also known as the pentose phosphate pathway , is present in the cornea . It's important for generating NADPH and ribose-5-phosphate , essential for biosynthetic reactions and maintaining redox balance in cells .
• Educational Objective
• In the cornea, metabolic pathways like glycolysis , the Krebs cycle , and the hexose monophosphate pathway are active , but gluconeogenesis is not a significant metabolic process in the cornea.
explanation.ref: pg 41/ Anatomy & Physiology of Eye /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Ananya, a 22-year-old female who has myopia visits the ophthalmologist and requests her to eliminate the need to wear spectacles in order to see clearly. The ophthalmologist examines her thoroughly, suggests her a surgery involving the cornea which can solve her problem. Of the outer fibrous coat of the eyeball, what fraction is contributed by the cornea?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Cornea contributes the anterior 1/6 th of the outer coat of the eye .
explanation.ref: pg 2/ Anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: In 2013, the Dua’s layer of the cornea was discovered by?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Layers of the cornea outermost to innermost :
• Epithelium: Grips the tear film with help of microvilli. Bowman’s membrane: Not a true basement membrane (PAS negative). Stroma: Thickest layer. Contains Collagen 1,3,5+ GAGs (Keratan Sulphate). Dua’s layer: Strongest layer. Descemet’s membrane Endothelium: Most metabolically active. Max Na+/K+ pumps.
explanation.ref: pg 35/ Anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 16-year-old boy came to consult you in your OPD where he gave history of trauma to his right eye. On carrying out slit-lamp examination you see a superficial injury on the cornea. Usually, in injuries involving only the corneal epithelium, the stratified squamous epithelium rapidly regenerates because of the presence of stem cells. Where are the Palisades of Vogt containing the stem cells located?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. The retina is the light-sensitive layer that processes visual information .
• Option C. Aqueous Humour is the clear fluid in the anterior chamber of the eye , between the cornea and the lens .
• Option D. Cornea, while the Palisades of Vogt are closely related to the health and maintenance of the cornea, they are specifically located in the limbus , not within the cornea itself.
• Educational Objective:
• The Palisades of Vogt, containing stem cells crucial for corneal regeneration and repair , are located in the limbus , the border area between the cornea and the sclera .
explanation.ref: pg 50/ Anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Each medium through which light rays travel to reach the retina has a distinct refractive power. The refractive power of the cornea is?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The refractive power of anterior surface of the cornea is +48 D. Posterior surface of the cornea is - 5D. Net refractive power of the cornea =   + 43 D 3/4 TH the total refractive power of the eye. The rest 1/4 th is contributed by Lens (+20D).
• Educational Objective:
• The net refractive power of cornea is +43 D .
explanation.ref: pg 32/ Anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A patient is being worked-up to be posted for LASIK surgery is undergoing Pachymetry, to check for the corneal thickness. Which layer of the cornea will have maximum thickness?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Epithelium: The corneal epithelium is the outermost layer of the cornea and is relatively thin , typically about 50-60 micrometers in thickness . It serves as a barrier against dust, debris , and microorganisms .
• Option C. Descemet's membrane is a thin layer of tissue that serves as the basement membrane of the corneal endothelium. Its thickness is much less compared to the stroma, only about 5-10 micrometers .
• Option D. The endothelium is the innermost layer of the cornea and is also quite thin , typically 5 micrometers or less in thickness . It is responsible for maintaining corneal dehydration and transparency .
• Educational Objective
• The stromal layer is the thickest part of the cornea , making up about 90% of its total thickness , and is crucial for the cornea's tensile strength and form .
explanation.ref: pg 96/ CORNEA/ Comprehensive ophthalmology/ AK KHURANA/ 6 th edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The corneal reflex is a demonstration performed by gently touching the cornea with a sterile cotton swab. This causes a protective reflex where the patient's eye immediately closes involuntarily. It helps in assessing the integrity of the sensory and motor pathways involved in eye protection. Which of the following is the afferent for this reflex?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Oculomotor Nerve: This is a motor nerve involved in controlling most of the eye's movements and is not the afferent limb of the corneal reflex.
• Option B. Short Ciliary Nerve: They supply parasympathetic and sympathetic nerve fibers to the ciliary muscle, iris , and cornea .
• Option C. Optic Nerve transmits visual information from the retina to the brain and is not involved in the sensory pathway of the corneal reflex.
• Educational Objective
• In the corneal reflex, the long ciliary nerves serve as the afferent limb , responsible for transmitting sensory information from the cornea to the brain upon stimulation.
• AAO/2020–2021 BCSC Basic and Clinical Science Course/ 7 Oculofacial Plastic and Orbital Surgery / Part I: Orbit/Chapter 1: Orbital Anatomy Yang AY, Chow J, Liu J. Corneal Innervation and Sensation: The Eye and Beyond. Yale J Biol Med. 2018 Mar 28;91(1):13-21. PMID: 29599653; PMCID: PMC5872636.

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Which of the following factors does not contribute to the control of drug penetration through the corneal epithelium?
options: Duration of contact of drug with epithelium

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Duration of Contact of Drug with Epithelium: The longer a drug remains in contact with the corneal epithelium, the greater the opportunity for absorption. Factors such as formulation and viscosity of the drug can affect this contact time.
• Option C. Molecular Weight <500: The molecular weight of a drug can influence its ability to penetrate biological membranes .
• Option D. Degree of Dissociation of Electrolytes: The corneal epithelium is more permeable to non-ionized ( non-dissociated ) drug forms . The degree of ionization (dissociation) affects drug penetration , as ionized molecules typically penetrate less effectively.
• Educational Objective
• The corneal epithelium is lipophilic ; therefore, drugs that are too hydrophilic ( water-soluble ) may have difficulty penetrating it . Optimal penetration often requires a balance between lipophilic and hydrophilic properties .
explanation.ref: PARSONS DISEASES OF EYE 22 ND EDITION PAGE 146

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
A 35-year-old farmer came to the ophthalmologist with complaints of a pain, redness, increased sensitivity to light and blurring of vision in left eye. He gave history of ocular trauma with a twig, 1 day back. After examination, the ophthalmologist suspects fungal keratitis and takes corneal scrapings to confirm her diagnosis. The following organism is observed under the microscope for which immediate treatment is to be initiated to prevent further corneal damage. What is the drug of choice in this case?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/05/10/c28.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Amphotericin B is more commonly used for yeast-like fungal infections (e.g., Candida) rather than filamentous fungal infections. It can be used in certain cases of severe or resistant fungal keratitis , but it is not the first choice.
• Option C. Fluconazole is effective against yeasts but has limited activity against filamentous fungi. It is not typically the first-line treatment for filamentous fungal corneal ulcers.
• Option D. Voriconazole is a broad-spectrum antifungal that can be effective against a wide range of fungi, including filamentous fungi. It is sometimes used in cases where natamycin is ineffective or the fungus is known to be resistant to natamycin . However, natamycin remains the first-line treatment.
• Educational Objective
• In the management of filamentous fungal corneal ulcers, natamycin is the treatment of choice due to its effectiveness against common filamentous fungi that cause these infections .
explanation.ref: KANSKI 9 TH EDITION PAGE 218

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Which of the following drugs is not associated with the clinical presentation shown below?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/08/01/whatsapp-image-2023-06-12-at-1901210110107.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Amiodarone is a medication used for certain types of cardiac arrhythmias . One of its known side effects is cornea verticillata . The deposits are usually asymptomatic and do not typically necessitate discontinuation of the medication.
• Option C. Chlorpromazine is an antipsychotic medication that can cause cornea verticillata . The deposits are again usually asymptomatic but can be a consideration in long-term treatment with chlorpromazine.
• Option D. Indomethacin, a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) , has been associated with the development of cornea verticillata . Like the other drugs listed, these deposits are typically asymptomatic .
• Educational Objective
• In the context of drugs causing cornea verticillata , ciprofloxacin does not typically lead to this condition, unlike amiodarone , chlorpromazine, and indomethacin , which are known to cause vortex keratopathy as a side effect .
explanation.ref: PARSONS DISEASES OF THE EYE 22 ND EDITION PAGE 220

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 29-year-old female came to the ophthalmologist with complaints of a pain in the eye and blurring of vision. She was diagnosed with a corneal ulcer and was sent for corneal scraping. Which of the following topical anaesthetic agents is preferred for the planned procedure?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
