subject: Ophthalmology
question:
This patient presented with bilateral ptosis. On application of ice, there was improvement in the ptosis. The likely diagnosis would be:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/12/13/picture22_sloPXMf.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Myotonic Dystrophy : This is a type of muscular dystrophy, characterized by progressive muscle wasting and weakness . It does not typically respond to the ice pack test.
• Option C . Progressive External Ophthalmoplegia (PEO): This is a condition characterized by weakness of the eye muscles . While PEO shares some symptoms with myasthenia gravis, such as drooping eyelids and double vision, it does not respond to the ice pack test.
• Option D . Millard-Gubler Syndrome: This is a rare condition characterized by facial paralysis , abducens nerve paralysis , and contralateral hemiplegia . It is not associated with a positive response to the ice pack test.
• Educational Objective
• The ice pack test is most commonly used to diagnose myasthenia gravis , particularly when ocular symptoms like ptosis are present . It is not typically used or effective in diagnosing myotonic dystrophy , progressive external ophthalmoplegia, or Millard-Gubler syndrome .
explanation.ref: KANSKI 9 TH EDITION page 815

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 48-year-old male patient with a history of RTA has multiple facial bone fractures with injury to optic nerve. The optic nerve fibres do not regenerate as they are not covered by:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Meninges : The meninges are the protective coverings of the brain and spinal cord , not individual nerves. While they are important for overall protection of the central nervous system, they are not directly involved in nerve regeneration.
• Option C . Myelin Sheath : The myelin sheath is present in both the central and peripheral nervous systems and serves to insulate nerve fibers and enhance signal transmission . In the CNS, including the optic nerve, oligodendrocytes form the myelin sheath. The presence or absence of myelin is not the primary factor determining the regenerative capacity of nerve fibers.
• Educational Objective
• Understand that the optic nerve fibers , part of the central nervous system , do not regenerate after injury due to the absence of the neurilemma , which is crucial for nerve regeneration in the peripheral nervous system .
explanation.ref: pg 179/ Anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 35-year-old female patient presents to the ophthalmology clinic with complaints of decreased vision and visual field defects in her right eye following a head injury sustained in a fall. Upon examination, the ophthalmologist suspects optic nerve damage and decides to investigate further. As part of the evaluation, the ophthalmologist wants to determine the total length of the patient's optic nerve to assess the extent of the potential injury. Normally, what is the total length of the optic nerve?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• The length of the optic nerve is 47-40 mm
• length of the optic nerve is 47-40 mm
explanation.ref: pg 180/ Anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The cranial nerve with the longest intracranial course is?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . 3 (Oculomotor Nerve) : While the oculomotor nerve has an important and complex role in eye movement, controlling most of the eye's muscles , its intracranial course is not as long as that of the trochlear nerve.
• Option C . 6 (Abducens Nerve) : The abducens nerve has a significant intracranial course , but it is not the longest . It innervates the lateral rectus muscle of the eye.
• Option D . 2 (Optic Nerve) : The optic nerve, which is responsible for vision, has a significant pathway starting from the retina to the optic chiasm and then to the brain . However, its intracranial length is not the longest among the cranial nerves.
• Educational Objective:
• Recognize that the cranial nerve with the longest intracranial course is the trochlear nerve (CN IV).
explanation.ref: pg 546/ anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A patient who came to the OPD had complete loss of vision in right eye. The lesion was suspected to be present in the ipsilateral optic nerve. What are the optic nerve fibres made up of?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Bipolar Cells : These cells are located in the retina and act as intermediaries between photoreceptors (rods and cones) and ganglion cells . They play a crucial role in the visual process within the retina but are not part of the optic nerve fibers.
• Option C . Rods & Cones : These are the photoreceptors in the retina . Rods are responsible for vision at low light levels (scotopic vision), while cones are active at higher light levels (photopic vision) and are capable of color vision. They are the first step in the visual transduction process but are not part of the optic nerve itself.
• Educational Objective
• Understand that the optic nerve fibers are composed of the axons of retinal ganglion cells , which transmit visual information from the retina to the brain .
explanation.ref: pg 179/ anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Which of the following is derived embryologically from para-axial mesenchyme?

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 35-year-old female patient presents to the endocrinology clinic with complaints of persistent headaches, visual disturbances, and irregular menstrual cycles. On physical examination, the patient's visual field is found to be compromised. You are suspecting a pituitary tumour. In case of a pituitary tumour, which part of the visual pathway is affected leading to the visual disturbance?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Optic Nerve : While the optic nerve can be affected by lesions or conditions anterior to the chiasm , such as optic neuritis or glaucoma , it is less likely to be directly affected by a pituitary tumor unless the tumor is very large or has an unusual pattern of growth.
• Option C . Optic Tract : The optic tract carries visual information from the optic chiasm to the lateral geniculate nucleus of the thalamus . It is less commonly affected by pituitary tumors compared to the optic chiasm.
• Option D. Lateral Geniculate Body : This is a part of the thalamus that acts as a relay center for visual information on its way to the visual cortex . It is not typically affected by pituitary tumors.
• Educational Objective
• Recognize that pituitary tumors most commonly affect the optic chiasm , leading to a characteristic bitemporal hemianopia due to compression of the crossing fibers from the nasal halves of each retina .
explanation.ref: pg 183/ Anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Identify the test given below:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/04/29/11.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . City University Color Vision Test : This is another type of color vision test , but it is not the same as the Ishihara test .
• Option C . Hardy, Rand, and Rittler (HRR) Plates : These are another set of pseudoisochromatic plates used for color vision testing , focusing on a wider range of color vision deficiencies , including blue-yellow deficiencies , but they are distinct from the Ishihara test.
• Option D . American Optical/Hardy-Rand-Rittler (AO HRR) Test : This is a revised version of the HRR test , used for detecting both red-green and blue-yellow color vision deficiencies , and it is not the same as the Ishihara test.
• Educational Objective
• Understand that the Ishihara chart is a type of pseudoisochromatic plate test , specifically designed for detecting red-green color vision deficiencies .
explanation.ref: Comprehensive Ophthalmology Khurana Page 326

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Identify the chart given below:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/04/29/22.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The chart shown is log MAR chart (Modified Bailey-Lovie chart) There are five letters in each row . The chart has a constant geometric progression , with the size of letters in each row decreasing by a factor of log 10 10. The distance between each letter is equal to the width of the letter . The chart used in the Early Treatment of Diabetic Retinopathy Study (ETDRS)
explanation.ref: Parson’s diseases of the eye 22 nd edition page 99

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The Snellen’s chart for distant vision was used to test a patient. He was able to read the letters on the top line of the chart clearly. The visual acuity of the patient is?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Snellen’s chart is assessed at a distance of 6m . If the patient reads the top line clearly (i.e., 60) at a distance of 6 metres , the visual acuity of the patient is 6/60 , indicating that the patient can read something from a distance of 6 meters that a person with normal vision can read from a distance of 60 meters.
explanation.ref: Parson’s diseases of eye 22 nd edition Page 99

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
The given chart is used in testing which of the following?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/04/29/33.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Color Vision : This is tested using methods like the Ishihara test , not the Pelli-Robson chart.
• Option B . Distance Visual Acuity : This is typically measured using charts like the Snellen chart , which have letters or symbols in different sizes.
• Option C . Near Acuity : This refers to the sharpness of vision at a short distance , often tested with reading charts or similar tools.
• Educational Objective
• Recognize that the Pelli-Robson chart is used to assess contrast sensitivity .
explanation.ref: Clinical optics by Elkington Page: 16

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A patient has come to the OPD with a complaint of blurring of vision in left eye. You are asked to test the patient’s vision using a Jaeger chart. What should be the distance between the chart and patient?

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 14-year-old girl comes to the ophthalmologist with a complaint of difficulty in seeing the class blackboard since 1 month. Her visual acuity is tested using Snellen’s chart. In this chart, the lines comprising each alphabet have a certain breadth, which subtends a particular angle at the nodal point of the patient’s eye. What is the angle subtended?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option D. 5 Minutes: The angle subteneded by the entire optotype at the nodal point of the eye is 5 minutes.
• Educational Objective
• Understand that in the Snellen chart, the breadth of the lines comprising each alphabet subtends a visual angle of 1 minute of arc at the patient's nodal point , which is a key aspect of its design for assessing visual acuity.
explanation.ref: pg 266/ Anatomy and physiology of eye/ AK KHURANA/ 3 rd edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 30-year-old man comes to the ophthalmologist with a complaint of blurring of vision since 15 days, noticed while driving. His visual acuity is to be tested using Snellen’s chart. The chart comprises of alphabets that are designed to fit in a square and subtend a particular angle at the nodal point of the patient’s eye. What is the angle subtended?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Each letter of the snellen’s chart is so designed that it fits in a square , and subtends an angle of 5 minutes at the nodal point .
• Educational Objective
• Understand that in the Snellen chart , the breadth of the optotype subtends a visual angle of 5 minute of arc at the patient's nodal point , which is a key aspect of its design for assessing visual acuity.
explanation.ref: pg 266/ Anatomy and physiology of eye/ AK KHURANA/ 3 rd edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 3-year-old child was brought to the ophthalmologist by her parents, complaining that she squints her eyes while watching TV and moves closer to the screen. Her visual acuity is to be tested. Which chart can be used?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Snellen’s Test : This chart uses letters and is generally used for individuals who are old enough to recognize and verbalize letters , usually around age 5 and older .
• Option B. Log MAR Chart : The Logarithm of the Minimum Angle of Resolution (Log MAR) chart also uses letters and is designed for a more precise measurement of visual acuity . Like the Snellen’s test, it is not suitable for young children who cannot read.
• Option C . Landolt’s C Chart : This chart uses a "C" shape and requires the test taker to identify the opening direction of the " C." It's more suitable for older children and adults , especially those who might have literacy or language barriers but can understand and follow instructions.
• Educational Objective
• Recognize that the visual acuity of a 3-year-old child is best tested with a picture chart , which uses simple and recognizable symbols appropriate for the child’s age and developmental stage .
explanation.ref: pg 268/ Anatomy and physiology of eye/ AK KHURANA/ 3 rd edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The storage form of vitamin A in liver is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Retinal : This is one of the active forms of vitamin A , important in the visual cycle, particularly in the conversion of light to visual signals in the retina of the eye . However, it is not the primary storage form of vitamin A in the liver.
• Option B . Retinoic Acid : This is another active form of vitamin A, crucial for regulating gene transcription and playing a role in processes such as cell growth and differentiation . Like retinal, it is not a storage form.
• Option D . Rhodopsin : This is a visual pigment found in the retina , formed by the combination of retinal and a protein called opsin. It is essential for vision in low-light conditions but is not a form of vitamin A storage in the liver.
• Educational Objective
• Understand that vitamin A is stored in the liver primarily as retinol , which can be converted to other active forms like retinal and retinoic acid as needed for various physiological functions.
explanation.ref: pg 201/ / Anatomy and physiology of eye/ AK KHURANA/ 3 rd edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Which of the following is also known as the VISUAL PURPLE?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Conopsin: Pigment present in cones of the eye .
• Option B: 11 cis retinal: Vitamin A derivative which combines with opsin to form Rhodopsin .
• Option D: Retinoic acid: Prevents squamous metaplasia and causes cellular differentiation.
• Educational Objective:
• Rhodopsin is known as visual purple .
explanation.ref: pg 202/ Anatomy and physiology of eye/ AK KHURANA/ 3 rd edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: What is the normal dark adaptation time in any individual?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The normal dark adaptation time is 20 to 30 minutes. It refers to the duration it takes for the eyes to adjust and regain optimal vision in low-light or dark conditions after being exposed to bright light. Dark adaptation occurs in two stages: Cone Adaptation : This occurs within the first 5 to 10 minutes . Rod Adaptation : Following cone adaptation, the rods (photoreceptor cells that are more sensitive than cones and better suited for low-light conditions) take over. This stage can take an additional 15 to 20 minutes, completing the process of dark adaptation. The rate of adaptation can vary based on factors such as age, the intensity of the preceding light exposure, and the individual's overall ocular health. Dark adaption Curve:
• Educational Objective
• Understanding the time course of dark adaptation is important for medical students , as it offers insights into the functioning of rods and cones in the retina and can be relevant in diagnosing and understanding various eye conditions that affect night vision .

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The ganglion cells responsible for colour vision is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. W cells : These are a less well-defined class of ganglion cells with diverse functions , but they are not primarily responsible for color vision.
• Option C. Y cells (M cells or magnocellular cells) : These cells are involved in processing motion and spatial information. They have larger receptive fields and are less sensitive to color, but more sensitive to large, fast-moving objects.
• Educational Objective
• X cells (P cells/Parvocellular Cells) are the retinal ganglion cells primarily responsible for color vision .
explanation.ref: pg 212/Anatomy and physiology of eye/ AK KHURANA/ 3 rd edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The primary visual cortex is area_______:

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective
• Area 17 is the primary visual cortex.
explanation.ref: pg 214/ Anatomy and physiology of eye/ AK KHURANA/ 3 rd edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The secondary visual cortex is area______?.

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Area 17 : This is known as the primary visual cortex or V1 . It's the main area responsible for processing visual information. Areas 16 and 20 : These are not typically associated with primary or secondary visual processing in the standard anatomical numbering of the cerebral cortex .
• Educational Objective
• The secondary visual cortex comprises Brodmann areas 18 and 19 and is crucial for understanding the brain's visual processing pathways .
explanation.ref: pg 214/ Anatomy and physiology of eye/ AK KHURANA/ 3 rd edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: All of the following are the primary colours except:

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• The primary colours are :
explanation.ref: 284/ Anatomy and physiology of eye/ AK KHURANA/ 3 rd edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Red green colour blindness is seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Retinal detachment : While serious and can lead to permanent vision loss , retinal detachment does not typically cause red-green color blindness . Symptoms usually include floaters, flashes of light, and a shadow or curtain effect over part of the visual field.
• Option C: Central serous retinopathy : This condition involves fluid buildup under the retina but doesn't usually result in red-green color blindness . Symptoms typically include distorted and blurred central vision.
• Option D: Glaucoma : This is a group of eye conditions that damage the optic nerve, usually due to high eye pressure. It primarily affects peripheral vision initially and can lead to tunnel vision in advanced stages, but it does not typically cause red-green color blindness.
• Educational Objective
• Understanding that while optic neuritis can alter color perception , classical red-green color blindness is genetic and not typically a symptom of common eye diseases like retinal detachment , central serous retinopathy, or glaucoma.
explanation.ref: pg 294/ Anatomy and physiology of eye/ AK KHURANA/ 3 rd edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The inheritance of colour blindness is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• OPTION A AND D: Autosomal dominant and autosomal recessive : These are other modes of inheritance involving non-sex chromosomes (autosomes), but they are not the primary modes for color blindness.
• OPTION B. Sporadic : This term is used when a condition occurs randomly or by chance , without a clear genetic link . Color blindness is generally not considered sporadic since it has a well-established genetic basis.
• Educational Objective
• Recognizing that color blindness is most commonly an X-linked condition is important for understanding its genetic transmission and for providing accurate genetic counseling , particularly in assessing the risk for male offspring in affected families.
explanation.ref: Pg 292/ Anatomy and physiology of eye/ AK KHURANA/ 3 rd edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: All are tests for colour vision except:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Farnsworth Munsell 100 hue test: This is indeed a color vision test . It is a highly detailed, sensitive test used to analyze an individual's ability to discern color. It involves the arrangement of colored caps in order of their hues.
• Option B. Holmgren’s wool test: This is also a color vision test . It involves matching colored wools under standardized lighting conditions. It's a simple and historical method used to assess color discrimination.
• Option C. Nagel’s anomaloscope test: This test is used for diagnosing red-green color deficiencies . It's a more precise test where individuals match the brightness and color of a light mixture composed of red and green lights to a yellow light.
• Educational Objective:
• Understand that the Farnsworth Munsell 100 hue test , Holmgren’s wool test , and Nagel’s anomaloscope test are all tests for color vision , whereas the pinhole test is used for assessing visual acuity, not color vision.
explanation.ref: pg 296/ Anatomy and physiology of eye/ AK KHURANA/ 3 rd edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The gene associated with red & green colour blindness is located on:

explanation.short: Ans. B) q arm of X chromosome

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Which of the following is derived embryologically from paraxial mesenchyme?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Optic nerve fibres : The optic nerve is primarily derived from the outgrowth of the diencephalon, which is a part of the central nervous system . Thus, its embryological origin is neuroectodermal, not mesenchymal.
• Option C. Ciliary body epithelium : The ciliary body epithelium, which includes the non-pigmented and pigmented epithelium of the ciliary body , originates from the anterior portion of the neural retina and the outer layer of the optic cup , respectively. This is also neuroectodermal in origin.
• Option D . Melanocytes : Melanocytes in the body are derived from neural crest cells, which, like the meninges, originate from ectomesenchyme. They are not derived from paraxial mesenchyme.
• Educational Objective:
• Understand that the optic nerve sheath is derived from paraxial mesenchyme , highlighting the mesenchymal contribution to the structure of the meninges and their extensions .
explanation.ref: pg 12/ COMPREHENSIVE ophthalmology/ 6 th edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 62-year-old male patient comes to the OPD with complaints of a swelling on the eyelid. He gives history of a similar complaint multiple times in the past. The ophthalmologist diagnoses it as a case of recurrent chalazion and sends it for histopathological examination. What is the ophthalmologist excluding the probability of?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Squamous Cell Carcinoma : This is a type of skin cancer that can occur on the eyelids , often resulting from prolonged exposure to ultraviolet (UV) light . While it can present as a lesion on the eyelid, its clinical appearance is usually different from that of a chalazion , typically showing more superficial, scaly, and ulcerative features .
• Option B . Basal Cell Carcinoma : The most common type of eyelid cancer , basal cell carcinoma often presents as a painless , slowly growing nodule on the eyelid with potential ulceration or a pearly appearance.
• Option C. Malignant Melanoma : This is a type of cancer that develops from the pigment-containing cells known as melanocytes.
• Educational Objective:
• The histopathological examination of a recurrent chalazion is primarily conducted to exclude the possibility of sebaceous cell carcinoma .
explanation.ref: pg 369 – eyelids- 6 th edition – comprehensive ophthalmology ak khurana

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
A 2-year-old male child was brought to the OPD with a history of drooping upper left eyelid since birth. What is the surgical procedure performed to correct ptosis in this case?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/05/12/83.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Fasanella-Servat : This procedure is typically used for mild ptosis with good levator function . It involves resection of a small amount of the tarsal plate and Müller’s muscle but is not commonly used for congenital ptosis.
• Option C . Levator Resection : Levator resection is preferred in cases of congenital ptosis with moderate levator function . The levator muscle is shortened, which helps to elevate the eyelid.
• Option D. Levator Advancement : Levator advancement is similar to levator resection and may be used for ptosis with good levator function . This procedure involves advancing the levator aponeurosis to effectively shorten and strengthen the muscle, helping to lift the eyelid.
• Educational Objective:
• For congenital ptosis , especially with poor levator function , the Frontalis Sling operation is often the surgery of choice.
explanation.ref: pg 380/ eye lids/ 6 th edition comprehensive ophthalmology ak khurana

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The tears secreted by the lacrimal glands are drained via the lacrimal drainage apparatus into which part of the nose?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Middle meatus:
• It is site of opening of the nasolacrimal duct after Dacryocystorhinostomy. (DCR)
• Educational Objective:
• The opening of the nasolacrimal duct is located in the inferior meatus .
explanation.ref: pg 459/ / Anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 30-year-old patient presents to the ophthalmology clinic with a complaint of a painless, localized swelling on their eyelid that has been present for several weeks. The patient reports that the swelling has gradually increased in size and is not associated with any discomfort or redness. On examination, a firm, non-tender nodule is palpated on the upper eyelid. The patient is diagnosed with a chalazion, a chronic inflammatory granuloma of Meibomian gland. Where are the involved glands located?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Eye lash : The glands associated with the eyelashes are the glands of Zeis and Moll .
• Option C. Conjunctiva : While the Meibomian glands open at the lid margin near the conjunctiva , they are not located in the conjunctiva itself.
• Option D. Iris :  There are no glands in the iris .
• Educational Objective:
• The Meibomian glands are located in the tarsal plates of the eyelids .
explanation.ref: pg 497/ Anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Anita, a 32-year-old female patient presents to the ophthalmology clinic with a complaint of persistent tearing since several months and occasional eye discharge in her left eye. She notices that her left eye frequently waters, especially when she is outdoors or exposed to windy conditions. She has no history of eye trauma or previous eye surgeries. The ophthalmologist suspects a nasolacrimal duct obstruction in the left eye. What is the normal length of the duct involved?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. 5 to 10 mm : This length is of the body of the lacrimal sac .
• Option B. 20 to 25 mm : This is the length of the inferior meatus .
• Option D. 1 to 2 mm: This is the length of the vertical part of the canaliculus .
• Educational Objective:
• The normal length of the nasolacrimal duct , which drains tears from the lacrimal sac into the nasal cavity , is approximately 12 to 18 mm
explanation.ref: pg 459/ Anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 4-month-old infant presents with persistent tearing, recurrent eye infections, and crusting around the left eye since birth. The pediatrician suspects nasolacrimal duct obstruction and suggests Crigler’s massage and prescribes antibiotics. In which direction is the involved duct directed?

explanation.short: Ans. D) Downwards, backwards and laterally
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The NLD runs in a bony canal formed by maxilla and inferior turbinate directed downwards, backwards and laterally .
• Students should read the question carefully. The direction of the NLD is not to be confused with the direction of the canaliculus which is directed medially.
• Educational Objective:
• The nasolacrimal duct is directed downwards, backwards and laterally .
• nasolacrimal duct is directed downwards, backwards and laterally
explanation.ref: pg 459/ / Anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The most common cause of congenital lacrimal sac obstruction is due to imperforate Valve of Hasner. Where is this valve located?
options: At the junction of lacrimal sac and NLD
In the middle part of NLD
At the opening of NLD in the inferior meatus
Between the common canaliculus and lacrimal sac

explanation.short: Ans. C) At the opening of NLD in the inferior meatus
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option D - Between Common Canaliculus and Lacrimal Sac : This is the location of the Valve of Rosenmuller.
• Educational Objective:
• The valve of Hasner is located at the opening of NLD in inferior meatus.
explanation.ref: pg 460 / Anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Lacrimal glands are derived from which embryonic layer?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Mesoderm : The mesoderm forms structures such as muscles, bones, connective tissues, and parts of the cardiovascular and lymphatic systems . It is not involved in the development of the lacrimal glands.
• Option C. Neuroectoderm: The neuroectoderm primarily gives rise to the central and peripheral nervous systems, including the brain and spinal cord. It is not responsible for the development of the lacrimal glands.
• Option D. Neuroectoderm & mesoderm: This combination is incorrect for the development of the lacrimal glands . While both neuroectoderm and mesoderm are crucial for various aspects of embryonic development, they do not contribute to the formation of the lacrimal glands.
• Educational Objective:
• The lacrimal glands are derived from the surface ectoderm
explanation.ref: pg 12/ COMPREHENSIVE ophthalmology/ 6 th edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Identify the gland marked with a red arrow:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/03/100.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Gland of Moll: These are apocrine sweat glands found in the eyelids . They are different from the tarsal (Meibomian) glands and are associated with the hair follicles of the eyelashes .
• Option C. Gland of Zeis : These are small sebaceous glands associated with the eyelashes and are not the same as the tarsal glands . They secrete a substance that lubricates the eyelashes.
• Option D. Gland of Krausse: These are minor glands in the eyelid.
• Educational Objective:
• The tarsal glands are the same as the Meibomian glands , which are sebaceous glands within the eyelids contributing to the tear film.
explanation.ref: pg 497/ Anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The glands of Zeis open into?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Conjunctiva : The glands of Zeis do not open into the conjunctiva. The conjunctiva is a mucous membrane.
• Option B. Lacrimal sac : The lacrimal sac is part of the tear drainage system and is not connected to the glands of Zeis . It collects tears from the eyes and drains them into the nasolacrimal duct.
• Option D . Lid margin: Meibomian glands open at the lid margin.
• Educational Objective:
• The glands of Zeis open into the eyelash follicles (Option C). They are sebaceous glands that secrete an oily substance to lubricate the eyelashes.
explanation.ref: pg 499/ Anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The lacrimal gland is present in which quadrant of the orbit?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Inferolateral: The inferolateral quadrant contains other structures like the orbital floor and muscles.
• Option C. Superonasal : This area includes structures such as the trochlea of the superior oblique muscle .
• Option D. Inferonasal :  It includes other anatomical structures like the nasolacrimal duct .
• Educational Objective:
• The lacrimal gland is located in the superolateral quadrant of the orbit
explanation.ref: pg 386/ LACRIMAL APPARATUS/ Comprehensive ophthalmology/ AK KHURANA/ 6 th edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The order of different layers in the tear film from anterior to posterior are: L-lipid, M-mucous, A- aqueous

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• The layers of the tear film are Lipid-Aqueous-Mucus .
explanation.ref: Pg 461/ Anatomy and physiology of eye/ AK KHURANA/ 3 rd edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Identify the test:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/05/10/c15.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Schirmer test : Measures total tear secretion . A value <10mm is suggestive of dry eye .
• Option B. Tear film break up time: Indicator of adequacy of mucin component of tear film . Less than 10 seconds indicates unstable tear film . The image below shows multiple dry spots in a fluorescein-stained tear film.
• Educational Objective:
• Jones test is the test to assess the patency of the lacrimal pathway using fluorescein dye.
explanation.ref: Patel J, Levin A, Patel BC. Epiphora Clinical Testing. [Updated 2023 Jul 25]. In: Stat Pearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): Stat Pearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK557424/

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
A 55-year-old male, presented to the OPD with grade 3 ectropion more pronounced on the lateral half of the left lower lid. Identify the surgical procedure of choice:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/05/12/64.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Lateral Tarsal Stripping : This procedure is used to correct lower eyelid laxity and malposition . It involves the surgical tightening of the lateral lower eyelid by excising a strip of the lateral tarsal conjunctiva and lower eyelid retractors . It helps to reposition and stabilize the lower eyelid, often used in cases of entropion or ectropion.
• Option C. Wies Procedure : The Wies procedure is a surgical technique used to correct minor-to-moderate entropion , particularly when it involves the lower eyelid . It involves a horizontal tightening of the eyelid margin without shortening the horizontal length of the eyelid . A small section of the outer eyelid is removed, and then the eyelid is sutured to tighten and reposition it, which helps in turning the eyelid margin outward, correcting the entropion.
• Option D. Z-Plasty : Z-plasty is a surgical technique used to revise scars , but it's also employed in eyelid surgeries for conditions like ectropion . It involves creating two triangular flaps of equal dimension that are transposed to reorient and lengthen the scar or contracted tissue . In eyelid surgery, this technique can be used to redistribute tissue, correct cicatricial ectropion, or address eyelid deformities that result from scarring.
• Educational Objective:
• In patients with Grade 3 Ectropion with lateral lid laxity, Kuhnt-Szymanowski procedure is the treatment of choice.
explanation.ref: pg 376/ eye lids/6 th edition/ comprehensive ophthalmology ak khurana

subject: Ophthalmology
question: What surgical procedure involves the plication of lower lid retractors to correct entropion?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Weis Procedure : The Weiss procedure , is designed to correct entropion , specifically of the lower eyelid . It involves a horizontal tightening of the eyelid margin , usually without shortening the horizontal length of the eyelid . A small section of the outer eyelid is removed and then sutured to reposition and tighten the eyelid, thereby correcting the inward turning of the eyelid.
• Option C. Quickert’s Procedure : Quickert’s procedure is a surgical technique specifically designed to correct involutional entropion .
• Quickert sutures tighten lower lid retractors . Accordingly, the safety, simplicity, quickness, and overall patient satisfaction have been cited as reasons to consider the Quickert procedure for patients in whom these features would be valuable, or patients with higher operative risk
• Option D. Modified Wheeler Operation : The Modified Wheeler Operation is another surgical technique for correcting lower eyelid entropion . It involves resecting a part of the tarsal plate along with the adjacent conjunctiva and then suturing the edges . This technique is often used when there's a need to shorten the horizontal length of the eyelid due to laxity.
• Educational Objective:
• Jones procedure is plication of lower lid retractors to correct entropion .
explanation.ref: 373/eye lids/6 th edition/ comprehensive ophthalmology ak khurana

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 27-year-old male patient came with complaints of a painful swelling in the region of left lacrimal sac associated with epiphora. He also gives history of fever and malaise. On examination, it is noted that the swelling is red, hot, firm and tender. The eyelids and cheek are also red and oedematous. On asking, he gives a history of having a dental abscess few days back. The ophthalmologist makes a diagnosis of Acute dacryocystitis in the stage of cellulitis. What treatment should be provided to this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Dacryocystorhinostomy: Dacryocystorhinostomy is a surgical procedure used to create a new tear drainage pathway between the lacrimal sac and the nasal cavity . This is typically indicated in chronic dacryocystitis or after the acute infection has been resolved. It is not the first-line treatment for acute dacryocystitis in the cellulitis stage.
• Option B. Abscess Drainage: Abscess drainage is performed if there is a formation of an abscess , which is a collection of pus . In acute dacryocystitis, abscess formation can occur, but drainage is indicated only if an abscess has actually formed.
• Option D. Dacryocystectomy: Dacryocystectomy, the removal of the lacrimal sac , is rarely performed and typically reserved for cases where other treatments have failed or are not appropriate. It is not a standard treatment for acute dacryocystitis.
• Educational Objective:
• Given the stage of cellulitis in acute dacryocystitis , the most appropriate treatment is the administration of systemic Antibiotics . This approach is aimed at controlling the infection and preventing further complications . Once the acute infection is resolved, further evaluation and possibly surgical intervention (like dacryocystorhinostomy) might be considered, especially if there are underlying issues like nasolacrimal duct obstruction.
explanation.ref: pg 395/lacrimal apparatus/6 th edition comprehensive ophthalmology ak khurana

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Name the condition:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/05/12/65.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Educational Objective:
• Ankyloblepharon is an adhesion between upper & lower lid margin .
explanation.ref: pg 62/eyelids/kanski’s clinical ophthalmology 8 th edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A patient complains of a painful, swollen bump on the eyelid. Upon examination, a tender, red lump is observed on the eyelid. The patient is diagnosed with hordeolum internum. Which gland is involved?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Zeis Gland : The Zeis glands are sebaceous glands associated with the eyelashes . Infection of a Zeis gland leads to an external hordeolum, commonly known as a stye.
• Option C. Moll Glands : Moll glands are sweat glands located near the eyelashes .
• Option D. Conjunctiva : Infection of conjunctiva causes conjunctivitis .
• Educational Objective:
• Hardeolum internum is infection of Meibomian Glands .
explanation.ref: Pg 369/ eyelids/ 6 th edition/ comprehensive ophthalmology ak khurana

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Identify the strip used in the following test:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/05/12/67.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. B) 5*35 mm whatman 41 filter paper
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Schirmer's test is used to determine whether the eye produces enough tears to keep it moist . The test is performed by placing filter paper inside the lower lid of the eye . After 5 minutes , the paper is removed and tested for its moisture content .
• A Schirmer test score >10 mm in 5 minutes is widely accepted as the normal value, whereas a score <5 mm is indicative of tear deficiency.
• Educational Objective:
• B. 5*35 mm whatman 41 filter paper is used for preparing Schirmer strips.
explanation.ref: Pg 389/ lacrimal apparatus/ 6 th edition/ comprehensive ophthalmology ak khurana

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Which of the following is not a component of the given clinical syndrome?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/05/12/71.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Ptosis: Ptosis, or drooping of the upper eyelid , is a key component of blepharophimosis syndrome . It's often present from birth and can be significant, sometimes impairing vision.
• Option C. Epicanthus Inversus: Epicanthus inversus is a characteristic feature of blepharophimosis syndrome . It involves a fold of skin that arises from the lower eyelid and covers the inner corner (canthus) of the eye.
• Option D. Blepharophimosis: Blepharophimosis, the reduction in the horizontal width of the eyelids , is a defining feature of this syndrome. It results in a narrow palpebral fissure (eyelid opening).
• Educational Objective:
• The given clinical picture shows blepharophimosis, epicanthus inversus and ptosis, indicating a diagnosis of Blepharophimosis syndrome . Exotropia is not a part of this syndrome.
explanation.ref: pg 59/ eyelids/ 6 th edition/ comprehensive ophthalmology ak khurana

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Identify the following lid tumour:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/05/12/72.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Malignant Melanoma : Appears as a rapidly growing pigmented lesion that can have variable colors , with an irregular border and may arise de novo or from a pre-existing mole.
• Option C . Squamous Cell Carcinoma : Typically manifests as a scaly, erythematous, and sometimes ulcerated lesion that can be mistaken for a benign lesion but has potential for local invasion and metastasis.
• Option D. Keratoacanthoma : Usually emerges as a dome-shaped, flesh-colored nodule with a central keratin-filled crater and may spontaneously regress over time.
• Educational Objective:
• The given clinical image shows a pearly nodule with telangiectatic vessels , on the lower eyelid suggestive of Basal Cell Carcinoma.
explanation.ref: pg 23/eyelids/kanski’s clinical ophthalmology 8 th edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
What is the immediate treatment at this stage of dacryocystitis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/05/12/73.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• The given clinical picture is suggestive of acute dacryocystitis with cellulitis and abscess formation . Thus, patient needs to be treated with systemic antibiotics and drainage of abscess.

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Which organism causes Blepharitis acarica?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Staphylococcus epidermidis : Staphylococcus epidermidis is a part of the normal skin flora and is also found in the mucous membranes . It can be a cause of blepharitis , typically contributing to bacterial blepharitis , but is not the organism associated with Blepharitis acarica.
• Option B: Ascaris : Ascaris refers to a genus of parasitic roundworms . Ascaris lumbricoides is the species responsible for ascariasis in humans , affecting the intestines . It is not involved in eye infections or conditions like blepharitis.
• Option D: Propionibacterium : Propionibacterium, known as Cutibacterium, includes species such as Propionibacterium acnes , which is part of the normal skin flora and has been implicated in acne development. While it can be associated with eyelid margin inflammation, it is not the causative agent of Blepharitis acarica.
• Educational Objective
• Demodex folliculorum is the organism that causes Blepharitis acarica , a condition characterized by infestation of the eyelash follicles leading to blepharitis.
explanation.ref: Singh A, Acharya M, Mathur U. Commentary: Demodex - Microscopic residents of the ocular biome. Indian J Ophthalmol. 2021 Sep;69(9):2394-2395. doi: 10.4103/ijo.IJO_1137_21. PMID: 34427229; PMCID: PMC8544099.

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 45-year-old female came with the complaints of sandy or gritty sensation and itching in the eyes with worsening of symptoms in the evening. After examining and carrying out tests on the patient, a diagnosis of dry eye disease is made. For management, the graded approach of dry disease is planned. According to level 2 of this approach, all of the following are included, except?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Secretagogues : Secretagogues are medications that stimulate the production of tears . These are often considered at higher levels of dry eye treatment when conservative measures are insufficient.
• Option B . Moisture Chamber Spectacles : Moisture chamber spectacles help to maintain humidity and reduce tear evaporation , and they are often recommended in Level 2 management for providing symptomatic relief in moderate dry eye.
• Option D. Nonpreserved Tear Substitutes : Nonpreserved tear substitutes are often recommended as a first-line treatment in the initial levels of DEWS guidelines due to their lower potential for causing irritation, especially in patients who require frequent instillation of artificial tears.
• Educational Objective:
• Permanent Punctal Occlusion is included in Level 3 of DEWS II Guidelines.
explanation.ref: KANSKI 9 TH EDITION PAGE 162

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Which of the following drugs is recently approved for treatment of dry eye disease?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Natalizumab : Natalizumab is a monoclonal anti-integrin antibody used to treat Crohn's disease or multiple sclerosis.
• Option C. Abciximab : Abciximab is a monoclonal anti-glycoprotein IIb/IIIa receptor antibody used to prevent thrombosis during percutaneous coronary intervention.
• Option D. Cilengitide : an RGD pentapeptide ανβ3 and ανβ5 integrin inhibitor in development for glioblastoma and other malignancies.
• Educational Objective:
• Lifitegrast is used for the treatment of dry eye .
• 2. Haber SL, Benson V, Buckway CJ, Gonzales JM, Romanet D, Scholes B. Lifitegrast: a novel drug for patients with dry eye disease. Ther Adv Ophthalmol. 2019 Aug 22;11:2515841419870366. doi: 10.1177/2515841419870366. PMID: 31489402; PMCID: PMC6710705.
• 3. Valgimigli M, Campo G, Tebaldi M, Carletti R, Arcozzi C, Ferrari R, Percoco G. Abciximab: a reappraisal of its use in coronary care. Biologics. 2008 Mar;2(1):29-39. doi: 10.2147/btt.s1374. PMID: 19707425; PMCID: PMC2727773.
explanation.ref: AAO SECTION: 2 – 2020 EDITION PAGE 415

subject: Ophthalmology
question: 2-year-old child is diagnosed with a high refractive error of -9 D. Which of the following form of squint is likely to develop in this child?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . A divergent (outward) misalignment: Exotropia.
• Option C . An upward misalignment: Hypertropia.
• Option D . A downward misalignment: Hypotropia.
• Refractive errors in children under the age of 5 years have a tendency to develop esotropia.
explanation.ref: Comprehensive Ophthalmology Khurana Page 33

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
This fundus appearance is seen in which form of refractive error?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/04/29/4.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Hypermetropia : Optic Disc: The optic nerve head may appear smaller or may have a crowded appearance due to the smaller eye size typically associated with hypermetropia . This can sometimes be mistaken for papilledema , which is swelling of the optic disc due to increased intracranial pressure .
• Option C. Astigmatism : There is no characteristic fundus findings in eyes with astigmatism.
• Option D. Presbyopia : There are no characteristic findings in eyes with presbyopia.
• Educational Objective:
• A tessellated fundus is most commonly seen in myopia, as the retinal thinning associated with this condition makes the choroidal vessels more apparent , giving the retina its characteristic mosaic-like appearance .
explanation.ref: Comprehensive Ophthalmology Khurana Page 35

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 35-year-old female patient presents with blurred and distorted vision. After a comprehensive eye examination, she is diagnosed with irregular astigmatism. What is the treatment of choice for her?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Spectacles - Glasses can correct regular astigmatism , but they are usually not effective for irregular astigmatism because they cannot compensate for the uneven curvature of the cornea .
• Option C . INTACS - These are corneal implants designed to flatten the cornea and are sometimes used to treat irregular astigmatism , particularly in cases related to keratoconus , a condition that leads to a cone-shaped distortion of the cornea .
• Option D . Phakic Intraocular lens - This is an artificial lens implanted inside the eye without removing the natural lens. It is generally used in patients with high degrees of myopia or hyperopia and is less commonly used for astigmatism, especially irregular astigmatism.
• Educational Objective:
• The treatment of choice for a patient presenting with irregular astigmatism is the use of rigid gas permeable contact lenses or scleral lenses , as they provide a uniform refractive surface that corrects the irregular curvature of the cornea .
explanation.ref: Comprehensive Ophthalmology Khurana Page 593

subject: Ophthalmology
question: An optician prescribes a power of -3.00DS / -0.50DC x 180°. The type of astigmatism noted is?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Spectacles for astigmatism have 3 components: Spherical lens: Concave (-) or Convex (+) power expressed as Dioptre Sphere (DS) . Cylindrical lens: Concave (-) or Convex (+) power expressed as Dioptre Cylinder (DC) . Cylindrical axis . In the given question, the prescription is -3.00DS / -0.50DC x 180° . Concave spherical lens of -3D power indicates the rays from this meridian focus in front of the retina . Cylindrical lens of -0.5D power indicates the rays from this meridian also focus in front of the retina . Axis of the cylinder is at 180°. As mentioned in the table above, if rays from both the meridian focus in front of the retina, it is a case of Compound myopic astigmatism .
• Option A. Simple myopic astigmatism - This occurs when one principal meridian is emmetropic (normal), and the other is myopic . Since there is a myopic correction in both meridians in this prescription, it is not simple myopic astigmatism.
• Option C. Mixed astigmatism - This occurs when one principal meridian is myopic , and the other is hypermetropic ( farsighted ). The given prescription does not have a hypermetropic component, so it is not mixed astigmatism.
• Option D. Compound hypermetropic astigmatism with presbyopia - This would involve hypermetropic corrections in both meridians , with different powers , and an additional correction for presbyopia , which is not indicated in this prescription.
• Educational Objective:
• The prescription of -3.00DS / -0.50DC x 180° is indicative of compound myopic astigmatism , where both principal meridians are myopic with different dioptre strengths .
explanation.ref: Comprehensive Ophthalmology Khurana 37

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
This test is based on?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/07/29/optic-10.jpeg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Interference - This is a phenomenon where waves overlap, and the resulting wave amplitude is either increased (constructive interference) or decreased (destructive interference). Polarised glasses are used in tests evaluating stereopsis .
• Option C. Prismatic effect - This occurs when light is bent as it passes through a prism . Prisms are used for evaluation and management of squint .
• Option D. Diffraction - This involves the bending of light waves around obstacles and the spreading of light from a coherent source . Edges of IOLS are designed to reduce diffraction .
• Educational Objective:
• The duo chrome test is based on the principle of chromatic aberration , which is utilized to fine-tune the refractive power needed for vision correction by comparing the focus of red and green light on the retina .
explanation.ref: Comprehensive Ophthalmology Khurana Page 588

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A child was referred from school screening camp due to difficulty in seeing blackboard. The teacher mentioned that he was squinting (half-shutting) his eyes while seeing the blackboard. What do you think is the probable cause for this?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The symptoms of myopia include –
• Defective distant vision hence it is also known as near-sightedness. Asthenopia symptoms Squinting ( half-shutting ) of eyes – this is done to achieve greater clarity of stenopaeic vision .
explanation.ref: Comprehensive Ophthalmology Khurana Page 33

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 12-year-old female student complains of experiencing blurred vision and difficulty seeing objects in the distance. On examination, her eyes appeared normal with vision of 6/6 in both eyes. What is the likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Index myopia refers to myopia caused by changes in the refractive index of the lens or other ocular media, often related to metabolic changes , and would not usually resolve to show a normal vision of 6/6 during an eye examination.
• Option C . Simple myopia, or true myopia, is a permanent change in the eye where the eyeball is too long relative to the focusing power of the cornea and lens . This would usually result in a permanent decrease in distance vision , not intermittent symptoms.
• Option D . Induced myopia can be caused by various factors, including medications , increased blood sugar levels , or exposure to various environmental factors . Like index myopia, it would not typically present with normal vision during an examination.
• Educational Objective:
• The likely diagnosis for a patient experiencing blurred distance vision intermittently but showing normal vision on examination is pseudo myopia , which is often due to a transient spasm of the ciliary muscles after prolonged close work .
explanation.ref: Parson’s diseases of eye 22 nd edition Page 71

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 65-year-old male patient presents to the ophthalmology clinic with a complaint of gradually worsening vision over the past year. The ophthalmologist plans to assess the patient's refractive state objectively to determine the appropriate course of action. Which test would he carry out for the same?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Ophthalmoscopy : Clinical exam of Retina. It is of 3 types: Distant direct , Direct and Indirect .
• Option C . Gonioscopy : Examination of angle of anterior chamber .
• Option D . Campimetry : also known as Scotometry , is done to evaluate the central and paracentral area of visual field .
• Educational Objective:
• Skiascopy (Retinoscopy) is the appropriate test to objectively assess a patient's refractive error to determine the necessary correction for conditions like myopia, hyperopia, and astigmatism .
explanation.ref: Comprehensive Ophthalmology Khurana Page 580

subject: Ophthalmology
question: During an eye examination, a 40-year-old patient presented with complaints of blurred vision and difficulty reading. While performing keratometry, it was observed that the horizontal meridian exhibited a higher degree of curvature compared to the vertical meridian. Based on these findings, the likely diagnosis is?

explanation.short: Ans. B) Against the Rule Astigmatism
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. With the rule astigmatism - With the rule astigmatism is characterized by a greater curvature of the cornea in the vertical meridian than in the horizontal meridian
• Option C . Oblique astigmatism - In this type, the steepest curve lies in an oblique axis , neither horizontal nor vertical.
• Option D . Bioblique astigmatism - This occurs when both principal meridians are oblique and at different angles that are neither vertical nor horizontal .
• Educational Objective:
• Against the rule astigmatism is when the horizontal meridian is more curved than the vertical meridian. This type of astigmatism is commonly corrected with glasses or contact lenses that have the appropriate cylindrical correction.
explanation.ref: Comprehensive Ophthalmology Khurana Page 36

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A patient had a refractive error of -5D in the right eye and -7.5D in left eye. The difference in size of retinal image would be.

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The above condition is suggestive of ANISOMETROPIA – unequal refraction in both eyes . 1D difference between two eyes produces 2% difference in retinal image . Difference of more than 2.5D causes more than 5% difference in retinal images of the two eyes. Up to 5% can be tolerated. Here, since difference is 2.5D , the retinal images will differ by 5%.
• By taking into account the magnification factor for lenses, which for myopic correction is approximately given by the formula:
• Magnification=11−(0.014×Lens Power in Diopters)
• Magnification=1−(0.014×Lens Power in Diopters)1​
• For both eyes, we will calculate the magnification factor and then determine the percentage difference in image size based on these magnification factors. Let's perform this corrected calculation. ​​
• The corrected calculation shows that the difference in size of the retinal image between the two eyes is approximately 3.9% , which would be closest to option D, 5%.
• Students do not need to learn this calculation. The approximations mentioned in the table are clinically relevant and need to be remembered.
• Educational Objective:
• For a refractive error of -5D in the right eye and -7.5D in the left eye, the approximate difference in retinal image size is 5%.
explanation.ref: Comprehensive Ophthalmology Khurana Page 38

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
The site of placement of this segment is?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/04/29/9.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Descemet’s Membrane - This is a thin but strong film of tissue that serves as the protective barrier between the corneal stroma and the endothelial layer of the cornea . It is not suitable for the placement of INTACS as it is too deep and its integrity is crucial for the health of the endothelial cells that are responsible for keeping the cornea dehydrated and transparent.
• Option C. Intraepithelial - This refers to within the epithelial layer , which is the outermost layer of the cornea . It provides a barrier to protect the cornea against foreign particles and absorbs oxygen and nutrients from tears . INTACS are not placed here because the epithelial layer is too thin and constantly regenerates, which would not provide a stable position for the rings.
• Option D. Subepithelial - This refers to beneath the epithelial layer but above the stroma . This location is also not suitable for the placement of INTACS as it could interfere with the regenerative capability of the epithelium and is not deep enough to allow for the desired refractive changes.
• Educational Objective:
• INTACS rings are placed intrastromally within the corneal stroma to utilize the thickness and structural integrity of the stroma for reshaping the cornea to correct refractive errors .
explanation.ref: Comprehensive Ophthalmology Khurana Page 48

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Most common form of Myopia is?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Index myopia - These results from changes in the refractive index of the eye's media , usually the lens , which can occur with conditions such as nuclear cataract or in cases of blood sugar fluctuations (as in diabetes ).
• Option B. Positional myopia - This is a less common term that might refer to myopia caused by the lens being positioned too close to the cornea, but it is not a widely recognized classification.
• Option C. Curvatural myopia - This form of myopia occurs due to an increase in the curvature of the cornea or lens , which causes light rays to be over-converged and focus in front of the retina .
• Educational Objective:
• Axial myopia is the most common form of myopia and is caused by the elongation of the axial length of the eye, leading to distant objects being focused in front of the retina and appearing blurred .
explanation.ref: Comprehensive Ophthalmology Khurana Page 33

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 65-year-old patient who previously underwent cataract surgery resulting in aphakia is prescribed spectacles for visual correction. She has been using the spectacles regularly, and now complains of experiencing a specific type of visual impairment or blind spot. What type of scotoma do you think she may have developed?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Siedel scotoma - This is associated with a glaucomatous defect near the blind spot and is not related to the correction of aphakia with spectacles .
• Option B. Scintillating scotoma - This is a type of visual aura commonly associated with migraine , which appears as a flickering , jagged arc of light .
• Option C. Bjerrum scotoma - This is a visual field defect associated with glaucoma , typically a wedge-shaped scotoma extending from the blind spot horizontally .
• Educational Objective:
• In aphakic patients corrected with spectacles , a roving ring scotoma can develop due to peripheral visual field distortions caused by the prismatic effect of the strong plus lens required to correct the absence of the crystalline lens .
explanation.ref: Comprehensive Ophthalmology Khurana Page 32

subject: Ophthalmology
question: In LASEK, which of the following substance is used for rising flap of epithelium?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Povidone-iodine: Antiseptic agent
• Option B. Carboxymethyl cellulose: Artificial tears
• Option D. Sodium chloride: Hypertonic solution which helps in reducing edema .
• Educational Objective:
• In LASEK, diluted alcohol is utilized to facilitate the gentle lifting of the epithelial flap , a critical step differentiating it from other refractive procedures.
explanation.ref: Parson Diseases of eye 22 nd edition page 83

subject: Ophthalmology
question: What is the total dioptric power in a Donders’ reduced eye model?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. +58.64D : This value is close to the actual combined dioptric power of the human eye , but it's slightly less than the standard value used for the reduced eye model.
• Option C. +43D : This value is the power of the cornea in the reduced eye model .
• Option D . +53D : This is also lower than the typical total dioptric power of the human eye .
• Educational Objective:
• The total dioptric power of the reduced eye , a simplified model of the human eye, is conventionally set at +60 diopters , encompassing the combined refractive powers of the cornea and the lens .
explanation.ref: Comprehensive Ophthalmology Khurana Page 26

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A Myopic patient was posted for Radial Keratotomy. What is the size of the optical zone left while making Peripheral Corneal Incisions?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Radial Keratotomy is one of the Surgical Procedures done for correction of Myopia . It is a type of Refractive Keratoplasty which is done to Modify the Refractive Power of the Cornea . In this, Multiple Radial Incisions are made in the periphery of the cornea leaving a Central 4mm Optical Zone . These incisions on Healing cause Flattening of the Corneal Curvature .
• Central Optional Zone in Radial Keratotomy is 4mm.
explanation.ref: Comprehensive Ophthalmology Khurana Page 592

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A cataract surgery with a premium multifocal intraocular lens implantation is being planned. To assess the suitability of the patient to the MFIOL, the surgeon is evaluating the angle alpha of the patient. Angle alpha is between:

explanation.short: Ans. A) Optical Axis and Visual Axis
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Fixation Axis and Optical Axis : The Fixation Axis (also known as the Line of Sight ) is the line connecting the fovea with the object of regard (the point one is looking directly at ).
• Option C . Visual Axis and Fixation Axis : As described, the visual axis connects the fovea with the point of focus on the object , while the fixation axis is essentially the same, connecting the fovea to the object of regard .
• Option D . Optical Axis and Pupillary Line : The Pupillary Line is a line drawn perpendicularly from the center of the pupil to the object of regard .
• Educational Objective:
• Angle Alpha is the angle between the optical axis and the visual axis of the eye, which is a key consideration in the alignment of corrective lenses for optimal vision correction .
explanation.ref: Comprehensive Ophthalmology Khurana Page 27

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A patient is being evaluated for squint. Which of the following angles can be measured?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Alpha : The angle alpha is the angle between the optical axis and the visual axis of the eye. It's a conceptual angle more than a measurable one in clinical practice .
• Option B . Beta : This angle is not commonly referenced in standard ophthalmological practice for measurement.
• Option C . Gamma : Like beta, the gamma angle is also not a standard measurable angle in the context of clinical ophthalmology.
• Educational Objective:
• In ophthalmology, the angle Kappa, which is the angle between the visual axis and the pupillary axis, is a measurable parameter that holds clinical significance , especially in refractive surgery and binocular vision assessments .
explanation.ref: Comprehensive Ophthalmology Khurana Page 27

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Functional Hypermetropia occurs in which of the nerve palsy?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Trochlear Nerve (Cranial Nerve IV) : This nerve innervates the superior oblique muscle , which primarily contributes to the rotation of the eye. Trochlear nerve palsy affects eye movement but does not directly impact accommodation or refractive status like hypermetropia.
• Option C . Abducens Nerve (Cranial Nerve VI) : The abducens nerve controls the lateral rectus muscle , which is responsible for abduction . Palsy of this nerve leads to an inability to move the eye laterally , but it does not affect accommodation or cause hypermetropia.
• Option D. Optic Nerve (Cranial Nerve II) : The optic nerve transmits visual information from the retina to the brain . Palsy or damage to the optic nerve affects vision, but it does not cause hypermetropia as it is not involved in the accommodation process.
• Educational Objective:
• Functional hypermetropia can occur as a result of oculomotor nerve ( Cranial Nerve III ) palsy , which impairs the ciliary muscle's ability to accommodate , leading to difficulty focusing on near objects .
explanation.ref: Comprehensive Ophthalmology Khurana Page 29

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 70-year-old patient presented with complaints of defective vision in the right eye associated with erythropsia and cyanopsia. On examination it was seen that his pupil was jet black and the Purkinje image test revealed only two images. What is likely to be the conclusion after performing retinoscopy?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Low Hypermetropia : Hypermetropia ( farsightedness ) occurs when the eye is too short or the lens is too weak , causing light to focus behind the retina . However, the absence of the lens in aphakia leads to a much more significant refractive error than what would be classified as "low" hypermetropia .
• Option B . Low Myopia : Myopia (near-sightedness) is when the eye is too long or the lens is too strong , causing light to focus in front of the retina . Aphakia does not typically result in myopia, as the removal of the lens significantly reduces the eye's refractive power.
• Option D. High Myopia : As mentioned, aphakia generally does not lead to myopia. The condition results in a loss of refractive power , not an excess of it, which would be necessary to cause high myopia.
• Educational Objective:
• In aphakic patients, where the natural lens of the eye is absent , retinoscopy typically reveals high hypermetropia due to the significant reduction in the eye's refractive power .
explanation.ref: Comprehensive Ophthalmology Khurana Page 31

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A patient with high myopia asks for thinner glasses. What is the ideal refractive index of the glass material that can be used?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. 1.52 : This is a standard refractive index for regular plastic lenses . While they are lighter than glass, lenses made with this refractive index for high myopia would still be relatively thick .
• Option C. 2.12 : Materials with such high refractive indices are generally not used in standard eyewear due to cost, weight , and manufacturing challenges .
• Option D . 2.57 : Materials with such high indices are not common place in the optical industry .
• Educational Objective:
• For patients with high myopia seeking thinner glasses , lenses made of high-index materials , such as those with a refractive index of 1.67 , are ideal . They offer a thinner and lighter solution compared to standard lens materials .
explanation.ref: Parson Disease of eye 22 nd edition Page 81

subject: Ophthalmology
question: An IT personnel in their mid-40s diagnosed with hypermetropia and presbyopia needs a new set of spectacles. What type of glasses can be prescribed to this individual?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Unifocal : Unifocal lenses have only one prescription throughout the entire lens . They can be optimized for either distance or near vision , but not both. This would not be suitable for someone who needs correction for both hypermetropia and presbyopia.
• Option B . Bifocal with ARC : Bifocal lenses have two distinct optical powers – one for distance and one for near vision , typically with a visible line separating them. ARC stands for Anti-Reflective Coating , which reduces glare and is beneficial for computer work . Bifocals could be a viable option , providing both near and far corrections.
• Option C. Flat Top Trifocal : Trifocal lenses provide three ranges of vision : near , intermediate (like computer screens), and distance . A flat-top trifocal has a segment at the top for distance , a middle segment for intermediate vision , and a lower segment for near vision . This could be a good choice for an IT professional who requires clear vision at multiple distances .
• Educational Objective:
• For a person in their mid-40s with hypermetropia and presbyopia , especially someone working in IT , progressive lenses or flat top trifocals are recommended to accommodate near , intermediate , and distance vision requirements seamlessly .
explanation.ref: Parson Disease of eye 22 nd edition Page 81

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Identify the type of lens in this picture:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/04/29/13.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. B) Rigid Gas Permeable Lens
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Soft Contact Lens : Made of soft, flexible plastics that allow oxygen to pass through to the cornea, soft contact lenses are comfortable and easier to adapt to than rigid lenses .
• Option C. Hard Contact Lens : Made of a rigid, durable plastic , hard contact lenses are less comfortable and less oxygen-permeable compared to soft or rigid gas permeable lenses and are rarely used today .
• Option D. Scleral Contact Lens : Large-diameter lenses that vault over the entire corneal surface and rest on the sclera , scleral lenses are used for specific corneal irregularities and provide comfort for sensitive eyes .
• Educational Objective:
• Rigid Gas Permeable Lens : are more durable than soft contact lenses , provide clearer vision , and are breathable , allowing more oxygen to reach the cornea .
explanation.ref: Parson Disease of eye 22 nd edition Page 82

subject: Ophthalmology
question: All are true regarding LASIK except?
options: Done in Myopes > 21 years of age

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . This procedure uses excimer laser : True. LASIK surgery uses an excimer laser to reshape the cornea, which corrects refractive errors like myopia, hyperopia, and astigmatism.
• Option B . Done in myopes > 21 years of age : True. LASIK is typically performed on patients over the age of 21 when the refractive error is more likely to have stabilized.
• Option C . Needs a stable refractive error : True. A stable refractive error for at least a year is one of the prerequisites for LASIK, to ensure the long-term effectiveness of the surgery.
• Educational Objective
• LASIK should not be performed on patients with keratoconus .
explanation.ref: Parson Disease of eye 22 nd edition Page 74

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Conductive Keratoplasty is a treatment option for refractive error between:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . +2.5D to +4D : This range represents moderate hyperopia . CK can also be effective in this range, though its effectiveness and predictability tend to decrease as the degree of hyperopia increases.
• Option C . +4D to +10D : This range is more indicative of high hyperopia . CK is generally not recommended for refractive errors in this range due to decreased effectiveness and increased risk of complications.
• Option D . +10D to +12.5D : This range represents very high hyperopia , where CK is not typically used. Such high levels of hyperopia often require more invasive surgical procedures for correction.
• Educational Objective:
• The most appropriate range for the use of Conductive Keratoplasty for refractive error correction is +1D to +2.5D , which covers mild hyperopia , where the procedure is most effective and commonly indicated .
explanation.ref: Parson Disease of eye 22 nd edition Page 76

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 65-year-old patient with aphakia in the right eye presents for vision correction. The ophthalmologist prescribes spectacles to address the refractive error. During the examination, a specific type of visual distortion or aberration is observed in the right eye. What is the likely aberration noted?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Barrel Distortion : This occurs when the magnification of the image decreases with increasing distance from the optical axis . In optics, it is seen with high minus lenses .
• Option C. Coma : This aberration occurs when an optical system images an off-axis point source , resulting in an asymmetric , comet-like blur . In the human eye, coma can be caused by imperfections in the lens or corneal surface , often exacerbated under conditions of large pupil diameters .
• Option D. Oblique Astigmatism : This type of astigmatism happens when light rays entering the eye off the optical axis have different refractive errors in different meridians. It's not specific to aphakia but is a common issue in spectacle and contact lens corrections , especially when the gaze is directed significantly away from the optical center of the lens .
• Educational Objective:
• In an aphakic patient corrected with spectacles, "Pin Cushion Distortion" is the typical visual aberration observed, where the strong positive lenses cause straight lines to appear curved inwards due to increased magnification away from the lens's optical axis .
explanation.ref: Parson Disease of eye 22 nd edition Page 77

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 68-year-old patient presents to the ophthalmologist with complaints of blurred vision in both eyes. She has a history of cataract surgery in both eyes, resulting in aphakia. She desires improved visual acuity and seeks the most appropriate intervention for her condition. Which one out these would be the best choice for her?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Contact Lens : Contact lenses can provide good visual acuity in aphakic patients, offering a more natural field of vision than spectacles. However, they require daily maintenance and may not be suitable for all patients, especially the elderly, due to handling and hygiene challenges.
• Option C . Spectacles : While spectacles are a non-invasive option , they can create significant visual distortions (like barrel or pin cushion distortion) in aphakic patients. They also offer a limited field of vision and can be cumbersome due to the thick lenses required for aphakic correction.
• Option D . Arcuate Keratotomy : This surgical procedure is used to correct astigmatism , not aphakia. It involves making arc-shaped incisions in the cornea to change its shape and correct refractive errors . It's not suitable for aphakia.
• Educational Objective:
• For patients with aphakia following cataract surgery, the placement of an intraocular lens (IOL) is the best choice for long-term, maintenance-free visual acuity improvement.
explanation.ref: Parson Disease of eye 22 nd edition Page 77

subject: Ophthalmology
question: What is the amount of accommodation exerted by the patient when he holds the book at a distance of 25cm from the eye?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• So, in this case since the object is 25 cm away from the eye , in order to see clearly, 1/0.25 or 4 D power of accommodation must be exerted .
explanation.ref: Parson Disease of eye 22 nd edition Page 58

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Identify the instrument:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/07/29/14.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B : Direct Ophthalmoscope
• Option C . Perkin’s tonometer
• Option D . Focimeter
• Educational Objective:
• The instrument shown is a streal retinoscope .
explanation.ref: Parson Disease of eye 22 nd edition Page 59

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 23-year-old female came to the ophthalmologist with complaints of blurring of vision since the past few months. During evaluation, she is subjected to retinoscopic refraction wherein the streak of light and the shadow moved in opposite directions. Based on this, what is the refractive error she is likely to have?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
