• Option A . Hypermetropia > 1D : Hypermetropia, or farsightedness , is indicated by "with motion" in retinoscopy (where the light streak and shadow move in the same direction ), not "against motion".
• Option B . Myopia < 1D : Produces with movement .
• Option C . Hypermetropia < 1D : Similar to option A, hypermetropia would not present with " with motion " in retinoscopy.
• Educational Objective:
• In retinoscopy, if the streak of light and the shadow move in opposite directions (against motion), it typically indicates myopia , and the speed of the shadow's movement can help determine the degree of myopia . A quick shadow movement against the light streak suggests myopia greater than 1 diopter .
explanation.ref: Parson Disease of eye 22 nd edition Page 59

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A patient who is scheduled for cataract surgery needs to have the curvature of the anterior surface of the cornea tested in order to calculate the intraocular lens (IOL) power. Which of the following instruments should the optometrist choose for the same?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Refractometer: Estimates refractive error of the eye.
• Option B. Priestley- Smith Retinoscope: Estimates refractive error of the eye.
• Option D. Specular Microscope: Measures corneal endothelial cell count and morphology .
• Educational Objective
• Ophthalmometer is the instrument used for measuring the curvature of the cornea .
explanation.ref: Parson Disease of eye 22 nd edition Page 65

subject: Ophthalmology
question: What is the refractive state of the newborn eye?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Emmetropia , is a state where the eye has perfect refractive power , meaning there is no need for corrective lenses. Light rays coming into the eye are focused precisely on the retina , providing clear vision . However, this is not common in newborns, as their eyes are still developing .
• Option B . In Myopia (Short-sightedness) , the eye's refractive power is too strong , or the eyeball is elongated . This causes light rays to focus in front of the retina , making distant objects appear blurred . Newborns are rarely myopic ; this condition usually develops in childhood or adolescence .
• Option D . Anisometropia, is a condition where there is a significant difference in the refractive power between the two eyes . It is not a common refractive state at birth but can develop as the child grows . Anisometropia can lead to amblyopia if not corrected, as the brain tends to favor the eye with better focus.
• Educational Objective
• Understand that the typical refractive state of the newborn eye is hypermetropia , where the eye's refractive power is too weak , leading to light focusing behind the retina . This condition is common and usually self-corrects as the child grows .
explanation.ref: Parson Disease of eye 22 nd edition Page 70

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Provided the normal axial length is 24mm, a patient with axial length of 26mm would have a refractive error of?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. +3 Hypermetropia : For change of +3 D Hypermetropia, eyeball must shorten by 1mm .
• Option B. – 3 Myopia : For change of +3 D Myopia, eyeball must elongate by 1.5mm .
• Option C. +6 Hypermetropia : For change of +6 D Hypermetropia, eyeball must shorten by 2mm .
• Educational Objective
• In the question, since there is an increase in axial length by 2mm , the refractive error is -6D of myopia .
explanation.ref: Parson Disease of eye 22 nd edition Page 71

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
A patient presents to the ophthalmologist with complaints of blurry near vision, eye strain, and headaches while performing close-up tasks such as reading. Upon examination, the ophthalmologist determines that the patient has refractive error. While counselling the patient regarding his refractive status, the ophthalmologist draws the following ray diagram. All of the following ocular changes can cause this condition, except?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/01/15/picture8_9oAcCl6.jpg]
options: Absence of Lens in Pupillary Area

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Shorter Axial Length : This is a primary cause of hypermetropia . When the axial length of the eye (the distance from the front to the back ) is shorter than normal, it causes light rays to focus behind the retina , leading to hypermetropia .
• Option B . Absence of Lens in Pupillary Area (Aphakia) : The absence of the lens typically results in hypermetropia. The lens plays a crucial role in focusing light onto the retina . Without it, the eye loses some of its refractive power, causing light to be focused behind the retina.
• Option D . Backward Displacement of the Lens : If the lens is displaced backward , it can lead to hypermetropia . This displacement increases the focal length within the eye, causing light to focus behind the retina .
• Educational Objective:
• A steeper cornea actually causes myopia , not hypermetropia. In myopia, the cornea bends light too much, or the eye is too long , causing light rays to focus in front of the retina . Therefore, a steeper cornea would not cause hypermetropia, but the opposite.
explanation.ref: Comprehensive ophthalmology Khurana page 29

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Pseudo Papillitis is seen in?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Myopia (Short-sightedness) : In myopia, the eye is longer than normal , which can affect the appearance of the optic nerve head . However, myopia is more commonly associated with other changes in the eye, such as posterior staphyloma , and does not typically present as pseudo papillitis.
• Option C . Aphakia (Absence of the lens) : Aphakia, the absence of the lens, either congenital or usually due to surgical removal , changes the eye's refractive state . However, it does not inherently cause changes in the optic disc appearance that would be mistaken for papilledema.
• Option D . Pseudophakia (Artificial lens implant) : Pseudophakia refers to the condition of having an artificial lens implant , typically after cataract extraction . While this alters the eye's focusing ability , it does not typically result in changes to the optic disc that mimics papilledema.
• Educational Objective
• Understand that pseudo papillitis, a condition where the optic disc appears swollen similar to papilledema but is non-pathological , is typically associated hypermetropia .
explanation.ref: Parson Disease of eye 22 nd edition Page 76

subject: Ophthalmology
question: In a patient tested for refractive error, the greater and least curvature was at right angles to each other, and the vertical meridian was more curved. Identify the type of error:

explanation.short: Ans. A) Regular Astigmatism with the Rule
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Irregular Astigmatism : This occurs when the principal meridians are not perpendicular to each other. This can be due to an eye injury , surgery , or keratoconus .
• Option C. Oblique Astigmatism : This is a type of regular astigmatism where the steepest curve lies in between the 120-degree and 150-degree meridians or between the 30 - degree and 60-degree meridians, neither vertical nor horizontal.
• Option D. Against-the-Rule Astigmatism : The horizontal meridian is more curved than the vertical meridian .
• Educational Objective:
• The greater curvature in the vertical meridian and the meridians being at right angles to each other – the correct type of refractive error is A. Regular Astigmatism with the rule.
explanation.ref: Parson Disease of eye 22 nd edition Page 52

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
In this Sturm’s conoid, if the retina cuts the conoid at A, the condition is termed as?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/07/29/20.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Simple Hypermetropic Astigmatism : One principal meridian of the eye is hypermetropic (farsighted) , and the other is emmetropic (normal) , causing light to focus behind the retina in one meridian only.
• Option C. Simple Myopic Astigmatism : One principal meridian of the eye is myopic (nearsighted) , and the other is emmetropic , causing light to focus in front of the retina in one meridian only.
• Option D . Compound Myopic Astigmatism : Both principal meridians of the eye are myopic , but to different degrees , leading to light focusing in front of the retina in both meridians .
• Educational Objective:
• In compound hyperopic astigmatism, both the principal meridians of the eye are hypermetropic , but to different degrees, resulting in light focusing behind the retina in both meridians .
explanation.ref: Parson Disease of eye 22 nd edition Page 53

subject: Ophthalmology
question: In order to obtain the size of the retinal image similar to that in Emmetropes, where should the optician place the optical centre of the spectacles?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The correcting lenses in axial ametropia have to be placed in the plane of the anterior focus of the eye, because in these conditions the size of the retinal image is the same as if the eye were emmetropic . The anterior focus is about 15 mm in front of the eye. The optician aims at placing the optical centre of the spectacle lens 12–13 mm from the cornea .
• Educational Objective:
• The optical centre of the spectacles should be placed 12-13 mm from the cornea to obtain the retinal size of the retinal image similar to emmetropes .
explanation.ref: Parson Disease of eye 22 nd edition 54

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The natural mechanisms of the eye to counteract the aberrations are all except?
options: Cutting off the peripheral rays of light by iris
Higher refractive index of peripheral cortex compared to nucleus
Greater sensitivity of photoreceptors to perpendicular rays

explanation.short: Ans. B) Higher refractive index of peripheral cortex compared to nucleus
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Cutting Off the Peripheral Rays of Light by Iris : True. The iris acts like an aperture , limiting the amount of light entering the eye and cutting off peripheral rays that can cause aberrations , particularly at the edges of the lens.
• Option C . Reduced Sensitivity of Peripheral Retina : True. The peripheral retina is less sensitive to fine detail compared to the central retina (the macula and especially the fovea ). This reduced sensitivity helps to minimize the impact of aberrations that predominantly affect peripheral vision .
• Option D . Greater Sensitivity of Photoreceptors to Perpendicular Rays : True. Photoreceptors, particularly cones in the central retina , are more sensitive to light rays that are perpendicular to the retina , which helps in reducing the effect of aberrations .
• Educational Objective:
• The natural mechanisms of the eye to counteract aberrations include the iris cutting off peripheral rays of light, the reduced sensitivity of the peripheral retina , and the greater sensitivity of photoreceptors to perpendicular rays . Contrary to the statement given, the nucleus of the lens typically has a higher refractive index than the peripheral cortex , which helps in reducing spherical aberration .
explanation.ref: Parson Disease of eye 22 nd edition 56

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The effects of accommodation on eye are all except?
options: Change in Curvature of Anterior Surface of Lens to 6mm

explanation.short: Ans. C) Backward Displacement of the Lens
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Increase in Lens Thickness : True. During accommodation, the ciliary muscle contracts , releasing tension on the zonular fibers , allowing the lens to become more rounded and thus thicker .
• Option B . Decrease in Lens Equatorial Diameter : True. As the lens becomes more spherical (thicker) during accommodation , its equatorial diameter decreases .
• Option D . Change in Curvature of Anterior Surface of Lens to 6mm : True. Accommodation involves a change in the curvature of the lens, particularly the anterior surface , to increase its refractive power .
• Educational Objective:
• During accommodation, the lens becomes thicker and more rounded , its equatorial diameter decreases , and the curvature of the anterior surface changes to increase its refractive power , but there is no backward displacement of the lens.
explanation.ref: Parson Disease of eye 22 nd edition 56

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Anti-reflective coating in the spectacles uses which of the following properties of light?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Total Internal Reflection : Overcoming total internal reflection is the principle of the gonioscopic lenses .
• Option C. Polarization : Polarised glasses are used for the assessment of stereopsis .
• Option D . Diffraction : The principle of pinhole is based on diffraction .
• Educational Objective
• Anti-reflective coating in the spectacles is based on the phenomenon of interference .
explanation.ref: Clinical optics by Elkington Page: 88

subject: Ophthalmology
question: All are advantages of contact lens over spectacles except?

explanation.short: Ans. B) Better magnification of image
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Better Field of Vision : True. Contact lenses conform to the curvature of the eye, providing a wider field of vision and causing fewer vision distortions compared to spectacles .
• Option C . Elimination of Aberration : True. Spectacles can sometimes introduce optical aberrations , especially towards the edges of the lenses . Contact lenses, being directly on the eye, largely eliminate these aberrations, providing clearer and more consistent vision .
• Option D . Absence of Prismatic Effect : True. The prismatic effect is an issue with spectacles , particularly for those with strong prescriptions or when looking through the edge of the lenses . Contact lenses , fitting directly over the cornea , do not create this effect.
• Educational Objective:
• Contact lenses offer several advantages over spectacles , including a better field of vision , elimination of aberration , and absence of prismatic effects . However, they do not provide better magnification of images, which is a function more related to the design of spectacle lenses .
explanation.ref: Comprehensive Ophthalmology Khurana Page 32

subject: Ophthalmology
question: An elderly woman came to the OPD with complaints of gradually progressive diminution of vision in right eye, not accompanied with pain. After examination she was diagnosed with mature senile cataract in right eye and underwent surgery for the same wherein her cataractous lens was removed. Now, which of the following UV rays can reach the retina in the operated eye?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• In aphakic patients, wavelengths in the UV-A region can reach the retina . Intraocular lens implants, made up of polymethylmethacrylate , absorb only UV rays < 320 nm . Modern intraocular lenses including polymethylmethacrylate, silicone and foldable acrylic are impregnated with UV-A absorbing substances called chromophores . Polymethylmethacrylate lenses with UV filtering chromophores absorb wavelengths < 400 nm .
• Educational Objective:
• In an eye operated for cataract surgery, UV A rays can reach the retina .
explanation.ref: Parson’s diseases of eye 22 nd edition page 40

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A lens with a focal length of 25cm is said to have a power of?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective
• The dioptric power is the reciprocal of the focal length ( D = 1/F in metres ).
explanation.ref: Parson’s diseases of eye 22 nd edition page 47

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
The optical state of the patient being tested is?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/07/29/optic-13.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Myopia : In myopia the rays of light focus in front of the retina .
• Option C . Emmetropia : In emmetropia the rays of light come to focus on the fovea .
• Option D . Anisometropia : In anisometropia, the two eyes have different refraction errors .
• Educational Objective
• In hypermetropia, the rays of light come to focus behind the retina .
explanation.ref: Parson’s diseases of eye 22 nd edition page 51

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
In a 30-year-old patient with astigmatism, you are asked to confirm the axis of the cylinder. How will you orient the given instrument?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/07/29/20-2.jpg]
options: Handle of instrument in alignment with axis of the cylindrical lens
Handle of instrument perpendicular to axis of the cylindrical lens
Axis of the cylinder in the instrument aligned with axis of the trial cylindrical lens
Axis of the cylinder in the instrument perpendicular to axis of the trial cylindrical lens

explanation.short: Ans. A) Handle of instrument in alignment with the axis of the cylindrical lens
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Handle of Instrument in Alignment with Axis of the Cylindrical Lens : This is not the correct method . Aligning the handle of the Jackson cross cylinder with the axis of the patient's cylindrical lens would not effectively test the axis.
• Option B . Handle of Instrument Perpendicular to Axis of the Cylindrical Lens : This method is also incorrect . The handle orientation does not directly determine the axis testing with the Jackson cross cylinder.
• Option D . Axis of the Cylinder in the Instrument Perpendicular to Axis of the Trial Cylindrical Lens : This orientation would not be correct for initially confirming the axis of the cylindrical lens. It is used after aligning the axes to refine the cylindrical power.
• Educational Objective:
• When confirming the axis of a cylindrical lens in astigmatism using a Jackson cross cylinder , align the axis of the cylinder within the instrument with the axis of the patient's current cylindrical correction . This alignment is crucial for accurate axis determination and refinement .
explanation.ref: Parson’s diseases of eye 22 nd edition page 67

subject: Ophthalmology
question: All the following are features of spherical concave lens except?
options: Image moves in opposite direction to lens movement

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Thin in Centre : This is true for spherical concave lenses. They are thinner at the centre and thicker at the edges , which is the opposite of convex lenses .
• Option C . Image Moves in Opposite Direction to Lens Movement : This is true . When you move a concave lens, the image formed by the lens will move in the opposite direction . This property is utilized in various optical instruments .
• Option D . Causes No Distortion of Image : This is not entirely accurate . While concave lenses do not cause the same type of distortion as convex lenses, they can still cause some distortion due to the way they diverge light rays . They can create a "barrel" distortion where the magnification decreases towards the edges .
• Educational Objective:
• Concave lenses , being diverging lenses , tend to produce images that are smaller than the object , causing minification .
explanation.ref: Parson’s diseases of eye 22 nd edition page 47

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
What is the type of lens used in this instrument?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/07/29/24.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The lens shown in the picture is Maddox Rod . It is used in the diagnosis of extra-ocular muscle imbalance . It consists of a series of powerful convex cylindrical lenses mounted side by side in a trial lens . The row of cylinders forms a row of foci on the retina . These foci join up and are seen as a line of light which lies at 90° to the axis of the Maddox rod .
• Educational Objective:
• The Maddox rod is made by placing a series of convex cylinders so that when a point source of light is seen through it, it appears as a line .
explanation.ref: Clinical optics by Elkington Page: 67

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Based on this image, Focal interval is between?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/07/29/25.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The distance between the two foci in Sturm’s conoid is called the focal interval of Sturm . At one place in this focal interval there will be a spot (D) where the vertical rays have diverged from the axis exactly as much as the horizontal rays have converged towards it . Here the cross-section is a circle , which is called the circle of least diffusion.
explanation.ref: Parson’s diseases of the eye 22 nd edition page 52-53

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Prisms are used in all the following instruments except?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Applanation Tonometer : This instrument is used to measure intraocular pressure (IOP) and typically use prisms . It employs a flat-tipped probe to flatten (applanate) a small area of the cornea, and the force required for this applanation is used to estimate the IOP.
• Option C . Indirect Ophthalmoscope : An indirect ophthalmoscope is used for examining the retina . It often incorporates prisms to adjust the path of light for proper visualization of the interior of the eye.
• Option D . Slit Lamp Biomicroscope : A slit lamp is used to examine the various parts of the eye under high magnification . It typically includes prisms to direct light into the eye and to provide stereoscopic views of eye structures .
• Educational Objective:
• Prisms are not used in keratometers.
explanation.ref: Clinical optics by Elkington Page: 52

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The change in the eye during accommodation is due to:

explanation.short: Ans. D) Contraction of Ciliary Muscles
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Increase in Zonular Tension : When the eye accommodates , the ciliary muscles contract . This contraction reduces the tension in the zonular fibers (zonules) that are attached to the lens capsule . This decrease in zonular tension allows the lens to become more convex (thicker), thereby increasing its refractive power to focus on near objects . This is a crucial aspect of accommodation .
• Option B . Relaxation of Ciliary Muscles : This is incorrect in the context of accommodation. During accommodation, the ciliary muscles contract , not relax .
• Option C . Flattening of Lens : This is actually the opposite of what occurs during accommodation. During accommodation, the lens becomes more convex (not flatter) to increase its refractive power for near vision .
• Educational Objective:
• During accommodation, the ciliary muscles contract , leading to a decrease in zonular tension , which allows the lens to become more convex and increase its refractive power for near vision .
explanation.ref: 141/ Anatomy and physiology of eye/ AK KHURANA/ 3 rd edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Which axis is a line passing through the centre of cornea, centre of lens and meets the retina on the nasal side of fovea?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. VISUAL AXIS : The line joining the fixation point, nodal point & fovea .
• Option C. FIXATION AXIS : The line joining the fixation point & centre of rotation .
• Educational Objective:
• The line passing through the centre of cornea, centre of lens and meeting the retina on the nasal side of fovea is the Optical Axis of the eye.
explanation.ref: Pg 2/ Anatomy & physiology of eyeball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A value of 0.75D was deduced from the retinoscopy reading as a part of drug correction. The likely drug used was?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Rough estimate of refractive error after Retinoscopy is given by:
• Amount of refractive error = Retinoscopic findings - deduction for distance - tonus allowance for cycloplegic drug used.
• TONUS ALLOWANCE OF CYCLOPLEGIC DRUGS
explanation.ref: KHURANA COMPREHENSIVE OPHTHALMOLOGY 5 TH EDITION PAGE 582

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Which of the following drugs is a Mydriatic but not a Cycloplegic?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Cyclopentolate : This is both a mydriatic and a cycloplegic agent . It dilates the pupil and also temporarily paralyzes the ciliary muscle , leading to loss of accommodation .
• Option B . Homatropine : Like cyclopentolate, homatropine is also both a mydriatic and a cycloplegic . It dilates the pupil and affects accommodation .
• Option C . Atropine : This is another example of a drug that is both a mydriatic and a cycloplegic . It causes prolonged pupil dilation and paralysis of accommodation .
• Educational Objective
• Phenylephrine is a mydriatic agent used to dilate the pupil without causing significant cycloplegia, making it suitable for procedures where pupil dilation is needed without affecting the eye's focusing ability.
explanation.ref: PARSONS DISEASES OF THE EYE 22 ND EDITION PAGE 155

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Homatropine was used for retinoscopic refraction. When is the ideal time for a post-mydriatic test?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Next Day : Patients in whom Tropicamide eye drop has been used as a cycloplegic agent can be called for post-mydriatic test the next day.
• Option C. 1 Week Later : When atropine is used as a cycloplegic agent, PMT can be done after 10 days .
• Option D. 2 Weeks Later : When atropine is used as a cycloplegic agent, PMT can be done after 10 days .
• Educational Objective:
• When homatropine is used for retinoscopic refraction, post-mydriatic test can be done after 3 days .
explanation.ref: KHURANA COMPREHENSIVE OPHTHALMOLOGY 5 TH ED PAGE 582

subject: Ophthalmology
question: LASIK is used to correct myopia upto how many dioptres?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. <2 D : This range is too low . LASIK can correct myopia beyond 2 dioptres (D).
• Option B . <10 D : LASIK is not commonly used to correct myopia in this range. Implantable Contact Lens or Refractive Lens exchange are preferred procedures in this range of myopia .
• Option D . <20 D : Correcting myopia of less than 20 dioptres with LASIK is generally considered too high and is associated with increased risks and potential complications . Implantable Contact Lens or Refractive Lens exchanges are preferred procedures in this range of myopia
• Educational Objective:
• LASIK can effectively and safely correct myopia up to approximately -8 dioptres , which is the standard acceptable range for this procedure.
explanation.ref: Pg 377/ theory & practise of optics & refraction/ 4 th edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Identify the false statement regarding LASIK procedure:
options: Minimal residual corneal thickness should be 450 microns
The minimum age for LASIK is above 18 years
Contact lens wear should be continued until procedure

explanation.short: Ans. C) Contact lens wear should be continued until procedure
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Minimal Residual Corneal Thickness Should be 450 Microns : This is true . A minimum residual corneal thickness is required post LASIK to ensure there's enough corneal tissue for reshaping and to maintain the integrity and health of the cornea post-surgery.
• Option B . The Minimum Age for LASIK is Above 18 Years : This is true . The minimum age for undergoing LASIK is typically 18 years, as it's important for the refractive error to have stabilized, which usually happens around or after this age.
• Option D . Done under Topical Anesthesia : This is true . LASIK surgery is usually performed under topical anesthesia (eye drops), which numbs the surface of the eye. This allows the procedure to be pain-free for the patient.
• Therefore, the false statement regarding LASIK procedure is C. Contact Lens Wear Should be Continued until Procedure .
• Educational Objective:
• Contact lens wear should be discontinued for a period before a LASIK procedure . Contact lenses can alter the shape of the cornea , and discontinuing their use allows the cornea to return to its natural shape , ensuring accurate measurements and a successful surgery .
explanation.ref: Pg 366/ theory & practise of optics & refraction/ 4 th edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Which among the following is not a contraindication for LASIK?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Keratoconus : This is a contraindication for LASIK. Keratoconus, a condition where the cornea thins and bulges into a cone-like shape , can worsen with LASIK surgery .
• Option C . Glaucoma : Active, uncontrolled glaucoma can be a contraindication for LASIK. The surgery can increase intraocular pressure , which can be detrimental in glaucoma patients .
• Option D . Blepharitis : This, a chronic inflammatory condition of the eyelids , can be a contraindication for LASIK. It increases the risk of postoperative infection and can affect the healing process .
• Educational Objective
• Among the options, an age greater than 18 years is not a contraindication for LASIK surgery. In fact, it is a requirement, as LASIK is generally recommended for adults with a stable refractive error.
explanation.ref: Pg 367/ theory & practise of optics & refraction/ 4 th edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 22-year-old female underwent LASIK procedure for right eye. What does the postoperative management include?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Patching/ Dark Goggles Systemic Analgesics Antibiotic Drops Topical Steroids Artificial Tears
• Educational Objective:
• Post-operative medications for LASIK surgery include topical antibiotic eyedrops , topical steroid eyedrops and systemic analgecics .
explanation.ref: Pg 383/ theory & practise of optics & refraction/ 4 th edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: What is true for SMILE surgery?

explanation.short: Ans. B) Small Incision Lenticule Extraction
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Small incision Lens extraction: It is a method of extracapsular cataract surgery .
• Option C . Also known as Phacoemulsification: Phacoemulsification is a method of extracapsular cataract surgery .
• Option D . Partial thickness keratoplasty: Also known as lamellar corneal grafting , this is a type of corneal transplantation .
• Educational Objective:
• SMILE is a type of refractive surgery .
explanation.ref: Pg 396/ theory & practise of optics & refraction/ 4 th edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: What is SMILE refractive surgery done on?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Lens : Surgeries performed on lens are cataract surgery and refractive lens exchange .
• Option C. Retina : Surgical procedures on the retina are typically related to treating retinal diseases , like retinal detachment or macular degeneration .
• Option D. Vitreous : Vitreous surgeries are generally conducted for conditions like vitreous hemorrhage or retinal detachment .
• Educational Objective
• SMILE refractive surgery is a corneal procedure used to correct myopia and astigmatism by reshaping the cornea through the removal of a small, lens-shaped tissue created using a laser .
explanation.ref: Pg 396 / theory & practise of optics & refraction/ 4 th edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
A 17-year-old female with no previous medical history, was brought to the emergency department following a high-speed motor vehicle collision. During evaluation, hyphema in the left eye was detected. Which of the following conditions does not require surgical intervention?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/05/12/66.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. D) Total hyphema within 1 hour
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. IOP ≥ 25 mmHg for > 5 Days : Elevated intraocular pressure (IOP) is a significant concern in hyphema . Persistent elevation (IOP ≥ 25 mmHg for more than 5 days) despite medical therapy (like topical beta-blockers, alpha agonists, and systemic carbonic anhydrase inhibitors) may indicate the need for surgical intervention . This is to prevent optic nerve damage and other complications associated with prolonged elevated IOP.
• Option B. Corneal Blood Staining : This occurs when red blood cells and hemoglobin infiltrate the corneal stroma . It is a severe complication of hyphema, especially in the setting of high IOP . Corneal blood staining is more likely when there is total hyphema or repeated bleeding. Surgical intervention is often recommended to prevent permanent visual impairment due to corneal opacity.
• Option C. IOP > 50 mmHg for >24 Hours : This is a critical condition. An IOP greater than 50 mmHg lasting more than 24 hours is a medical emergency due to the risk of optic nerve damage and other complications, such as corneal blood staining. Immediate surgical intervention is typically required to reduce the pressure and prevent irreversible damage.
• Educational Objective:
• In the management of hyphema following trauma , surgical intervention is typically indicated for IOP ≥ 25 mmHg lasting more than 5 days , corneal blood staining, and IOP > 50 mmHg lasting more than 24 hours . However, total hyphema within 1 hour of injury usually does not require immediate surgical intervention unless complications develop.
explanation.ref: American association of ophthalmology.  Https://eyewiki.aao.org/hyphema

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Which of the following is not a sign of a blow-out fracture of orbit?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Diplopia : It is a common symptom of blow-out fractures , often resulting from impaired movement of the affected eye due to muscle entrapment or nerve damage.
• Option C. Enophthalmos : This is the posterior displacement of the eye within the orbit , leading to a sunken appearance. It is a characteristic sign of blow-out fractures. The increase in the orbital volume caused by the fracture allows the eyeball to sink backward.
• Option D. Infraorbital nerve anesthesia : This refers to a loss of sensation in the area supplied by the infraorbital nerve , typically the lower eyelid, cheek, and upper lip . It can occur in blow-out fractures if the fracture involves the infraorbital groove or canal, where the nerve runs.
• Educational Objective
• Enophthalmos, not exophthalmos , is a sign of blow-out fracture of the orbit . Blow-out fractures typically increase orbital volume , allowing the eyeball to sink backward , leading to enophthalmos , rather than protruding forward as in exophthalmos.
explanation.ref: pg 864 kanski’s clinical ophthalmology 8 th edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 35-year-old woman presents with complaints of intermittent tearing from her left eye. Examination reveals no significant eye redness or visual impairment. She mentions the symptoms are more pronounced in windy conditions or when she is tired. Further examination of the eyelid function reveals a slight delay in tear drainage. Which part of the orbicularis oculi muscle, involved in tear drainage and commonly affected in such cases, is most likely responsible for her symptoms?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Orbital Part : This part of the orbicularis oculi muscle is involved in the forceful closure of the eyelids , as in blinking . It's not directly involved in tear drainage.
• Option B. Temporal Part : This is not a distinct part of the orbicularis oculi muscle ; rather, the muscle has palpebral and orbital parts . The temporal part does not relate to tear drainage.
• Option D. Muller’s Muscle : Muller's muscle, or the superior tarsal muscle , is a smooth muscle that contributes to eyelid elevation . It is not a part of the orbicularis oculi muscle and does not influence tear drainage.
• Educational Objective:
• Horner’s muscle refers to the lacrimal part of the orbicularis oculi muscle , which is integral in tear drainage.
explanation.ref: pg 492/ anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The only extraocular muscle with sympathetic nerve supply is?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Superior Rectus : This muscle, responsible for elevating the eye , is innervated by the oculomotor nerve (Cranial Nerve III), not by sympathetic nerves.
• Option B. Levator Palpebrae Superioris (LPS) : The LPS raises the upper eyelid and is also innervated by the oculomotor nerve . It is not supplied by sympathetic nerves.
• Optiion C. Medial Rectus : This muscle, which moves the eye medially (towards the nose), is innervated by the oculomotor nerve as well . It does not receive sympathetic innervation.
• Educational Objective:
• The only extraocular muscle with sympathetic nerve supply is Müller’s Muscle , a smooth muscle in the upper eyelid that aids in eyelid elevation.
explanation.ref: pg 497/ Anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Which is the thickest wall of the orbit?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Medial Wall : The medial wall of the orbit is one of the thinnest and most fragile parts . It is formed primarily by the ethmoid bone and partly by the lacrimal bone , the frontal bone , and the lesser wing of the sphenoid. This wall separates the orbital cavity from the ethmoid air cells , making it prone to injury and subsequent communication with the nasal cavity.
• Option C . Roof : The roof of the orbit is formed primarily by the orbital plate of the frontal bone and, to a lesser extent , by the lesser wing of the sphenoid . It separates the orbital contents from the anterior cranial fossa and provides protection from superior impacts . It's relatively thin compared to the lateral wall but stronger than the medial wall.
• Option D . Floor : The floor of the orbit is formed by the orbital surface of the maxillary bone , along with small contributions from the zygomatic bone and the palatine bone . This wall is thin and vulnerable to fractures , especially from trauma to the orbit, as it separates the orbital contents from the maxillary sinus.
• Educational Objective:
• The lateral wall is the thickest wall of the orbit , providing substantial protection to the eye from lateral impacts , and distinguishing itself in strength and thickness from the more fragile medial wall , the moderately strong roof , and the thin, vulnerable floor of the orbit .
explanation.ref: pg 578/ anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: How much What is the volume of the vitreous?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• The volume of the vitreous is = 4 ml .
• volume of the vitreous is = 4 ml
explanation.ref: pg 152/ anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: How much What is the volume of orbital cavity?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• The volume of the orbital cavity is 29-30 ml.
explanation.ref: anatomy & physiology of eye 3 rd edition – Ak Khurana (pg 575)

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a two-day history of severe headache, fever, and double vision. She recently had a dental abscess treated. On examination, she has a temperature of 38.5°C, and her visual examination shows impaired lateral movement of the right eye. There is no ptosis or pupillary abnormality. Her facial sensation is intact. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain shows evidence suggestive of cavernous sinus thrombosis. Which cranial nerve is most likely involved first in this condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/06/screenshot-2024-03-06-170036.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Oculomotor (Cranial Nerve III) : This nerve controls most of the eye's movements , the constriction of the pupil , and maintains an open eyelid . In cavernous sinus thrombosis , its involvement typically results in ptosis (drooping eyelid), pupillary dilation, and limited eye movement except for lateral and downward gaze.
• Option B. Trochlear (Cranial Nerve IV) : This nerve innervates the superior oblique muscle , which is responsible for downward and lateral eye movement . Its involvement would lead to vertical diplopia, particularly when looking down.
• Option D. Trigeminal (Cranial Nerve V) : This nerve is responsible for facial sensation and motor functions of the muscles of mastication . Involvement in cavernous sinus thrombosis would cause facial sensory loss and chewing difficulties but not eye movement disorders.
• Educational Objective:
• In cavernous sinus thrombosis , the abducent nerve (Cranial Nerve VI) is typically the earliest to be affected due to its vulnerability within the cavernous sinus , often presenting with an inability to abduct the eye and resulting in horizontal diplopia .
explanation.ref: pg 412,6th edition, comprehensive ophthalmology ak khurana

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 6-month-old infant is brought to the clinic by his parents who are concerned about his unusual eyelid movement. They report that every time he suckles while breastfeeding, his right upper eyelid retracts unusually. There is no history of trauma or surgery. On examination, you notice that the right upper eyelid droops slightly in a neutral facial expression and elevates when the infant is smiling or crying. This condition is likely due to an aberrant connection between the muscles innervated by which two cranial nerves?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. 3 & 7 : This option suggests a connection between the oculomotor nerve (CN III), which innervates the levator palpebrae superioris (responsible for eyelid elevation), and the facial nerve (CN VII), which controls facial expressions .
• Option B.  4 & 5 : The trochlear nerve (CN IV) innervates the superior oblique muscle of the eye , and the trigeminal nerve (CN V) is responsible for facial sensation and mastication . An aberrant connection here is unlikely to cause the described eyelid phenomenon.
• Option D. 5 & 7 : This option suggests a connection between the trigeminal nerve (CN V) and the facial nerve (CN VII). While the facial nerve is involved in facial expressions , the trigeminal nerve's role in sensation and mastication does not typically lead to eyelid synkinesis.
• Educational Objective
• For medical students preparing for residency, it's essential to understand that congenital synkinetic ptosis typically results from an aberrant innervation between the oculomotor nerve (CN III) and the motor division of Trigeminal nerve (CN V), leading to an unusual elevation of the eyelid during facial movements like smiling or crying.
• 2. Demirci H, Frueh BR, Nelson CC. Marcus Gunn jaw-winking synkinesis: clinical features and management. Ophthalmology. 2010 Jul;117(7):1447-52. doi: 10.1016/j.ophtha.2009.11.014. Epub 2010 Feb 25. PMID: 20188419.
explanation.ref: pg 41, eyelids -kanski’s clinical ophthalmology 8 th edition.

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
A 59-year-old male patient came with the complaint of shortness of breath. On examination, fullness in the right supraclavicular region is observed. On ocular examination, right pupil is constricted. Which of the following is not a feature of the probable diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/05/12/62.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Ptosis: This is a hallmark feature of Horner's syndrome. It occurs due to paralysis of the Müller's muscle, a sympathetically innervated muscle in the eyelid, leading to a slight drooping of the upper eyelid.
• Option B. Anhidrosis: This refers to the absence or decrease in sweating on the affected side of the face . It is a feature of Horner's syndrome due to the disruption of sympathetic nerves that innervate sweat glands.
• Option D. Enophthalmos: This refers to the posterior displacement of the eyeball within the orbit, leading to a sunken appearance. While mild enophthalmos can be associated with Horner's syndrome due to the lack of sympathetic tone to the orbitalis muscle, it is not as prominent or diagnostic as the other features.
• Heterochromia iridis is not a typical feature of adult onset Horner's syndrome. The classic triad of Horner's syndrome consists of ptosis, miosis, and anhidrosis, with enophthalmos being a less prominent associated feature.
explanation.ref: pg344/anatomy & physiology of eye 3 rd edition – ak Khurana (pg 575)

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A patient present with proptosis, vision changes, and eye pain. After thorough examination and imaging, the ophthalmologist makes the diagnosis of an orbital tumour. Which is the most common benign orbital tumour in adults?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Rhabdomyosarcoma : This is a type of malignant tumor that arises from skeletal muscle. It is the most common primary orbital malignancy in children , not adults.
• Option C. Non-Hodgkin’s Lymphoma : This represents a group of blood cancers that develop in the lymphatic system. While it can involve the orbit, it is not classified as a benign tumor . Orbital involvement in non-Hodgkin’s lymphoma usually indicates a systemic disease rather than a primary orbital tumor.
• Option D . Neuroblastoma : This is a malignant tumor that arises from nerve tissue . It is most commonly seen in children and is not a benign tumor.
• Educational Objective:
• The most common benign orbital tumor in adults is a cavernous hemangioma.
explanation.ref: pg 102/orbit/kanski’s clinical ophthalmology 8 th edition.

subject: Ophthalmology
question: In a 32-year-old female presenting with muscle tremors and palpitations, ocular examination reveals proptosis, eyelid retraction, and restricted eye movements. Graves' disease is suspected, and further investigations are underway for confirmation. What is the sequence of involvement of extraocular muscles in this case?
options: Superior rectus> Lateral rectus> Inferior rectus> Medial rectus
Lateral rectus> Inferior rectus> Medial rectus> Superior rectus
Medial rectus> Superior rectus> Lateral rectus> Inferior rectus
Inferior rectus> Medial rectus> Superior rectus> Lateral rectus

explanation.short: Ans. D) Inferior rectus> Medial rectus> Superior rectus> Lateral rectus
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• The sequence of involvement of the extraocular muscles in Graves' ophthalmopathy typically follows a specific pattern:
• Inferior Rectus Muscle : This muscle is most commonly affected first . Its involvement leads to restricted upward gaze and may contribute to the appearance of exophthalmos . Medial Rectus Muscle : Following the inferior rectus , the medial rectus is often the next muscle to be involved. Its dysfunction can result in limited lateral gaze . Superior Rectus Muscle : This muscle, often affected concurrently with the levator palpebrae superioris ( responsible for eyelid elevation), can lead to difficulties in downward gaze . Lateral Rectus Muscle : This muscle is less commonly affected than the others , but its involvement can lead to limited medial gaze. Superior Oblique Muscle : This muscle is the least commonly affected in Graves' ophthalmopathy .
• Educational Objective:
• Mnemonic: Order of involvement of EOM in Graves ophthalmopathy: IM SLOw ( I nferior rectus> M edial rectus> S uperior rectus> L ateral rectus> O bliques)
explanation.ref: pg 83/orbit/ kanski’s clinical ophthalmology 8 th edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
A 40-year-old male patient with an alleged history of assault was reported at the department of oral and maxillofacial surgery with orbital ecchymosis, periorbital oedema, subconjunctival haemorrhage and tenderness in the infraorbital region. Limitation of which of the following extra ocular movement occurs?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/05/12/69.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Depression (Inferior Rectus Muscle) : Normally, the inferior rectus muscle facilitates the depression of the eyeball (looking downward). In orbital floor fractures , the inferior rectus muscle can be entrapped or edematous, leading to restricted movement. However, this typically results in difficulty with elevation due to inhibited action against the superior rectus muscle, rather than depression itself.
• Option B. Intorsion (Superior Oblique Muscle) : Intorsion, or inward rotation of the eyeball , is controlled by the superior oblique muscle . Orbital floor fractures usually do not impact the trochlear nerve or the superior oblique muscle, so intorsion is generally unaffected.
• Option D. Abduction (Lateral Rectus Muscle) : Abduction, the movement of the eyeball outward , away from the nose, is controlled by the lateral rectus muscle . This movement is typically not affected in orbital floor fractures as the lateral rectus muscle and its nerve supply (abducens nerve) are not involved in the trauma.
• Educational Objective:
• In the context of an orbital floor fracture , the most commonly affected extraocular movement is elevation , primarily due to the entrapment or impairment of the inferior rectus muscle , which creates resistance against the superior rectus muscle responsible for elevating the eye.
explanation.ref: pg 423/ orbit/ 6 th edition/ comprehensive ophthalmology ak khurana

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Identify the sign:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/05/12/70_0SipIVh.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Dalrymple's Sign : This is characterized by an abnormal widening of the palpebral fissure due to retraction of the upper eyelid , giving the appearance of a stare .
• Option C. Stellwag's Sign : This sign is marked by a reduced frequency or absence of blinking , contributing to the characteristic stare of Graves' disease .
• Option D. Sign : It indicates an inability or weakness in maintaining convergence of the eyes , often observed during prolonged focusing on a near object .
• Educational Objective:
• The delayed movement of the upper eyelid when the eye moves downward , creating a noticeable rim of sclera above the iris is Von Graffe’s sign .
explanation.ref: American association of ophthalmologyhttps://eyewiki.aao.org/thyroid_eye_disease

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
The position of an intraocular foreign body is estimated from its relationship with the metallic ring placed at which part of the eye?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/05/12/74.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Anterior Chamber : This is the fluid-filled space between the cornea and the iris. While foreign bodies can be present here, the anterior chamber is not typically used as a reference point for localizing IOFBs , as it doesn't provide a comprehensive reference for the entire eye.
• Option C. Cornea : The cornea is the transparent front part of the eye that covers the iris and pupil . While it is critical in the examination of the eye, it is not typically used as a reference for localizing IOFBs , as its curvature and clarity can complicate precise localization.
• Option D. Vitreous : The vitreous is a clear gel that fills the space between the lens and the retina . Although IOFBs can be located in the vitreous , using it as a reference point is challenging due to its transparency and the lack of anatomical landmarks within it.
• Educational Objective:
• When localizing an intraocular foreign body , the limbus , the junction between the cornea and the sclera , serves as the primary reference point due to its circumferential nature and alignment with key eye structures , aiding in precise three-dimensional localization of the foreign body .
explanation.ref: pg 435/ ocular injuries/ comprehensive ophthalmology 6 th edition ak khurana

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
A 30-year-old male patient gave an alleged history of RTA leading to injury to right eye. He presented with severe proptosis, chemosis, diplopia and an orbital bruit three weeks after the accident. What is the probable diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/05/12/75.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Orbital Cellulitis : An infection of the orbital tissues resulting in painful eye movements , redness , swelling , and sometimes impaired vision . While it can cause proptosis and diplopia, the absence of an orbital bruit and the acute onset of symptoms post-trauma make it less likely in this scenario.
• Option C. Thyroid Orbitopathy : Associated with autoimmune thyroid disease (like Graves' disease), it presents with eyelid retraction, proptosis, and diplopia . However, it usually develops gradually and does not produce an orbital bruit , which makes it an unlikely diagnosis in this acute post-traumatic setting.
• Option D. Preseptal Cellulitis : Involves the eyelid and periorbital skin without affecting the orbit itself . Symptoms include eyelid redness and swelling. It doesn’t typically cause proptosis, diplopia, or an orbital bruit, and it's unrelated to the kind of trauma described in the scenario.
• Educational Objective:
• In a patient presenting with proptosis , chemosis , diplopia , and an orbital bruit following a traumatic event , the most probable diagnosis is a Carotico-Cavernous Fistula , a vascular anomaly resulting from a connection between the carotid arterial system and the cavernous sinus , typically seen after trauma.
explanation.ref: pg.93/ orbit/ kanski’s clinical ophthalmology 8 th edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
A 55-year-old woman presented with complaints of swelling and pain at the lateral aspect of the right eye since 1 month. The swelling was associated with proptosis and abduction restriction. Computed tomography (CT) showed extra ocular muscle enlargement involving tendon. What is the probable diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/05/12/77.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Orbital varix:
• Intermittent proptosis Increases on bending forward.
• Option D. Ocular myasthenia:
• Usually B/L Fatigability Diurnal variation
• 2. Winter J, Centeno RS, Bentson JR. Maneuver to aid diagnosis of orbital varix by computed tomography. AJNR Am J Neuroradiol. 1982 Jan-Feb;3(1):39-40. PMID: 6800238; PMCID: PMC8332902.
explanation.ref: pg 412/ orbit/ 6 th edition/comprehensive ophthalmology ak khurana

subject: Ophthalmology
question: In a 35-year-old woman with a history of right eye ptosis for 6 months, the eyelids appear asymmetrical, and the ptosis fluctuates throughout the day, worsening in the evening. The fatigability test shows positive results. Which of the following tests is not helpful in diagnosing the condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Edrophonium Test : This test is useful in diagnosing MG . Edrophonium, a short-acting acetylcholinesterase inhibitor , temporarily increases the availability of acetylcholine at the neuromuscular junction , leading to an improvement in muscle strength in patients with MG.
• Option B. Ice Pack Test : This test is also helpful in MG . Cooling improves neuromuscular transmission , and thus, application of an ice pack on the eyelids can temporarily improve ptosis in MG.
• Option D. Electromyography (EMG) : EMG, especially single-fiber EMG, is a key diagnostic tool in MG . It measures the electrical activity of muscles and can detect the characteristic patterns of neuromuscular transmission failure seen in MG.
• Educational Objective:
• In the evaluation of a patient with fluctuating ptosis and positive fatigability , suggesting Myasthenia Gravis , the Snap Back Test is not useful as it assesses eyelid laxity rather than neuromuscular strength or transmission, which are the key pathological features in Myasthenia Gravis.
explanation.ref: pg 838/ neuro ophthalmology/ kanski’s clinical ophthalmology 8th edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 30-year-old male presented with fever, chills, and rigor. He has a history of prolonged use of nasal decongestants. On examination, decreased vision, painful ophthalmoplegia, diplopia on lateral gaze, proptosis, and chemosis are noted. Additionally, there is optic disc congestion. What is the probable diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Orbital cellulitis:
• Similar presentation. Slow onset. Mild systemic features. EOM restriction is present from the beginning.
• Option C. Superior orbital fissure syndrome:
• Only cranial nerves III, IV, VI involvement. Vision loss absent.
• Option D. Thyroid orbitopathy:
• Usually B/L. Fever, chills not present. Systemic features of thyrotoxicosis present.
• Educational Objective:
• Given the clinical presentation and history of the acute onset of systemic and ocular symptoms , as well as the history of prolonged use of nasal decongestants , the most probable diagnosis is Cavernous Sinus Thrombosis .
explanation.ref: pg 411/ orbit/ 6 th edition comprehensive ophthalmology ak khurana

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Superior orbital fissure syndrome involves cranial nerves except:

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Optic Nerve is not a content of the superior orbital fissure .

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
A 10-year-old boy was brought to the pediatric emergency with complaints of 5 days of progressive left facial swelling and fever. On ocular inspection, left periorbital oedema, erythema and discharge are noted. On examination, left eye showed decrease in visual acuity, and the pupil showed relative afferent pupillary defect. What is the probable diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/05/12/82.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. AV Malformation (Arteriovenous Malformation) : This is an abnormal connection between arteries and veins , bypassing the capillary system . It can cause swelling and other issues due to altered blood flow , but it typically does not present with infection-like symptoms (fever, discharge) or a rapid onset of visual changes and RAPD.
• Option C. Retinoblastoma : A malignant tumor of the retina , common in children . It usually presents with a white pupillary reflex (leukocoria) and may cause vision loss . However, retinoblastoma does not typically cause fever , periorbital edema, or erythema, which are indicative of an infectious process.
• Option D. Neuroblastoma : A cancer that develops from immature nerve cells , mostly occurring in children . It can present with various symptoms depending on the site of the tumor but is unlikely to cause localized periorbital edema , erythema, and discharge without other systemic or neurological symptoms.
• Educational Objective:
• In a pediatric patient presenting with fever , progressive facial swelling , periorbital edema , erythema , discharge , decreased visual acuity , and RAPD , the most probable diagnosis is Orbital Cellulitis , an infectious condition characterized by inflammation and infection of the orbital tissues, often requiring prompt medical intervention.
explanation.ref: pg 409/ orbit/ 6th edition comprehensive ophthalmology  ak khurana

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The only extraocular muscle with sympathetic nerve supply is?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Superior Rectus : Innervated by the oculomotor nerve (Cranial Nerve III).
• Option B. Levator Palpebrae Superioris (LPS) : Also innervated by the oculomotor nerve , this muscle primarily elevates the upper eyelid .
• Option C. Medial Rectus : Innervated by the oculomotor nerve , this muscle controls the movement of the eye towards the nose (adduction).
• Educational Objective:
• Muller’s muscle is innervated by sympathetic nervous system .
explanation.ref: pg 497/ Anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The contents of the superior orbital fissure are all except?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• CN2 does not pass through the superior orbital fissure .
explanation.ref: pg 580/ Anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The optic canal contains?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Optic nerve & Ophthalmic artery
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Optic nerve & Oculomotor nerve – CN 3 passes through the superior orbital fissure .
• Option B. Optic Nerve and Abducent Nerve – CN 6 passes through the superior orbital fissure .
• Option D. Optic Nerve and Central Retinal Artery – The Central Retinal Artery originates from the ophthalmic artery , which is a branch of the internal carotid artery . The ophthalmic artery enters the orbit through the optic canal along with the optic nerve . The central retinal artery, a critical vessel for the retina, branches off from the ophthalmic artery and enters the optic nerve approximately 8-15 mm behind the globe (eyeball). It then runs within the optic nerve before emerging on the optic disc in the eye to supply the inner layers of the retina.
• Educational Objective:
• The contents of the optic canal are the optic nerve and the ophthalmic artery.
explanation.ref: pg 580/ Anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The following structures pass through the infra orbital fissure, except?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Infraorbital Nerve : This nerve, a branch of the maxillary nerve (V2), indeed passes through the infraorbital fissure to enter the orbit and then continues to the face through the infraorbital canal.
• Option B. Orbital Branch of the Pterygopalatine Ganglion : This is correct ; the orbital branches of the pterygopalatine ganglion pass through the infraorbital fissure to supply the orbit.
• Option C. Inferior Ophthalmic Vein : The inferior ophthalmic vein, which drains blood from the orbit, also passes through the infraorbital fissure.
• Educational Objective:
• In the context of orbital anatomy, the facial nerve does not pass through the infraorbital fissure.
explanation.ref: pg 581/ Anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 50-year-old male patient who visited the OPD with difficulty in opening his left eye and complaints of double-vision in the same eye, has been diagnosed with left oculomotor nerve palsy. Which of the extraocular muscle will be spared?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Inferior Rectus : The inferior rectus muscle is innervated by the oculomotor nerve (Cranial Nerve III). This nerve innervates most of the extraocular muscles , except for the superior oblique and lateral rectus muscles.
• Option C. Levator Palpebrae Superioris : This muscle, which elevates the upper eyelid , is also innervated by the oculomotor nerve (Cranial Nerve III). Its proper functioning is essential for normal eyelid elevation.
• Option D. Superior Rectus : Like the inferior rectus and levator palpebrae superioris , the superior rectus muscle is also innervated by the oculomotor nerve (Cranial Nerve III). This muscle is responsible for elevating the eyeball (looking up).
• Educational Objective:
• The superior oblique muscle is innervated by the trochlear nerve (Cranial Nerve IV), while the inferior rectus, levator palpebrae superioris, and superior rectus muscles are all innervated by the oculomotor nerve (Cranial Nerve III).
explanation.ref: pg 359/ / Anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 45-year-old male presents with horizontal double vision and limited abduction of the right eye, indicating the weakness of Lateral Rectus muscle of the right eye. Which cranial nerve supplies the involved muscle?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Abducens nerve supplies the lateral rectus , responsible for abduction of the eyeball .
explanation.ref: pg 358/ Anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 45-year-old patient presents to the ophthalmology clinic with a complaint of gradual protrusion of the right eye over the past few months. The patient reports no pain or visual disturbances but is concerned about the noticeable change in eye position. Upon examination, the ophthalmologist notices axial proptosis (forward displacement) of the right eye. Further evaluation reveals a palpable resistance to retropulsion of the globe suggestive of a mass behind the eye. Which surgical space in the orbit is most likely involved in this case, causing the axial proptosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Extraconal Space : This space lies outside the muscle cone , between the orbital bones and the orbital septum . While masses here can cause proptosis, they typically do not cause the globe to resist retropulsion as much as intraconal masses do.
• Option B. Subperiosteal Space : This space is located between the periosteum and the orbital bones. While subperiosteal hematomas or abscesses can cause proptosis, they are usually more localized and do not commonly result in a palpable resistance to retropulsion of the globe.
• Option D. Subtenon’s Space : This space lies between the sclera and Tenon’s capsule . Lesions in this space are less likely to cause significant proptosis or resistance to retropulsion as compared to intraconal lesions.
• Educational Objective:
• In cases of axial proptosis with palpable resistance to retropulsion of the globe , the intraconal space of the orbit is most likely involved.
• Ref : Pg 483 /Ch. 30 Diseases of the orbit/ 22 nd edition/ Parsons diseases of the eye
explanation.ref: pg 585/ Anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The optic nerve leaves the eye by piercing the sclera at lamina cribrosa, traverses the optic canal and enters the intracranial cavity. The optic canal connects the orbit to the which part of the cranium?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Anterior Cranial Fossa : This is the front part of the floor of the cranial cavity , housing the frontal lobes of the brain . However, the optic canal does not open into the anterior cranial fossa .
• Option C . Posterior Cranial Fossa : This is the back part of the cranial cavity, containing the cerebellum, brainstem, and related structures . The optic canal does not connect to the posterior cranial fossa.
• Option D. Maxillary Sinus : This is one of the paranasal sinuses, located in the maxillary bone . It is not connected to the orbit via the optic canal.
• Educational Objective:
• The optic canal connects the orbit to the middle cranial fossa , allowing the optic nerve and ophthalmic artery to pass from the orbit to the brain .
explanation.ref: pg 580/ Anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
The highlighted muscle is innervated by which nerve?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/06/18/picture-8.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Oculomotor Nerve (Cranial Nerve III): This nerve primarily controls most of the eye's movements and the constriction of the pupil . However, it does not innervate the orbicularis oculi muscle.
• Option C . Abducent Nerve (Cranial Nerve VI): This nerve innervates the lateral rectus muscle of the eye , which is responsible for moving the eye laterally (outward). It does not innervate the orbicularis oculi.
• Option D . Trigeminal Nerve (Cranial Nerve V): The trigeminal nerve is the main sensory nerve of the face and also provides motor fibers to the muscles of mastication. It does not provide innervation to the orbicularis oculi muscle.
• Educational Objective:
• The orbicularis oculi muscle , responsible for closing the eyelids , is innervated by the facial nerve (Cranial Nerve VII), which is key to understanding facial muscle control and the implications of facial nerve pathologies.
explanation.ref: pg 491/ / Anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Identify the marked structure in the cavernous sinus:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/06/18/ophthalmology-diagrams-217.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Central Retinal Artery : The Central Retinal Artery is a branch of the ophthalmic artery and enters the eye along with the optic nerve , supplying blood to the inner retinal layers.
• Option B. Anterior Cerebral Artery : The Anterior Cerebral Artery originates from the internal carotid artery and travels anteriorly and medially to supply the medial portion of the frontal lobes and the superior medial parietal lobes of the brain.
• Option D. Middle Meningeal Artery : The Middle Meningeal Artery is a branch of the maxillary artery (itself a branch of the external carotid artery) and enters the cranium through the foramen spinosum to supply the dura mater and the calvaria.
• Educational Objective:
• The internal carotid artery lies within the cavity of the cavernous sinus .
explanation.ref: pg 519/ / Anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
The marked muscle is innervated by?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/06/18/ophthalmology-q-bank-36.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . CN 2 – It is the optic nerve a purely sensory nerve .
• Option C . CN 4 – The supratrochlear nerve supplies the Superior Oblique Muscle .
• Option D . CN 6 – The Abducens nerve supplies the lateral rectus muscle .
• Educational Objective:
• The muscle marked in the diagram is the superior rectus muscle , supplied by the CN 3 .

subject: Ophthalmology
question: At what stage of gestation does the development of the eyeball begin?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Day 1 : This marks the beginning of gestation when fertilization typically occurs . At this stage, the zygote (fertilized egg) is just beginning to undergo cell division . There is no specific organ development occurring yet, including the development of the eyeball.
• Option B. Day 30 : By this time, major developmental processes are underway , but this is past the initial formation of the eye structures. The eye begins to develop earlier than day 30 .
• Option D. Day 7 : At this stage, the embryo is undergoing implantation in the uterine wall . There is no specific organ development occurring at this early stage, including the eyes.
• Educational Objective:
• The development of the eyeball begins around Day 22 of gestation with the formation of the optic grooves or sulci , leading to the development of optic vesicles .
explanation.ref: pg 8/ Comprehensive ophthalmology/ AK KHURANA/ 6 th edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Which is the smallest diameter of an eyeball?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Horizontal : The horizontal diameter , also known as the equatorial diameter , typically measures about 24 mm . This is the widest diameter of the eye .
• Option C. Anteroposterior : The anteroposterior diameter , or axial length , of the eye is the distance from the front (cornea) to the back (retina) of the eye . This measures approximately 22 to 24 mm in adults , making it similar or slightly less than the horizontal diameter.
• Option D. Transverse : The term "transverse diameter" is less commonly used in reference to eye anatomy and generally considered synonymous with the horizontal diameter.
• Educational Objective:
• The smallest diameter of an eyeball is the vertical diameter.
explanation.ref: pg 2/ anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Which structure of the eye marks the boundary for the anterior segment, including all the structures located in front of it?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Cornea : The cornea is the transparent anteriormost structure of the eyeball .
• Option C. Iris : The iris is the pigmented part of the eye that controls the size of the pupil and amount of light that enters the eye . It is part of the anterior segment but does not define its boundary. The iris-pupillary plane divides the anterior segment of eye into anterior chamber and posterior chamber.
• Option D. Vitreous : The vitreous is a clear gel that fills the space between the lens and the retina in the posterior segment of the eye.
• Educational Objective:
• The crystalline lens marks the boundary for the anterior segment of the eye , including all the structures located in front of it .
explanation.ref: pg 5 / anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 3-year-old child presents with leukocoria (white glow) in the right eye and occasional eye pain. Clinical examination reveals a white, elevated mass with vascular changes in the posterior segment of the eye. The posterior segment of the eyeball encompasses structures located behind which specific part of the eye?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Pupil : The pupil is the opening in the center of the iris that allows light to enter the eye . The iris-pupil plane divides the anterior segment of the eye into anterior chamber and posterior chamber .
• Option B . Ciliary body : The ciliary body is part of the eye's uveal tract and is situated behind the iris. It controls the shape of the lens and produces aqueous humor, but it is not the dividing line between the anterior and posterior segments.
• Option C . Anterior surface of lens : The anterior surface of the lens is the front part of the lens . It would be more accurate to consider the entire lens as the dividing structure, rather than just its anterior surface.
• Educational Objective:
• The posterior segment of the eyeball encompasses structures located behind the posterior surface of the lens .
explanation.ref: pg 5/ anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Which drug causes the given presentation?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/01/29/picture2_hPKDWFh.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Topical Steroids are used in the treatment of Cystoid Macular Edema .
• Option B . Topical Beta Blockers are commonly used for glaucoma treatment and do not typically cause Cystoid Macular Edema. Their primary mechanism of action is reducing aqueous humor production in the eye .
• Option D . Topical Cycloplegics are used to paralyze the ciliary muscle and dilate the pupil . While they have various effects on the eye , they are not typically associated with causing Cystoid Macular Edema .
• Educational Objective
• Topical Prostaglandin Analogues can cause Cystoid Macular Edema .

• Educational Objective
