subject: Ophthalmology
question: Henle’s Layer is the thickened Outer Plexiform Layer in which of the following region?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . The parafoveal area is the region surrounding the fovea. While it is a significant part of the macula and has a high density of photoreceptors , Henle's layer is not specifically characterized as being thickened in this region.
• Option C . The optic disc, where the optic nerve exits the eye , does not contain photoreceptors, and therefore does not have Henle's layer. This region is known as the blind spot because it lacks photoreceptors.
• Option D . The peripheral retina is important for peripheral vision and is rich in rods , but it does not feature the thickened Henle's layer that is characteristic of the fovea .
• Educational Objective
• Henle’s layer is a thickened part of the outer plexiform layer predominantly found in the foveal region of the retina .
• Pg 172/ Anatomy & Physiology of Eyeball /3 rd Edition/ AK KHURANA
explanation.ref: Pg 172/ Anatomy & Physiology of Eyeball /3 rd Edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 30-year-old patient diagnosed with Best Vitelliform Dystrophy, characterized by the classic "Egg-Yolk" appearance in the fundus examination, is evaluated for Retinal function. Given this Hereditary Retinal Disorder's typical Fundus findings and its pathophysiology, which of the following statements most accurately describes the expected results of electrophysiological testing in this patient?
options: Electrooculography (EOG) will likely show a normal light peak, while Electroretinography (ERG) will demonstrate Abnormal Rod and Cone responses.
Both EOG and ERG will show significantly diminished responses due to Generalized Retinal Dysfunction.
EOG will likely show an Abnormal Arden Ratio, while ERG will remain Normal or Minimally Affected.
ERG will reveal a significant reduction in B-Wave Amplitude, while EOG will show a Normal Dark Trough.

explanation.short: Ans. C) EOG will likely show an Abnormal Arden Ratio, while ERG will remain Normal or Minimally Affected.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . This choice is incorrect . In Best vitelliform dystrophy , the primary abnormality is detected in the EOG , not the ERG. The ERG generally remains normal or minimally affected , as the primary pathology is in the retinal pigment epithelium rather than the photoreceptors.
• Option B . This is not typically seen in Best vitelliform dystrophy . While the EOG shows abnormalities , the ERG is usually normal or only slightly affected , as the disorder primarily affects the retinal pigment epithelium .
• Option D . This choice is incorrect . In Best vitelliform dystrophy , the significant abnormality is observed in the EOG , not the ERG. The ERG , which measures photoreceptor function , is generally normal or only slightly affected .
• Educational Objective
• In Best vitelliform dystrophy , electrooculography (EOG) typically shows an abnormal Arden ratio , indicating retinal pigment epithelium dysfunction , while electroretinography (ERG) remains normal or minimally affected .

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 55-year-old patient presents with visual disturbances and complains of difficulty seeing objects clearly. During the examination, you suspect dysfunction in the visual pathway and decide to assess the first order neuron of the pathway. Which of the following serve as the first order neurons in the visual pathway?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Ganglion cells are the second order neurons in the visual pathway . They receive signals from the bipolar cells and their axons form the optic nerve , carrying the visual information to the brain .
• Option C . Rods & Cones are the photoreceptors located in the retina and are responsible for converting light into electrical signals . These signals are then transmitted to the bipolar cells .
• Option D. None of the Above.
• Educational Objective
• The first order neurons in the visual pathway is the bipolar cells .
explanation.ref: Pg 172/ Anatomy & Physiology of Eyeball /3 rd Edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Which of the following is the 3 rd order Neuron in the Visual Pathway?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . The visual cortex, located in the occipital lobe of the brain , is the destination of the visual pathway , where the third-order neurons terminate and the processing of visual information occurs. However, the visual cortex itself is not a neuron, but rather a brain region composed of many neurons.
• Option C . The ganglion cells in the retina are the second-order neurons in the visual pathway . Their axons form the optic nerve , which carries visual information to the brain. They receive input from the bipolar cells ( first-order neurons ) and transmit signals to the LGB .
• Option D . Bipolar cells are the first-order neurons in the visual pathway . They receive input from the photoreceptors ( rods and cones ) and transmit signals to the retinal ganglion cells .
• Educational Objective
• The third-order neurons in the visual pathway are the axons from the Lateral Geniculate Body that project to the visual cortex .
• Pg 184/ Anatomy & Physiology of Eyeball /3 rd Edition/ AK KHURANA
explanation.ref: Pg 184/ Anatomy & Physiology of Eyeball /3 rd Edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Mr. Singh, a 65-year-old with hypertension, undergoes regular eye screening. The ophthalmologist examines his retinal blood vessels and confirms a normal arteriovenous (A: V) ratio. What is the normal A:V ratio of retinal blood vessels?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• The normal artery: vein ratio in the retina is 2:3 .
explanation.ref: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK525980/ , Pg 312/ Ch. 20 Diseases of theRetina/ Ed. 22 Parsons Disease of the Eye

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The point of maximum visual acuity on the Retina is?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . The blind spot , or optic disc , is the point on the retina where the optic nerve exits the eye . There are no photoreceptors ( rods or cones ) in this area, so it actually corresponds to a lack of visual perception , not maximum acuity.
• Option C . The macula lutea is an area of the retina that includes the fovea centralis . It is responsible for central vision and contains a high concentration of photoreceptors . However, the specific point of maximum visual acuity is the fovea centralis , not the entire macula lutea.
• Option D . The temporal retina refers to the portion of the retina situated towards the temporal side of the head . While important for vision, it is not the site of maximum visual acuity. The highest visual acuity is localized in the fovea centralis .
• Educational Objective
• The point of maximum visual acuity on the retina is the fovea centralis .
• Pg 260/ Anatomy & Physiology of Eyeball /3 rd Edition/ AK KHURANA
explanation.ref: Pg 260/ Anatomy & Physiology of Eyeball /3 rd Edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Identify the false statement:
options: Deprivation of Glucose for 8 to 10 minutes results in Irreversible Damage.
Ability to Discriminate between Different Colours is a Function of Rods.
Retina does not need Insulin for Glucose to Enter the Cells.
Photopic Vision is controlled by Cones.

explanation.short: Ans. B) Ability to Discriminate between Different Colours is a Function of Rods.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . This statement is generally true , especially in the context of brain cells . Neurons are highly dependent on a continuous supply of glucose , and deprivation for this length of time can lead to irreversible neuronal damage .
• Option C. This statement is true . The retina is one of the few tissues in the body that does not require insulin for glucose uptake. Retinal cells can absorb glucose from the bloodstream through insulin-independent mechanisms .
• Option D . This statement is true . Photopic vision refers to vision under well-lit conditions , and it is mediated by cones , which are responsible for high-acuity color vision .
• Educational Objective
• Colour discrimination is a function of cones . Rod cells mediate scotopic vision .
• Pg 146/ Anatomy and Physiology of Eye/ AK KHURANA/ 3 rd Edition
explanation.ref: Pg 146/ Anatomy and Physiology of Eye/ AK KHURANA/ 3 rd Edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: ERG (Electroretinogram) is used for the diagnosis & prognosis of which of the following Retinal Disorder(s)?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Electroretinogram can be used for identifying retinal disorders at an early stage such as:
• Retinitis Pigmentosa Diabetic Retinopathy Retinal Detachment Vascular Occlusions Chloroquine Toxicity
• Pg 235/ Anatomy and Physiology of Eye/ AK KHURANA/ 3 rd Edition
explanation.ref: Pg 235/ Anatomy and Physiology of Eye/ AK KHURANA/ 3 rd Edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The important Retinal Vessels are present in which layer of the Retina?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . The retinal pigment epithelium is a layer of cells located adjacent to the choroid . It plays a critical role in supporting the photoreceptors and maintaining the visual cycle , but it does not contain the retinal blood vessels.
• Option C . The inner plexiform layer is situated between the inner nuclear layer and the ganglion cell layer . It contains synapses between bipolar cells , amacrine cells , and ganglion cells . Although it is vascularized , it does not contain the main retinal blood vessels.
• Option D . Outer Nuclear Layer contains the cell bodies of the photoreceptors ( rods and cones ) but does not contain the retinal blood vessels.
• Educational Objective
• The important retinal vessels are located in the nerve fiber layer of the retina .
• pg 175/ / anatomy & physiology of eyeball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA
explanation.ref: pg 175/ / anatomy & physiology of eyeball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The total number of Short Posterior Ciliary Arteries are?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• There are 10 to 20 short posterior cilliary arteries .
• Pg 150/ Anatomy & Physiology of Eyeball /3 rd Edition/ AK KHURANA
explanation.ref: Pg 150/ Anatomy & Physiology of Eyeball /3 rd Edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Central Retinal Artery is a Branch of?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Middle Meningeal Artery is a branch of the maxillary artery , which is a terminal branch of the external carotid artery . The middle meningeal artery primarily supplies the meninges of the brain and does not give rise to the central retinal artery.
• Option B . While the internal carotid artery is a major artery supplying the brain and ophthalmic structures , the central retinal artery specifically is not a direct branch of it. Instead, the internal carotid artery gives rise to the ophthalmic artery , from which the central retinal artery branches off.
• Option D . The posterior cerebral artery is one of the terminal branches of the basilar artery and supplies parts of the brain , including the occipital lobe and the inferior temporal lobe . It is not involved in the blood supply to the retina or the eye.
• Educational Objective
• The Central Retinal Artery is a branch of the Ophthalmic Artery
• Pg 510/ Anatomy & Physiology of Eyeball /3 rd Edition/ AK KHURANA
explanation.ref: Pg 510/ Anatomy & Physiology of Eyeball /3 rd Edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Identify the highlighted structure.

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/01/29/picture13.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . The Central Retinal Artery originates from the ophthalmic artery , which is a branch of the internal carotid artery .
• Option B . The Anterior Choroidal Artery is a branch of the internal carotid artery , typically arising near the origin of the posterior communicating artery .
• Option C . The Internal Carotid Artery arises from the common carotid artery in the neck .
• Educational Objective:
• The Ophthalmic Artery is the first major branch of the internal carotid artery once it enters the cranial cavity through the carotid canal .
• Pg 510/ Anatomy & Physiology of Eyeball /3 rd Edition/ AK KHURANA
explanation.ref: Pg 510/ Anatomy & Physiology of Eyeball /3 rd Edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The strongest attachment of the Vitreous Body to the surrounding is at the level of which structure?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . The vitreous body does not have a strong attachment to the macula. The vitreous is in contact with the macula but does not adhere strongly to it.
• Option C . The optic disc is another point of contact for the vitreous body , but it is not the strongest point of attachment. The vitreous can exert traction on the optic disc , particularly in pathological states , but this is not a primary site of firm attachment.
• Option D . The vitreous body is posterior to the lens and supports its position in the eye . However, the primary attachment of the vitreous body is not at the lens.
• Educational Objective
• The strongest attachment of the vitreous body is at the vitreous base , a band of collagen fibers at the ora serrata , crucial for maintaining the structural integrity of the eye.
explanation.ref: Pg 157/ Anatomy & Physiology of Eyeball /3 rd Edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Short Posterior Ciliary Artery is a Branch of?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . The Long Posterior Ciliary Arteries (there are usually two ) run forward between the sclera and the choroid to supply the ciliary body and the iris . They are distinct and separate from the Short Posterior Ciliary Arteries and do not give rise to them.
• Option B . The Anterior Ciliary Arteries supply the anterior segment of the eye , including the conjunctiva, sclera , and part of the iris . They are not the source of the Short Posterior Ciliary Arteries.
• Option D . Anterior Conjunctival Artery supply the conjunctiva and are not related to the supply of the posterior segment of the eye. They do not give rise to the Short Posterior Ciliary Arteries.
• Educational Objective:
• The Short Posterior Ciliary Arteries are branches of the Ophthalmic Artery .
• Pg 510/ Anatomy & Physiology of Eyeball /3 rd Edition/ AK KHURANA
explanation.ref: Pg 510/ Anatomy & Physiology of Eyeball /3 rd Edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The Type of Collagen that is not found in Vitreous is?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Type 2 Collagen is indeed found in the vitreous . It is the primary collagen type in the vitreous body , providing structural support and playing a crucial role in maintaining the vitreous gel structure .
• Option C . Type 9 collagen is also present in the vitreous . It is associated with type 2 collagen and plays a role in the formation of the collagen fibrils that are characteristic of the vitreous body .
• Option D . Type 5 collagen is found in the vitreous . This type of collagen is involved in the formation of the vitreous's fibrillar network, contributing to its structure and function .
• Educational Objective:
• Type 4 collagen , associated with basement membranes , is not found in the vitreous body of the eye, which primarily contains Type 2 , Type 9 , and Type 5 collagen .
• Pg 157/ Anatomy & Physiology of Eyeball /3 rd Edition/ AK KHURANA
explanation.ref: Pg 157/ Anatomy & Physiology of Eyeball /3 rd Edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 68-year-old woman with a history of neovascular age-related macular degeneration (nAMD) presents to the ophthalmology clinic for a follow-up visit. She has been receiving anti-VEGF therapy for the past year with some improvement in her vision. The ophthalmologist discusses the potential use of a newer anti-VEGF agent, brolucizumb. Which of the following is a potential advantage of brolucizumab compared to other anti-VEGF agents in the management of nAMD?
options: Absence of Risk for Geographic Atrophy

explanation.short: Ans. C) Longer Duration Between Injections.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . This is incorrect . Brolucizumab , has a longer interval between injections compared to other anti-VEGF agents, which is one of its potential advantages , thereby reducing frequency of injection .
• Option B . Brolucizumab has been associated with a higher incidence of intraocular inflammation , including rare cases of retinal vasculitis , compared to other anti-VEGF agents.
• Option D . This is incorrect . All anti-VEGF agents , including brolucizumab , do not eliminate the risk of geographic atrophy in patients with AMD .
• Educational Objective:
• Brolucizumab , a newer anti-VEGF agent for age-related macular degeneration , offers the potential advantage of a longer duration between injections compared to other anti-VEGF agents , which can improve treatment adherence and patient comfort . However, it is associated with a higher risk of intraocular inflammation .
explanation.ref: Tadayoni R, Sararols L, Weissgerber G, Verma R, Clemens A, Holz FG. Brolucizumab: A Newly Developed Anti-VEGF Molecule for the Treatment of Neovascular Age-Related Macular Degeneration. Ophthalmologica. 2021;244(2):93-101. doi: 10.1159/000513048. Epub 2020 Nov 16. PMID: 33197916.

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
The ability to obtain an impression of depth by the superimposition of 2 pictures of the same object is:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/05/10/c13.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Fusion – ability of 2 eyes to produce composite pictures from 2 similar pictures.
• Option C . Amblyopia- partial loss of sight in one or both eyes in the absence of ophthalmoscopic or objective signs.
• Educational Objective:
• Stereopsis refers to the ability to perceive depth by integrating the slightly different images from each eye , crucial for tasks that require depth perception.
explanation.ref: pg 408/ Anatomy and physiology of eye/ AK KHURANA/ 3 rd edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
A child was referred to an ophthalmologist in view of squint. The child’s eyes had the following appearance. What would be your likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/06/10/picture27.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Accommodative Squint : A form of strabismus usually presenting in childhood where the eyes turn inward (esotropia) as a result of excessive effort to accommodate or focus, often correctable with glasses .
• Option B. Essential Infantile Esotropia : A type of early-onset, constant inward turning of one or both eyes (esotropia) that typically appears in infants within the first year of life and often requires surgical correction .
• Option C. Congenital Ptosis : A condition present at birth where the upper eyelid droops over the eye due to underdevelopment or weakness of the eyelid-lifting muscle , potentially obstructing vision if severe.
• Educational Objective:
• The corneal light reflex is central in both eyes . The child has prominent epicanthal folds giving the appearance of esotropia – this is known as Pseudoesotropia.
explanation.ref: KHURANA COMPREHENSIVE OPHTHALMOLOGY 5 TH EDITION pg.no: 341

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
In Worth four dot test, the patient’s perception is shown in the image below. What would be the interpretation?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/06/21/915.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Right Suppression : A person with suppression of the right eye will see only two green dots because the right eye (covered by the red lens) suppresses the red dot, and the white dot appears green through the green lens of the left eye.
• Option B. Left Suppression : A person with suppression of the left eye will see three dots - two green and one red . The left eye (covered by the green lens) suppresses the green dots, and the white dot appears red through the red lens of the right eye.
• Option D. Diplopia : If the person has double vision (diplopia), they will see five dots - three green (two from the right eye and one white dot seen as green by the left eye) and two red (one from the left eye and one white dot seen as red by the right eye).
• Educational Objective:
• The Worth Four Dot Test results vary based on the patient's visual condition : right suppression shows two green dots , left suppression shows two green and one red dot , orthophoria shows two green , one red , and one combined dot , and diplopia shows three green and two red dots.
explanation.ref: KHURANA COMPREHENSIVE OPHTHALMOLOGY 5 TH EDITION PAGE 348

subject: Ophthalmology
question: The only extraocular muscle with sympathetic nerve supply is?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Superior Rectus : This muscle, responsible for elevating the eye , is innervated by the oculomotor nerve (Cranial Nerve III), not by sympathetic nerves.
• Option B. Levator Palpebrae Superioris (LPS) : The LPS raises the upper eyelid and is also innervated by the oculomotor nerve . It is not supplied by sympathetic nerves.
• Optiion C. Medial Rectus : This muscle, which moves the eye medially (towards the nose), is innervated by the oculomotor nerve as well . It does not receive sympathetic innervation.
• Educational Objective:
• The only extraocular muscle with sympathetic nerve supply is Müller’s Muscle , a smooth muscle in the upper eyelid that aids in eyelid elevation.
explanation.ref: pg 497/ Anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A patient is diagnosed with left oculomotor nerve palsy. Which of the following muscles is spared?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Inferior Rectus : This muscle is innervated by the oculomotor nerve . In the case of oculomotor nerve palsy , the inferior rectus would be affected , leading to impaired downward movement of the eye.
• Option C. Levator Palpebrae Superioris : This muscle, which elevates the upper eyelid , is also innervated by the oculomotor nerve . It would be affected in oculomotor nerve palsy , often resulting in ptosis (drooping of the eyelid).
• Option D. Superior Rectus : Also innervated by the oculomotor nerve , the superior rectus muscle is responsible for elevating the eye . It would be impaired in oculomotor nerve palsy.
• Educational Objective:
• In a case of oculomotor nerve palsy , the superior oblique muscle , innervated by the trochlear nerve , is spared.
explanation.ref: pg 359/ / Anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A patient is diagnosed with left oculomotor nerve palsy. Which of the following movements is spared?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Depression : The primary muscle for depression of the eye is the inferior rectus , which is innervated by the oculomotor nerve . This movement would be affected in oculomotor nerve palsy.
• Option C . Adduction : This movement is controlled by the medial rectus muscle , which is also innervated by the oculomotor nerve . Thus, adduction is impaired in oculomotor nerve palsy.
• Option D . Elevation : The superior rectus , which is responsible for elevation of the eye, is innervated by the oculomotor nerve. This movement would be compromised in oculomotor nerve palsy.
• Educational Objective:
• In oculomotor nerve palsy, intorsion, controlled by the superior oblique muscle and innervated by the trochlear nerve , is the movement that is spared.

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 45-year-old male presents with horizontal double vision diplopia and limited abduction of the right eye, indicating the weakness of Lateral Rectus muscle of the right eye. Which cranial nerve supplies the involved muscle?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Right Oculomotor Nerve (Cranial Nerve III): This nerve innervates most of the extraocular muscles except for the lateral rectus and superior oblique muscles . Since the lateral rectus is involved, the right oculomotor nerve is not the affected nerve in this scenario.
• Option C . Left Oculomotor Nerve (Cranial Nerve III): This nerve controls most of the extraocular muscles of the left eye . Since the issue is with the right eye's lateral rectus muscle, this nerve is not involved in this case.
• Option D . Left Abducent Nerve (Cranial Nerve VI):  The lateral rectus muscle of the left eye is innervated by the left abducent nerve .
• Educational Objective:
• In a case presenting with horizontal diplopia and limited abduction of the right eye , the right abducent nerve (Cranial Nerve VI), which innervates the lateral rectus muscle , is implicated.
explanation.ref: pg 358/ Anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Out of all the extraocular muscles, which one is the longest?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Superior Rectus : This muscle originates from the common tendinous ring at the back of the orbit and inserts into the superior surface of the eyeball . It primarily causes elevation of the eye .
• Option C. Medial Rectus : Originating from the common tendinous ring , this muscle attaches to the medial surface of the eyeball . It primarily moves the eye medially.
• Option D. Inferior Rectus : This muscle also starts from the common tendinous ring and attaches to the inferior surface of the eyeball . Its main action is the depression of the eye .
• Educational Objective:
• The superior oblique is the longest extraocular muscle , primarily responsible for intorsion and depression of the eye , particularly when the eye is adducted.
explanation.ref: pg 359/ / anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Which extra ocular muscle is the shortest?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Superior Rectus : This muscle, originating from the common tendinous ring , is not the shortest. It primarily elevates the eye.
• Option B. Medial Rectus : Also arising from the common tendinous ring , this muscle is responsible for adducting the eye . It is longer compared to the inferior oblique.
• Option D. Inferior Rectus : Originating from the common tendinous ring , this muscle primarily depresses the eye . It is not the shortest among the extraocular muscles.
• Educational Objective:
• The inferior oblique muscle is the shortest of the extraocular muscles , primarily responsible for extorsion, elevation, and abduction of the eye .
explanation.ref: pg 360/ anatomy & physiology of eye ball /3 rd edition/ AK KHURANA

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Amongst the following cranial nerves, which nerve supplies the longest extraocular muscle?
options: Superior Division of the Oculomotor Nerve

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Superior Division of the Oculomotor Nerve (Cranial Nerve III) : This division supplies the superior rectus and levator palpebrae superioris muscles . The superior rectus elevates the eye, while the levator palpebrae superioris raises the upper eyelid.
• Option B. Abducent Nerve (Cranial Nerve VI) : This nerve innervates the lateral rectus muscle , which abducts the eye (moves it away from the nose). It does not supply the superior oblique muscle.
• Option D. Inferior Division of the Oculomotor Nerve (Cranial Nerve III) : This division supplies the medial rectus, inferior rectus, and inferior oblique muscles . These muscles are involved in moving the eye medially, downwards, and in extorsion and elevation (inferior oblique), respectively.
• Educational Objective:
• The trochlear nerve (Cranial Nerve IV) supplies the superior oblique muscle , which is the longest extraocular muscle , mainly involved in intorsion and depression of the eye when adducted.

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 5-month-old infant with cerebral palsy is brought in by her mother due to a recent onset of eye deviation. The mother reports that the deviation is more noticeable when the child is focusing on objects. The child has no history of trauma, infection, and is up-to-date with vaccinations. During the physical examination, you note that the child's eyes do not align properly, especially in lateral gaze. Based on this presentation, which of the following is the most likely characteristic of this child's strabismus?
options: Onset is within 2 years of age
Cross fixation in side gaze noted

explanation.short: Ans. C) Cross fixation in side gaze noted
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Small Angle of Squint: Early onset esotropia often presents with a large angle of squint rather than a small one.
• Option B. Onset is Within 2 Years of Age: Early onset esotropia, as the name suggests, typically begins in infancy or early childhood. The most common form , congenital or infantile esotropia , often appears before the child reaches 6 months of age.
• Option D. Presence of Significant Refractive Error: While refractive errors can be associated with some forms of strabismus , early onset esotropia is not typically characterized by significant refractive errors.
• Educational Objective:
• Recognize that early onset esotropia typically presents within the first two years of life , often with large-angle squint and cross fixation behavior , but not necessarily with significant refractive errors.
explanation.ref: KANSKI 9 TH EDITION pg.no: 728

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Normal AC/A ratio is?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. 2∆-6∆: This range is slightly lower than the typical normal AC/A ratio . It would suggest less convergence per diopter of accommodation, which might be seen in cases of under-convergence.
• Option C. 4∆-8∆: This range is on the higher side of the normal AC/A ratio . A higher ratio indicates a greater amount of convergence for each diopter of accommodation, which might be observed in cases of over-convergence.
• Option D. 6∆-8∆: This range is usually considered higher than the normal AC/A ratio , indicating excessive convergence in response to accommodation.
• Educational Objective:
• The normal AC/A ratio , indicative of the relationship between accommodative convergence and accommodation , is typically in the range of 3∆ to 5∆.
explanation.ref: KANSKI 9 TH EDITION PAGE 702

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Where does the marked muscle originate from?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/06/21/92.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Zygomatic bone : The zygomatic bone is a facial bone that forms the prominence of the cheek. It does not serve as an origin point for any of the extraocular muscles.
• Option C. Body of sphenoid : None of the extraocular muscles originate from the body of sphenoid .
• Option D. Orbital plate of maxilla : The orbital plate of the maxilla forms a large part of the floor of the orbit . However, it is not the origin point for any extraocular muscle.
• Educational Objective:
• The lateral rectus muscle originates from the Annulus of Zinn .
explanation.ref: KHURANA COMPREHENSIVE OPHTHALMOLOGY 5 TH EDITION pg.no 333 and KANSKI 9 TH EDITION pg.no: 700

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Based on the test finding given below, what would the diagnosis be?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/06/21/93.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Findings : In orthophoria, the eyes are properly aligned with each other . When using the Maddox Rod, the patient will see the point light and the line in alignment with each other.
• Interpretation : The alignment of the line with the light indicates that there is no latent deviation of the eyes , and the ocular alignment is normal.
• Findings : In exophoria, the eyes tend to diverge away from each other . When the Maddox Rod is placed in front of right eye, the patient with exophoria will see the line displaced to the right of the point light
• Interpretation : The displacement of the line to the right indicates a tendency for the covered eye to turn outward , characteristic of exophoria.
• Findings : In hypophoria, there is a tendency for one eye to drift downward . When the Maddox Rod is placed horizontally in front of one eye, the patient with hypophoria will see the line displaced either above or below the light, depending on which eye is covered and the direction of the deviation.
• Interpretation: The vertical displacement of the line indicates a tendency for the covered eye to deviate downward , characteristic of hypophoria.
• Educational Objective:
• In the Maddox rod test , the point source of light is seen by the uncovered eye , while the covered eye sees the line . In esotropia, the line is displaced to left of the dot, when right eye is covered and vice-versa when left eye is covered.
explanation.ref: PARSON’S DISEASES OF EYE PG.NO: 423

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 4-year-old child with squint was undergoing corrective surgery. Heart rate recorded prior to initiation was 100 bpm. After anaesthesia and start of surgery, heart rate reduced to 50 bpm. How to intervene further?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Increase the dose of anesthesia :
• Increasing the dose of anesthesia in the case of bradycardia is generally not advisable . Many anesthetics can depress myocardial function and further reduce heart rate. This action could exacerbate the situation rather than alleviate it.
• Option B. Administer beta blockers :
• Beta blockers are contraindicated in this situation. They are typically used to decrease heart rate and blood pressure. Administering beta blockers to a patient who is already experiencing bradycardia could be dangerous, as it could further lower the heart rate, potentially leading to hemodynamic instability.
• Option C . Cessation of surgery :
• While it is important to always be prepared to stop surgery if the patient's condition deteriorates significantly, immediate cessation of surgery for bradycardia is not always necessary. The cause of the bradycardia should be identified and treated accordingly. If the bradycardia is directly related to the surgical procedure or anesthesia, then pausing might be considered while corrective measures are taken.
• Educational Objective:
• In the case of a significant reduction in heart rate to 50 bpm in a child during surgery, the appropriate intervention is to administer Inj. Atropine. This acts to increase heart rate by antagonizing the vagal effects on the heart , and is a critical response in managing oculocardiac reflex during squint surgery.
explanation.ref: AAO SECTION:6 – PAEDIATRIC OPHTHALMOLOGY AND STRABISMUS. PG.NO: 174

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
What is the given instrument used for?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/06/10/picture10_mV3Ci8W.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. D) Measurement of angle of deviation
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Determination of keratometry reading: Keratometer
• Option B Field analysis: Ophthalmic Perimeter
• Option C. Measurement of axial length: Optical biometer and ultrasound biometer.
• Educational Objective:
• The Synoptophore is used for the measurement of the angle of deviation in strabismus , as well as for the diagnosis and treatment of binocular vision anomalies .
explanation.ref: Parson’s diseases of the eye 22 nd edition – Pg 421

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Based on the evaluation of a patient with strabismus, a 15º primary deviation and 30º secondary deviation were recorded. What type of strabismus does this indicate?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Concomitant Squint :
• In concomitant squint (also known as non-paralytic or comitant squint), the degree of deviation remains constant regardless of the direction of gaze . It means that the angle of misalignment does not change when the patient looks in different directions.
• Primary deviation = Secondary deviation.
• Option B. Accommodative Esotropia :
• Accommodative esotropia is a type of concomitant squint where the eyes turn inward . It is often related to uncorrected hyperopia and the eye's effort to accommodate leads to convergence and thus inward turning of the eyes . Like other forms of concomitant squint, the primary and secondary deviations in accommodative esotropia are equal.
• Option D. Convergence Insufficiency :
• Convergence insufficiency is a condition where the eyes do not work together well while focusing on a near object . The eyes have difficulty converging adequately for near work , leading to symptoms like eyestrain and blurring - asthenopia. It does not typically present with a difference between primary and secondary deviations.
• Educational Objective:
• In a case with a 15º primary deviation and a 30º secondary deviation in strabismus , the diagnosis is indicative of a paralytic squint . This finding is characteristic of paralytic strabismus , where the secondary deviation (when the affected eye is fixating) is greater than the primary deviation (when the unaffected eye is fixating), reflecting the impact of muscle weakness or paralysis.
explanation.ref: KHURANA COMPREHENSIVE OPHTHALMOLOGY 5 TH EDITION pg.no 351

subject: Ophthalmology
question: When we fix our visual attention on an object of regard, the image is formed on the fovea of both our eyes separately; but we perceive a single image. Which of the following is not a component of this state of binocular single vision?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Fusion is a key component of binocular single vision . It is the process by which the brain combines the two slightly different images received from each eye into a single , unified image. This is crucial for achieving binocular single vision.
• Option B . Simultaneous Perception :
• Simultaneous perception is also an essential component of binocular single vision . It refers to the ability of the visual system to perceive images presented to each eye simultaneously, which is a prerequisite for fusion to occur.
• Stereopsis is the perception of depth that arises from the brain's processing of the slightly different images from each eye . It is a higher-level outcome of binocular single vision, but not a fundamental component of achieving binocular single vision itself. Stereopsis is dependent on the successful integration of the other components (fusion and simultaneous perception).
• Educational Objective:
• In the context of binocular single vision , fixation is not a component specific to this state.
explanation.ref: KHURANA COMPREHENSIVE OPHTHALMOLOGY 5 TH EDITION pg.no 339

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Match the following muscles with their primary actions:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/08/05/whatsapp-image-2023-06-12-at-19012101101087.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Superior and Inferior Rectii and Oculii have Primary and Subsidiary actions . Medial and Lateral Rectii have only primary action .
explanation.ref: PARSON’S DISEASES OF THE EYE PG.NO: 405

subject: Ophthalmology
question: What is the nerve supply to the longest extraocular muscle?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Oculomotor Nerve (CN III) : The oculomotor nerve innervates most of the extraocular muscles , including the superior, inferior, and medial recti ; the inferior oblique; and the levator palpebrae superioris . However, it does not innervate the superior oblique muscle.
• Option C. Trigeminal Nerve (CN V) : The trigeminal nerve is the main sensory nerve of the face and does not innervate any of the extraocular muscles. It has three branches (ophthalmic, maxillary, and mandibular) that provide sensory innervation to different regions of the face.
• Option D. Abducens Nerve (CN VI) : The abducens nerve innervates the lateral rectus muscle , which is responsible for abducting the eye (moving it outward). It does not innervate the superior oblique muscle.
• Educational Objective:
• The nerve supply to the superior oblique muscle , the longest extraocular muscle , is the trochlear nerve (cranial nerve IV).
explanation.ref: KHURANA COMPREHENSIVE OPHTHALMOLOGY 5 TH EDITION pg.no 333

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A patient presented with deviation of right eye in primary gaze. On testing, the corneal reflex was noted to be at the nasal limbus. The deviation in prism diopter is?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. 45∆ esotropia – Indicates that the Hirschberg reflex should lie between pupil and limbus.
• Option C. 30∆ esotropia – Indicates that the Hirschberg reflex should lie at the pupillary border .
• Option D. 60∆ exotropia - Indicates that the Hirschberg reflex should lie between pupil and limbus .
• Educational Objective:
• The reflection of light is at the nasal (inner) limbus of the right eye , which means it is 45 degree or 90∆ exotropia.
explanation.ref: KHURANA COMPREHENSIVE OPHTHALMOLOGY 5 TH EDITION pg.no 347

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Identify the condition:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/06/10/picture14_7Ek6d4w.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Möbius Syndrome : A rare congenital disorder characterized by facial paralysis and the inability to move the eyes laterally due to cranial nerve VI and VII dysfunctions.
• Option B. Brown Syndrome : A condition marked by defects in the superior oblique tendon sheath , leading to restriction in the upward movement of the eye , particularly when it is adducted.
• Option D. Marcus Gunn Syndrome : Also known as Marcus Gunn Jaw-Winking Phenomenon , it is a rare congenital condition where the upper eyelid rises abnormally when the jaw moves , due to aberrant nerve connections.
• Educational Objective:
• Retraction of globe on attempted horizontal eye movements with restriction of adduction and abduction is suggestive of Duane’s syndrome.
explanation.ref: KANSKI 9 TH EDITION PG.NO: 736

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A child was brought to the ophthalmologist with complaint of inability to look up. On examination it is noticed that on attempted upgaze, there are convergence-like movements accompanied by retraction of the globe. A lesion involving the vertical gaze nucleus is suspected. Which of the following is the nucleus for upward gaze?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Interstitial nucleus of Cajal
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Cuneiform Nucleus : The cuneiform nucleus is part of the midbrain but is not specifically associated with control of upward gaze . It is involved in various functions including modulating the pain response and controlling certain aspects of autonomic function.
• Option B. Paramedian Pontine Reticular Formation (PPRF) : The PPRF is crucial for horizontal eye movements, particularly for initiating rapid eye movements known as saccades. It is located in the pons and is not directly responsible for upward gaze.
• Optin D. Edinger-Westphal Nucleus : The Edinger-Westphal nucleus is part of the oculomotor nuclear complex and is primarily associated with the parasympathetic control of the eye , contributing to pupillary constriction and accommodation, rather than eye movements.
• Educational Objective:
• The Interstitial Nucleus of Cajal , located in the midbrain , is responsible for the control of upward gaze .
explanation.ref: PARSON’S DISEASES OF EYE PG.NO: 411

subject: Ophthalmology
question: All are true statements regarding the sequelae of extraocular muscle palsy except:

explanation.short: Ans. B) Overaction of Contralateral antagonist
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Contracture of Direct Antagonist : This statement is true . In cases of chronic extraocular muscle palsy, the direct antagonist can undergo contracture due to prolonged unopposed action.
• Option C . Overaction of Direct Antagonist : This statement is true . The direct antagonist (the muscle that opposes the action of the paralyzed muscle in the same eye) overacts in response to muscle palsy because it is no longer being counteracted by the paralyzed muscle.
• Option D. Inhibitional Palsy of Contralateral Antagonist : This statement is also true . Inhibitional palsy of the contralateral antagonist is a phenomenon observed in extraocular muscle palsy, where the antagonist of the paralyzed muscle in the opposite eye shows underaction.
• Due to unopposed overaction of ipsilateral antagonist, there is reduced innervation of its yolk muscle, i.e. the contralateral antagonist of the paralysed muscle, following Hering’s law. This leads to a pseudoparesis of the contralateral antagonist in the early stages.
• Educational Objective:
• The incorrect statement regarding the sequelae of extraocular muscle palsy is the overaction of the contralateral antagonist . In cases of extraocular muscle palsy , common sequelae include contracture of the direct antagonist , overaction of the direct antagonist, and inhibitional palsy of the contralateral antagonist.
explanation.ref: PARSON’S DISEASES OF EYE PG.NO 407

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A patient underwent recession of medial rectus for esotropia. After the surgery, a small angle residual squint was noted. A patient developed esotropia secondary to CN6 palsy. The resultant dipopia was hindring day-to-day activities. An injection was given to medial rectus muscle to correct the squint. Identify the drug used:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Edrophonium : This is a cholinesterase inhibitor commonly used in the diagnosis of myasthenia gravis . It has no role in the treatment of squint.
• Option B. Atropine : An anticholinergic drug , used in ophthalmology primarily to dilate pupils and paralyze accommodation . It is not used via injection for squint correction.
• Option D. Adrenaline : Primarily used in emergency situations for its vasoconstrictive and bronchodilating effects , adrenaline has no therapeutic role in the direct treatment of squint.
• Educational Objective:
• The drug used for injection into the medial rectus muscle to correct squint is Botulinum toxin , as it temporarily paralyzes the muscle , aiding in the realignment of the eyes.
explanation.ref: KANSKI 9 TH EDITION PAGE 743

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Match the following muscles with their site of insertion:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/08/07/whatsapp-image-2023-06-12-at-19012101101094.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. C) A-iii, B-i, C-ii, D- iv
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• This picture shows the insertion of all four recti muscles to the sclera and their distance from the limbus . The insertions are located progressively further away from the limbus in a spiral pattern .
• Educational Objective:
• This The insertion of the rectii forms an imaginary line known as THE SPIRAL OF TILLAUX .
explanation.ref: KANSKI 9 TH EDITION PAGE 700

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Identify the yoke muscles in this gaze:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/06/21/910.jpg]
options: Right lateral rectus and left medial rectus
Right lateral rectus and right medial rectus
Left lateral rectus and left medial rectus
Left lateral rectus and right medial rectus

explanation.short: Ans. A) Right lateral rectus and left medial rectus
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Right lateral rectus and right medial rectus : These muscles work antagonistically in the same eye (right eye), so they are not yoke muscles for dextroversion .
• Option C. Left lateral rectus and left medial rectus : These are muscles in the same eye (left eye) and work antagonistically , not as yoke muscles.
• Option D. Left lateral rectus and right medial rectus : These muscles would be used in laevoversion (leftward gaze), not dextroversion.
• Educational Objective:
• The yoke muscles for dextroversion (rightward gaze) are the right lateral rectus and the left medial rectus . These muscles work together to move the eyes rightward.
explanation.ref: Parson’s diseases of the eye page 407

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Identify the condition:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/06/10/picture20.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Möbius syndrome : Characterized by congenital facial paralysis and abducens nerve palsy , leading to limited facial expression and lateral gaze palsy.
• Option C . Marcus Gunn syndrome (Marcus Gunn Jaw-Winking Phenomenon): Involves an abnormal connection between the muscles of jaw movement and the eyelid , causing the upper eyelid to elevate involuntarily during jaw movement.
• Option D. Duane’s syndrome : Marked by limited horizontal eye movement , often with retraction of the eyeball into the socket and narrowing of the eyelid opening when trying to look inward or outward.
• Educational Objective:
• Brown syndrome presents with inability to elevate the affected eye in adduction due to a restriction in the superior oblique tendon .
explanation.ref: KHURANA COMPREHENSIVE OPHTHALMOLOGY 5 TH EDITION pg.no 355

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 25-year-old male patient came with complaint of inability to look up following a hit to the eye with a tennis ball. On examination, the forced duction test is positive. What is the muscle abnormality in this case?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Superior rectus entrapment: This muscle is responsible for upward gaze , but entrapment is less likely because it is not commonly entrapped following orbital trauma.
• Option B. Superior rectus palsy : Palsy would imply a nerve dysfunction , which is less likely given the positive forced duction test indicating a mechanical restriction.
• Option D. Inferior rectus palsy : Similar to superior rectus palsy , this would suggest nerve dysfunction, not a mechanical restriction.
• Educational Objective:
• The likely muscle abnormality in a patient with trauma to the eye , inability to look up , and a positive forced duction test is entrapment of the inferior rectus muscle .
explanation.ref: KHURANA COMPREHENSIVE OPHTHALMOLOGY 5 TH EDITION pg.no 355

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Identify the test:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/06/10/picture21.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Lees screen : A test for binocular vision anomalies , where the patient views a pattern through polarizing glasses , allowing the detection of suppression or abnormal retinal correspondence.
• Option C. Lang chart : A stereo test used , often in children , to detect amblyopia and strabismus , involving a card with hidden 3D images that can only be perceived correctly with normal binocular vision .
• Option D. Frisby test : A simple stereopsis test that involves viewing a series of transparent plates with embedded patterns , used to assess depth perception in patients , particularly for diagnosing amblyopia.
• Educational Objective:
• Hess chart is used in identifying the paralyzed muscle and its sequelae .
explanation.ref: KHURANA COMPREHENSIVE OPHTHALMOLOGY 5 TH EDITION PG.NO 353

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Which of the following statements is true about Möbius syndrome?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Presence of vertical gaze palsy : Möbius syndrome primarily involves horizontal gaze palsy due to abducens (6th cranial nerve) and facial (7th cranial nerve) nerve paralysis . Vertical gaze palsy is not a characteristic feature.
• Option B. Acquired progressive condition : Möbius syndrome is a congenital, non-progressive condition. It is present from birth and does not worsen over time.
• Option D. Only 6 th nerve involvement : While the 6 th cranial nerve (abducens nerve) is involved in Möbius syndrome , it is not the only nerve affected . The 7 th cranial nerve (facial nerve) is also typically involved, leading to facial muscle weakness or paralysis.
• Educational Objective:
• Möbius syndrome is characterized by its bilateral presentation, involving paralysis of the 6 th (abducens) and 7 th (facial) cranial nerves , leading to facial weakness and horizontal gaze palsy ; it is a congenital and non-progressive condition.
explanation.ref: KANSKI 9 TH EDITION pg.no: 736

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
The inclination of lenses in each eye is:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/06/21/picture4.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• The test shown is Bagolini test using Bagolini striated glasses . It is done for detecting Binocular single vision (BSV), Abnormal retinal correspondence (ARC) or suppression. Each lens has fine striations that convert a point source of light into a line . The two lenses are placed at 45° and 135° in front of each eye and the patient is made to fixate on a focal light source.
• Educational Objective:
• In Bagolinni’s striated glasses the glasses are aligned as Left eye 45 º and Right Eye 135°.
explanation.ref: KANSKI 9 TH EDITION PG.NO 713

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 35-year-old female patient came to the OPD with a complaint of double vision since few months which was worse for distant objects. The patient gave a history of deviation of one eye since childhood. On examination,right esotropia was noted and after further evaluation, a diagnosis of right lateral rectus palsy was made. What is the ideal surgical procedure for this patient?
options: Recession of right lateral rectus and medial rectus
Resection of right lateral rectus and medial rectus
Resection of right lateral rectus and recession of right medial rectus
Recession of right lateral rectus and resection of right medial rectus

explanation.short: Ans. C) Resection of right lateral rectus and recession of right medial rectus
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Recession of right lateral rectus and medial rectus: This would not be ideal for lateral rectus palsy . Recession (weakening) of the already palsied lateral rectus would exacerbate the problem , and recessing the medial rectus of the same eye could lead to significant imbalance.
• Option B. Resection of right lateral rectus and medial rectus : Resecting (shortening/strengthening) the palsied lateral rectus might be considered , but resecting the medial rectus in the same eye typically would not be indicated as it would further imbalance the eye.
• Option D. Recession of right lateral rectus and resection of right medial rectus : Recession (weakening) of a palsied muscle and resection (strengthening) of its antagonist will worsen the squint and the diplopia in this case.
• Educational Objective:
• For a patient with right lateral rectus palsy , the ideal surgical intervention typically involves resecting (strengthening) the weak right lateral rectus and recessing (weakening) the opposing right medial rectus to restore ocular balance and alignment .
explanation.ref: KHURANA COMPREHENSIVE OPHTHALMOLOGY 5 TH EDITION pg.no: 354

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Identify the pattern of deviation:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/06/10/picture24.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. V pattern esotropia : A type of strabismus where the inward deviation of the eyes (esotropia) increases when looking upwards as compared to looking downwards.
• Option C. A pattern exotropia: A form of strabismus where the outward turning of the eyes (exotropia) becomes more pronounced when the eyes are directed downwards compared to upwards.
• Option D. V pattern exotropia : A strabismus condition characterized by an increase in the outward deviation of the eyes (exotropia) when looking upwards as compared to looking downwards.
• Educational Objective:
• “A” pattern esotropia is a strabismic condition where the esotropia is more pronounced when the eyes are directed downwards compared to upwards.
explanation.ref: KHURANA COMPREHENSIVE OPHTHALMOLOGY 5 TH EDITION pg.no: 355

subject: Ophthalmology
question: All are true regarding penalization except:
options: It is a treatment option for amblyopia

explanation.short: Ans. B) Done in squinting eye
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Useful in very young children : This is true . Penalization is often used in young children as a treatment for amblyopia, especially when patching therapy is not feasible or not tolerated.
• Option C. This procedure uses atropine: True . Atropine is used in penalization therapy to blur the vision in the non-amblyopic eye by dilating the pupil and paralyzing the accommodation, thereby forcing the amblyopic eye to work harder and improve.
• Option D. It is a treatment option for amblyopia: True . Penalization is a well-recognized treatment option for amblyopia, particularly in cases where traditional patching is not suitable or effective.
• Educational Objective:
• Penalization therapy for amblyopia involves applying atropine to the non-amblyopic (non-squinting) eye to blur its vision and stimulate use of the amblyopic (squinting) eye , making it an effective treatment in very young children.
explanation.ref: KANSKI 9 TH EDITION PG.NO 708

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A patient with reading difficulty presented to ophthalmology OPD. On evaluation, the patient was found to have 6PD of esotropia with eccentric fixation. Which of the following tests would you suggest?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. 2PD test : Such a test is not described.
• Option C . 6PD test : Such a test is not described.
• Option D. 3PD test : Such a test is not described.
• Educational Objective:
• A 4 PD test is performed on patients suspected to have small angle deviations of less than 10 prism dioptres , a microtropia , that may or may not have been observed on cover test because of subtle eye movements. The test determines whether the patient has bifoveal fixation or monofixation despite their eyes seeming straight.
explanation.ref: KANSKI 9 TH EDITION PG.NO 713 & 732

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Identify the given scenario:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/06/10/picture25.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Acquired Skew Deviation : A neurological disorder characterized by vertical misalignment of the eyes due to disruption of vestibular or brainstem pathways, often associated with brain lesions or injuries.
• Option B. Superior Oblique Palsy : A condition caused by weakness or paralysis of the superior oblique muscle , leading to vertical diplopia (double vision), head tilt, and difficulty with downward gaze, especially when looking towards the nose.
• Option D. Superior Oblique Overaction: A condition where the superior oblique muscle is overactive , leading to excessive inward and downward movement of the eye , often causing a V-pattern strabismus and tilting of images.
• Educational Objective:
• Dissociated Vertical Deviation (DVD) is a unique type of strabismus where one eye drifts upward when the other eye is fixating , often seen in patients with a history of congenital or early-onset strabismus.
explanation.ref: AAO PAEDIATRIC OPHTHALMOLOGY AND STRABISMUS PAGE 139

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Identify the likely muscle involvement from this picture:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/06/21/914.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. B) Right lateral rectus palsy
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Right medial rectus palsy.
• Option C . Left superior oblique palsy
• Option D. Left inferior oblique palsy
• Educational Objective:
• In diplopia charting , uncrossed separation of images with maximum separation on right gaze suggestive of right lateral rectus palsy .
explanation.ref: KHURANA COMPREHENSIVE OPHTHALMOLOGY 5 TH EDITION pg no: 353

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
The following images are taken after asking the patient to look up and right, straight up and up and left. Identify the condition:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/06/10/picture28.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Superior Oblique Palsy : A condition characterized by weakness or paralysis of the superior oblique muscle , leading to vertical and torsional double vision , often accompanied by a compensatory head tilt.
• Option B. Möbius Syndrome : A rare congenital disorder characterized by facial paralysis and abducens nerve palsy , resulting in limited facial expression and lateral gaze palsy.
• Option D. Brown Syndrome : A disorder characterized by a restriction in the movement of the superior oblique tendon , resulting in difficulty elevating the affected eye , particularly in adduction.
• Educational Objective:
• Double Elevator Palsy is rare condition where there is a limitation of elevation in one eye , thought to be due to a palsy of both the superior rectus and the inferior oblique muscles .
explanation.ref: KHURANA COMPREHENSIVE OPHTHALMOLOGY 5 TH EDITION pg no: 353

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 14-year-old boy presented with complaints of defective vision. His best corrected visual acuity in right eye was 6/6 and in left eye was 6/60. Retinoscopy revealed +1D in right eye and +7D in left eye. Ocular examination was within normal limits. The probable diagnosis would be?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Malingering: This would imply the patient is feigning or exaggerating the extent of visual impairment . However, the significant difference in refractive error between the eyes and the corresponding difference in visual acuity suggest a genuine visual problem, making malingering unlikely.
• Option C. Retrobulbar Neuritis : This condition typically presents with pain on eye movement and a rapid reduction in visual acuity , often associated with color vision impairment . There's no indication of these symptoms from the given information.
• Option D. Refractive Accommodative Esotropia : This condition is characterized by esotropia (inward turning of the eye) that is caused primarily by uncorrected hyperopia (farsightedness). While the patient has hyperopia in the left eye, there's no mention of esotropia.
• Educational Objective:
• The most probable diagnosis in this case is amblyopia , likely anisometropic amblyopia , due to a significant uncorrected refractive difference between the two eyes during a critical period of visual development.
explanation.ref: KHURANA COMPREHENSIVE OPHTHALMOLOGY 5 TH EDITION pg.no: 339

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Which of the following types of amblyopia is associated with worst prognosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Anisometropic Amblyopia : This results from unequal refractive errors in the two eyes . Its prognosis is generally good with appropriate corrective lenses and amblyopia therapy, especially if treated early.
• Option B. Strabismic Amblyopia : Caused by misalignment of the eyes (strabismus), this type of amblyopia has a variable prognosis depending on the severity and treatment of the strabismus.
• Option D. Meridional Amblyopia: Associated with uncorrected astigmatism, this type of amblyopia has a generally good prognosis with appropriate corrective lenses and amblyopia therapy.
• Educational Objective:
• Stimulus deprivation amblyopia has the worst prognosis among the types of amblyopia listed, due to the profound impact of visual deprivation during the critical developmental period of the visual system.
explanation.ref: AAO PAEDIATRIC OPHTHALMOLOGY AND STRABISMUS PAGE 55

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A patient visits the ophthalmologist with a complaint of inward deviation of the right eye, known as esodeviation. After a thorough evaluation, the ophthalmologist determines that a prism prescription would be beneficial for the patient. What is the ideal orientation or direction in which the prism should be positioned?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Base up : Positioning the prism with the base upwards is used to correct hypotropia, where one eye is positioned lower than the other. The light path is deviated upwards, helping to align the lower eye with the other.
• Option B. Base down : This orientation is used for hypertropia , where one eye is higher than the other. The prism base is positioned downwards, deviating the light path downwards to align the higher eye with the fellow eye.
• Option D. Base in : The base is positioned towards the nose (medially). This is the correct orientation for correcting exotropia.
• Educational Objective:
• For the correction of esotropia , the prism should be positioned with base-out to realign the inward-turned eye outward.
explanation.ref: Clinical optics by Elkington page 51

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
What is the given test used in assessment of?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/04/29/31.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Contrast Sensitivity: This test measures the ability to discern between finer and finer increments of light versus dark (contrast), often using a chart with varying shades of gray or with spatial frequency patterns. Eg. Pelli Robson Chart.
• Option B. Visual Acuity in Children : Various age-appropriate methods are used , such as LEA symbols, Snellen's E chart, or picture chart.
• Option D. Malingering : Various techniques are employed , such as the fogging test or the use of duochrome test , to detect exaggeration or feigning of poor vision, often by presenting visual stimuli that are impossible to misinterpret if vision is normal.
• Educational Objective:
• Assessed using tests like the Titmus Fly test or Random Dot Stereogram , which require the patient to wear special glasses and identify 3D shapes from 2D patterns , evaluating depth perception and binocular vision.
explanation.ref: Clinical optics by Elkington Page: 19

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Match the following:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/08/screenshot-2024-03-08-104926.jpg]
options: A - iii, B - i, C - iv, D - ii
A - i, B - iii, C - iv, D - ii
A - ii, B - i, C - iii, D - iv
A - iv, B - ii, C - i, D - iii

explanation.short: Ans. A) A - iii, B - i, C - iv, D - ii
explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• As shown in the image, iris nodules are seen in various pathologies.
• KANSKI 9 TH EDITION
explanation.ref: KANSKI 9 TH EDITION

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 42-year-old female, presented with sudden onset eye pain, redness, and light sensitivity in her left eye. Examination reveals reduced IOP and anterior chamber inflammation. She is diagnosed with acute iridocyclitis. What is the probable cause of reduced IOP?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Inflammation : does not directly affect the intraocular pressure .
• Option C. Posterior synechiae : Partial occlusion of pupil called seclusio papillae does not intraocular pressure. 360 degrees posterior synechiae cause mechanical pupillary block and raise intraocular pressure due to resultant appositional angle closure due to formation of iris bombae.
• Option D. Occlusion of trabecular meshwork : Can occur due to clogging of the trabecular meshwork by the intraocular inflammatory cells , leading to secondary open angle glaucoma.
• Educational Objective:
• Intraocular pressure can be low in cases of intraocular inflammation due to ciliary shock .
• Khurana Comprehensive Ophthalmology 5 th edition
explanation.ref: Khurana Comprehensive Ophthalmology 5 th edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: What clinical finding can be demonstrated using the Tyndall effect?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Aqueous cells : Early sign of iridocyclitis . Cells counted in an oblique slit-lamp beam.
• Option B. Keratic precipitates: Proteinaceous cellular deposits occurring at the back of cornea . Mostly, these are arranged in a triangular fashion occupying the centre and inferior part of cornea , called Arlt’s triangle due to convection currents in the aqueous humour.
• Option C. Hypopyon : A hypopyon is the accumulation of white blood cells that form a whitish layer of fluid in the lower portion of the eye's anterior chamber. It may be mobile (in uveitis and bacterial keratitis) or immobile (in fungal keratitis).
• Educational Objective:
• Students must be able to identify the signs of intraocular inflammation
explanation.ref: Khurana Comprehensive Ophthalmology 5 th edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Given below are statements regarding panophthalmitis, All are true except?
options: Untreated cases can progress to cavernous sinus thrombosis
It involves all three coats of the eyeball
Enucleation is the treatment of choice
Presents with painful limitation of movements

explanation.short: Ans. C) Enucleation is the treatment of choice
explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Enucleation in contraindicated in panophthalmitis due to risk of intracranial spread of infection via dura mater of the severed optic nerve .
explanation.ref: Khurana Comprehensive Ophthalmology 5 th edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
The following finding is noted in a patient. What is the reason behind this pattern of deposition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/08/picture7_ETl92bL.jpg]
options: Increased affinity to endothelium in inferior cornea
Lipophilic nature of the suspended particles

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Increased affinity of endothelium :  The nutrition of the corneal endothelium becomes affected in uveitis so that the cells become sticky and may desquamate in places. The exudates tend to stick there, forming keratic precipitates.
• Educational Objective:
• Convection currents in the eye cause deposition of keratic precipitates in an Arlt’s triangle configuration .
• Khurana Comprehensive Ophthalmology 5 th edition
explanation.ref: Khurana Comprehensive Ophthalmology 5 th edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 29-year-old female patient, who is a known case of uveitis, came to the OPD with complaints of headache and ocular pain. After thorough examination, she was diagnosed with secondary open angle glaucoma. Which of the following drugs is contraindicated in this case?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. TIMOLOL : It is a non selective beta blocker and is contraindicated in patients with history of asthma . Beta-1 selective beta blocker Betaxolol is used in patients with asthma and COPD
• Option B. DORZOLAMIDE : It is a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor . It inhibits the function of corneal endothelial cells and is relatively contraindicated in patients with endothelial dysfunction.
• Option C. BRIMONIDINE: It is a alpha agonist . It has CNS suppressant effect and may cause somnolence.
• Educational Objective:
• Pilocarpine and Prosyaglandin analogues increase intraocular inflammation .
• Khurana Comprehensive Ophthalmology 5 th edition
explanation.ref: Khurana Comprehensive Ophthalmology 5 th edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A young male patient with unilateral mature cataract during preop evaluation was found to have stellate keratic precipitates. The following are features of the underlying condition except?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Bridging Vessels Noted in Angle: This is a feature of FHI . Fine blood vessels, known as bridging vessels or iris angiogenesis , can cross the anterior chamber angle in FHI , which is an abnormal finding.
• Option B. Heterochromia: Heterochromia, a difference in color between the two irises , is a classic sign of FHI. It's typically subtle and may not always be easily noticeable.
• Option C. Non-Granulomatous Anterior Uveitis : FHI typically presents as a chronic, mild, anterior uveitis .
• Educational Objective:
• Unlike other forms of anterior uveitis, iris nodules (Koeppe and Busacca nodules) are typically absent in FHI . This is one of the distinguishing features that sets FHI apart from other types of uveitis.
explanation.ref: Parson’s diseases of eye

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Identify.?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/08/picture16_qZd8WeF.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Irideremia : Irideremia, also known as aniridia, is a congenital condition characterized by the complete or partial absence of the iris , often associated with vision problems and light sensitivity.
• Option B. Corectopia: Corectopia refers to a displacement or misalignment of the pupil from its normal, central position , often occurring as a result of trauma, surgery, or congenital anomalies.
• Option D. Polycoria : Polycoria is a rare condition where there are apparent multiple pupils in the eye, often due to iris defects or additional openings within the iris.
• Educational Objective:
• A coloboma is a congenital defect where normal tissue in or around the eye is missing , often affecting the iris, retina, choroid, or optic nerve , and can appear as a keyhole or notch in the affected structure.
• Khurana Comprehensive Ophthalmology 5 th edition
explanation.ref: Khurana Comprehensive Ophthalmology 5 th edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
What is the reason behind this presentation?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/08/picture20_ROOEwab.jpg]
options: Contraction of fibrinous exudate over iris

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Ring Synechiae : Ring synechiae, also known as annular synechiae, involve the adhesion of the entire pupillary margin to the lens , forming a continuous ring . This condition can lead to pupil deformation, but it typically results in a more fixed and circular shape rather than a festooned appearance.
• Option C. Iris Edema : While iris edema can alter the appearance of the iris , it generally does not cause a festooned pupil. Edema typically leads to a diffusely swollen and thickened iris rather than specific alterations in pupil shape.
• Option D. Contraction of Fibrinous Exudate Over Iris : The contraction of fibrinous exudates, which can occur in cases of severe anterior uveitis or after intraocular surgery, can lead to irregularities in the pupil shape.
• Educational Objective:
• Segmental posterior synechiae refer to localized adhesions of the iris to the lens . These can lead to an irregular pupil shape as portions of the pupil margin are anchored, while others are not, potentially resulting in a festooned appearance.
• Khurana Comprehensive Ophthalmology 5 th edition
explanation.ref: Khurana Comprehensive Ophthalmology 5 th edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: A 50-year-old patient came to the ophthalmologist for a routine check-up. On fundoscopy, an elevated pigmented oval mass was noticed. What is the likely origin of this mass?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Ectoderm : The ectoderm itself primarily gives rise to the outer layer of skin , the central and peripheral nervous systems, and the eyes.
• Option C . Mesoderm: The mesoderm forms muscles, bones, the circulatory system, and internal organs , but not melanocytes.
• Option D . Endoderm : The endoderm forms the lining of the digestive and respiratory systems , and other internal organs, but not melanocytes.
• Educational Objective:
• Melanocytes are derived form neuralcrest cells .

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Triad of congenital toxoplasmosis includes all except?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Intracranial Calcification : Periventricular calcification is a common finding in congenital toxoplasmosis.
• Option B. Chorioretinitis : Chorioretinitis is an inflammation of the choroid and retina of the eye . In congenital toxoplasmosis , it can lead to visual impairment or blindness . It is one of the most common and long-lasting consequences of this infection.
• Option C. Convulsions : Convulsions or seizures can occur as a result of the central nervous system involvement in congenital toxoplasmosis.
• Educational Objective:
• The classic triad of congenital toxoplasmosis includes intracranial calcifications, chorioretinitis, and convulsions, with cardiac abnormalities not being a typical component of this triad.
• Khurana Comprehensive Ophthalmology 5 th edition

subject: Ophthalmology
question: All the following can be administered through an intravitreal route except?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Vancomycin: Vancomycin can be administered intravitreally, particularly for the treatment of bacterial endophthalmitis caused by gram-positive bacteria .
• Option B. Ceftazidime: Ceftazidime is also used intravitreally, especially for treating bacterial endophthalmitis caused by gram-negative bacteria.
• Option C. Voriconazole : Voriconazole, an antifungal medication , can be administered intravitreally for fungal infections of the eye, such as fungal endophthalmitis.
• Educational Objective:
• Out of the following drugs, Gentamycin is avoided for intravitreal injection because of its increased risk of retinotoxicity (leads to MACULAR INFARCTION).

subject: Ophthalmology
question: Mobile hypopyon is seen in which condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Fuchs Uveitis Syndrome: Fuchs Uveitis Syndrome is a chronic, typically unilateral uveitis . It is characterized by minimal or no pain and low-grade inflammation . Hypopyon is not a typical feature of this condition.
• Option C . HLA B27 Associated Anterior Uveitis: HLA B27 associated uveitis can be acute and intense and immobile hypopyon . HLA B27 uveitis tends to present with acute, painful red eye and significant photophobia.
• Option D . Traumatic Uveitis : Traumatic uveitis can follow an injury to the eye . While hypopyon can occur in traumatic uveitis , it's more commonly associated with infectious complications post-trauma rather than being directly mobile.
• Educational objective:
• Immobile hypopyon is seen in Behcets Disease.
• KANSKI 9 TH EDITION

subject: Ophthalmology
question:
Identify.

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/08/picture35_M6pXQ8V.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Periphlebitis Retinae : Periphlebitis retinae is an inflammatory condition affecting the retinal veins, often seen in association with systemic diseases like tuberculosis presenting with multiple peripheral branch retinal vein occlusions. This is also known as Eales disease.
• Option C . Behçet Disease : Behçet disease is a systemic vasculitis that can involve the retina, causing recurrent uveitis and vasculitis , potentially leading to complications like retinal hemorrhage and vision loss.
• Option D . Acute Retinal Necrosis : Acute retinal necrosis is a rare but severe viral infection of the retina , usually caused by herpes viruses, characterized by rapid progression of retinal necrosis , uveitis, and can lead to retinal detachment and severe vision loss. It starts in retinal periphery and spreads centripetally.
• Educational Objective:
• Clinical picture of Candle wax dripping is suggestive of sarcoidosis .

What is the given fundus picture suggestive of?

question:
